title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Intellectual developmental disorder (ICD-11: 6A00 Disorder of Intellectual Development; DSM-5-TR: 319 Intellectual Developmental Disorder / formerly "Mental Retardation") is a neurodevelopmental disorder that begins during the developmental period (as a rule, before the age of 22) and is characterized by significant limitations both in intellectual functioning (approximately 2 standard deviations

title: 4. Aetiology and pathogenesis
text: Severe prematurity (<32 weeks), low birth weight (<1500 g). Birth asphyxia, hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy. Neonatal kernicterus (treatable — phototherapy). Neonatal sepsis, meningitis. Bacterial meningitis (especially pneumococcal, meningococcal), viral encephalitis. Head trauma (TBI) — during childhood. Lead poisoning — a preventable cause (BLL > 5 µg/dL risk of cognitive impairment; CDC).

title: 6. Diagnosis
text: Conceptual (language, reading, writing, mathematics, memory, problem-solving); Social (interpersonal skills, social responsibility, matters of gullibility); Practical (self-care, work, health, safety). Developmental history (congenital, perinatal, postnatal) + family history (3 generations). Physical examination — height, head circumference, dysmorphic features, neurological, skin (café-au-lait, h

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Brain MRI — in focal neurological signs, micro/macrocephaly, seizures, regression. EEG — suspected seizures, regression. Vision and hearing — baseline indicators in all cases. Cardiac echocardiography and ECG — in syndromes such as Down and 22q11.2 deletion.

title: 9. Prognosis
text: Mild severity (~85% of all IDD); intellectual and adaptive functioning at a close level. Treatable aetiology (PKU, congenital hypothyroidism, lead) — early detection. Early intervention (<3 years). Stable and supportive family environment. Active treatment of comorbid psychiatric and medical conditions. Accessibility of community and school resources. Profound or severe severity.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Developmental speech or language disorders are a group of neurodevelopmental disorders manifesting as persistent and significant difficulties in the acquisition of language and/or speech that are not fully explained by hearing impairment, an intellectual disorder or another neurological cause. In ICD-11, under the 6A01 code, four subtypes are coded:

title: 4. Aetiology and pathogenesis
text: DLD heritability 50–75% in twin studies (Bishop D.V.M. Trends Cogn Sci 2014). CNV and GWAS studies — a polygenic risk architecture is also characteristic of DLD. Atypical patterns in the development of the left perisylvian region (around Broca and Wernicke areas), the arcuate fasciculus, and the basal ganglia. In stuttering, dopaminergic dysfunction in the basal ganglia–supplementary motor area co

title: 5. Clinical manifestations
text: Age-inappropriate sound omission, substitution, distortion (for example, "r" → "l", "s" → "t"). Unintelligibility — unfamiliar people have difficulty understanding the speech. Up to 4 years of age it is considered "mild" within the developmental framework; those persisting beyond 4 years should be reviewed diagnostically. Repetition of sounds and syllables (f-f-f-fish), prolongation (ssshello), bl

title: 6. Diagnosis
text: Parent and teacher interview (developmental history, language milestones). Hearing test (audiometry — mandatory in all cases). Standardized speech and language assessment (CELF-5, PLS-5, GFTA-3, DELV). Naturalistic speech sample (in a play or conversation setting). Non-verbal IQ measure (for the exclusion of ASD and intellectual disability). Autism spectrum screening (M-CHAT-R/F < 30 months; SCQ p

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Not mandatory; based on specific suspicion: Hearing test — mandatory in all cases (audiometry, tympanometry, otoacoustic emissions). Fragile X (FMR1) — in cases of severe DLD + comorbid intellectual disability. Thyroid function (TSH, T4) — if congenital hypothyroidism has not been detected. Lead level — if risk factors are present. Brain MRI — when there are focal neurological signs, regression, m

title: 8. Treatment
text: Articulation therapy — a phoneme-by-phoneme approach (van Riper); minimal pair training (comparison of contrastive pairs of similar sounds). Reduction of phonological processes — systematic process-based therapy (Bowen C., Cupples L. 1999). In complex and severe cases — PROMPT, a multisensory approach.

title: 9. Prognosis
text: Mild-to-moderate severity, expressive component only. Early identification (<3 years) and intervention. Absence of comorbid disorders (ASD, intellectual disability). Parental involvement and a language-rich environment at home. Severity of the receptive component (poorer prognosis than expressive only). Comorbid intellectual disability or ASD. Late identification (>5 years).

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD; ICD-11: 6A02 ; DSM-5-TR: 299.00 Autism Spectrum Disorder) is a neurodevelopmental disorder with onset in the early developmental period, accompanied by persistent deficits in two core domains: In ICD-11, the diagnosis is specified with qualifier s: with/without intellectual developmental disorder; impairment of functional language (absent / mild-to-moderate / severe)

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Father's age > 40 — associated with new mutation risk. Parental valproate use (during pregnancy) — increases risk 5–8 times (FDA and EMA warning; Christensen J. et al. JAMA 2013). Premature birth (<32 weeks) — increases risk 7 times. Low birth weight, perinatal hypoxia. Parental diabetes, severe obesity, gestational hypertension — indicated as risk factors.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Reciprocal social-emotional exchange deficit — atypical social proximity, reduced reciprocal conversation, restricted shared interests. Non-verbal communicative behavior deficits – atypical use of eye contact, reduced coordination of gestures and facial expressions. Difficulty forming and maintaining relationships — paucity of age-appropriate friendships, lack of hypothetical imaginative play.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Social-emotional reciprocal exchange. In social reciprocity, nonverbal communicative behavior; Development, maintenance, and understanding of relationships. Stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, object use, speech; Insistence on sameness, adherence to routine, ritualized behavior or speech; High intensity, focused limited interests; Hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input, or atypical senso

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: EEG — in case of seizure, regression, bizarre behavior episodes. Brain MRI — focal neurological signs, micro/macrocephaly, regression. Hearing test — mandatory in all cases (audiometry, ABR).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Early Start Denver Model (ESDM) Joint Attention, Symbolic Play, Engagement, and Regulation (JASPER) Pivotal Response Treatment (PRT) Enhanced Milieu Teaching (GMT / EMT — Enhanced Milieu Teaching) Paediatric Autism Communication Therapy (PACT)

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Early diagnosis (<3 years) and early intensive intervention. Intellectual function preserved (IQ >70). Acquisition of functional language by age 5. Management of comorbid disorders. Stabil family support and community resources. Comorbid intellectual disability (severe-profound). Absence of functional language by age 5. Epileptic seizures — additional risk increase in adulthood.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Specific learning disability (ICD-11: 6A03 Developmental Learning Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 315.00–315.2 Specific Learning Disorder — a neurodevelopmental disorder manifesting with persistent and significant difficulties in acquiring one or more academic skills, unexpected given the patient's age, intellectual level, and educational opportunities. Subspecifications in ICD-11:

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: In mathematical disorder, high heritability (~70%), MYT1L and other genes are proposed (Mascheretti S. et al. Trends Genet 2018). Early educational quality and reading instruction methodology — lack of phonological approach creates greater academic disparity in children with polygenic risk. Low socioeconomic status — not etiology, but a factor in identification and access to intervention.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Inaccuracy and slowness in word promotion — reads words slowly, with struggle, substitutes or omits letters. Phonological awareness deficit — difficulty with rhyming, letter-sound correspondence, segmenting words into sounds. Writing and spelling — phonetic rule non-conforming writing errors. Reading comprehension — secondary impairment due to word retrieval deficit.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Inaccuracy or slowness in reading words; Difficulty understanding the text being read; Spelling difficulties; Difficulties in written expression (grammar, punctuation, organization); Challenges in emotional and cognitive skills; Difficulties in mathematical reasoning. Teacher and parent interview — academic history, response to intervention. Visual and hearing tests (to rule out sensory impairment

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: No routine laboratory indication. Based on specific clinical suspicion: Lead level – if risk factors exist. Thyroid function — if congenital hypothyroidism is undetected. Genetic research — if comorbid intellectual disability or syndromic features present. Brain MRI — no routine indication; in case of focal neurological signs, seizures, regression. EEG — suspected seizure, rule out Landau-Kleffner

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Early identification (preschool or early school age). Evidence-based Structured Literacy intervention. High IQ — development of compensatory strategies. Family support and motivation. Affects only one academic domain (e.g., single reading, math normal). Late identification (middle or late school age). The impact across multiple academic domains. Comorbid ADHD, language disorder, anxiety.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Developmental coordination disorder (ICD-11: 6A04 Developmental Motor Coordination Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 315.4 Developmental Coordination Disorder, DCD — a neurodevelopmental disorder manifesting with persistent difficulties in acquiring and performing daily motor skills, unexpected given the child's chronological age, intellectual level, and motor learning opportunities.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability studies limited, but family history of DCD cited as risk factor (Gaines R. et al. Phys Occup Ther Pediatr 2008). Neurobiological — atypical patterns of functional connectivity in the cerebellum, basal ganglia, parietal cortex, and premotor areas (Zwicker J.G. et al. Eur J Paediatr Neurol 2012 fMRI review). Internal modeling deficit hypothesis ... disruptions in the processes of predic

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Self-care — clothing, food intake, hygiene. Academic performance — handwriting speed, paper organization, sports participation. Community activity — playgrounds, sports teams, hobbies. Psychosocial outcomes — decreased self-esteem, anxiety, depressed mood, social isolation (Cairney J. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2013). Early signs (1–3 years) — delay in motor milestones (sitting, standing, first s

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Parent and teacher interview — developmental history, motor milestones, daily activity problems. Standardized motor test—MABC-2 (Movement Assessment Battery for Children-2, age 3–16) or BOT-2 (Bruininks-Oseretsky Test of Motor Proficiency). Functional daily activity assessment — DCDQ (Developmental Coordination Disorder Questionnaire, parent form), VABS adaptive component.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: No routine indication. Based on specific clinical suspicion: CK (creatine kinase) — suspected muscular dystrophy. Thyroid function — in some hypothyroid cases motor slowing. Genetic research — if syndromic features present. Brain MRI — only if focal neurological signs, ataxia, stable deficits (to rule out cerebellar/cerebral pathology). EMG/nerve conduction study — suspected neuromuscular disease.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Early identification and intervention. CO-OP or NTT is an evidence-based intervention. Comorbid ADHD, learning disorder, anxiety management. Family and school support. Normal intellectual level. Multiple comorbid disorders (ADHD + LD + DCD together). Social isolation and complete avoidance of sports activities. Secondary anxiety, depression, social phobia.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD; ICD-11: 6A05 Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder; DSM-5-TR: 314.0x ADHD) — a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by a pattern of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity that begins in early development (by age 12), causing functional impairment in academic, social, or occupational activities.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: CNV — rare; common variations are fundamental. Preterm birth and low birth weight — increases risk 2–3 times. Maternal use of tobacco, alcohol, and cannabis during pregnancy. Maternal perinatal stress and severe psychosocial deprivation. Lead exposure in childhood. Early traumatic brain injury (TBI). Sugar does not cause ADHD (Wolraich M.L. et al. JAMA 1995 meta-analysis).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Failure to pay attention to details, careless mistakes. Difficulty sustaining attention in tasks. Appears not to listen when spoken to directly. Noncompliance with instructions, leaving tasks unfinished. Difficulty organizing tasks and activities. Persistent avoidance of tasks requiring sustained mental effort. Losing necessary items. Easily distracted by external stimuli.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Parent and teacher (for children) or patient themselves (for adults) interview — symptom history, onset age, contexts. Standardized rating scales—Conners-3 (parent/teacher/patient), Vanderbilt (AAP recommendation), SNAP-IV; for adults ASRS (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale, WHO/Kessler). Clinical interview — confirmation of symptoms in two contexts, documentation of functional impairment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: No specific laboratory indication for routine DDHP. Based on clinical suspicion: Complete blood count, ferritin (iron deficiency), thyroid function (TSH). Lead level – if risk factors exist. Toxicology screening — suspicion of substance use in adolescents. Before stimulant initiation—ECG only if cardiac risk history or family history (AAP, AHA 2008; routine ECG not required).

title: 8. Treatment
text: 4–5 years — first-line parent training (PCIT, Triple P, Incredible Years); second-line methylphenidate. 6–11 years — first-line Stimulant + behavioral intervention . 12–18 years — first line Stimulant + behavioral intervention ; adolescent consent. Adults — Stimulant or non-stimulant + CBT .

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Early identification and intervention. High IQ — compensatory strategies. Stable and supportive family environment. Active treatment of comorbid disorders. Good response to stimulant and behavioral intervention. Maintaining adherence in adolescence and adulthood. Comorbid conduct disorder, oppositional defiant disorder. Low socioeconomic support.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Stereotyped movement disorder (ICD-11: 6A06 Stereotyped Movement Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 307.3 Stereotypic Movement Disorder—characterized by seemingly voluntary, repetitive, rhythmic, purposeless motor behavior, interfering with daily activity or causing bodily injury, a neurodevelopmental disorder. Typical examples: hand flapping, body rocking, head banging, hair pulling, lip biting, finger knuckle

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: In primary stereotypy, familial clustering is noted — family history 25–30% (Mahone 2014). Stereotypy is highly prevalent in specific genetic syndromes—Rett (MECP2), Lesch-Nyhan (HPRT1), Cornelia de Lange (NIPBL), Smith-Magenis (RAI1), Fragile X (FMR1), Phelan-McDermid (22q13). Stress and anxiety disorders may intensify stereotypic severity.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Intellectual disability (in secondary forms). Autism spectrum disorder (comorbid 30–50% in primary stereotypy — Mahone 2014). ADHD — comorbid in 20–35% of children with primary stereotypies. Anxiety and OCD-like symptoms. Tic disorders — differentiation may be difficult.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Parent interview and video documentation (family-recorded episodes via phone are diagnostically valuable). Developmental and neurological examination. Tics and other movement disorders (chorea, myoclonus, dystonia) differential diagnosis — neurologist consultation. Comorbidity screening — autism spectrum (M-CHAT-R/F, ADOS-2), intellectual disability (Vineland-3, IQ), ADHD, anxiety.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Stereotypy Severity Scale (SSS)—Miller J.M., Singer H.S. et al. Repetitive Behavior Scale-Revised (RBS-R) — in comorbid cases of autism and intellectual disabilities. Video recordings — home episode logs conducted by family assessed by clinician Functional behavior analysis (FBA) — for intervention planning in self-harm or context-dependent stereotypy.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Primary form, normal IQ. Early HRT intervention response. Absence of comorbidity. Family support and realistic expectations. Secondary form — within the context of severe intellectual disability or genetic syndrome. Self-injurious behavior — especially in Lesch-Nyhan, Cornelia de Lange. Komorbid autizm spektri pozuntusu.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Tikli pozuntular (XBT-11: 6A07 Primary Tics or Tic Disorders; DSM-5-TR: 307.20–307.23) — a group of neurodevelopmental disorders characterized by sudden, rapid, recurrent, nonrhythmic, involuntary or semi-voluntary motor movements and/or vocal/sound productions. Tics typically premonitor hiss (preceded by an urge or tension) and can be voluntarily suppressed for a short period.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: High density of OCD and ADHD in family history — genetic overlap. Preterm birth, perinatal hypoxia — moderately increases risk. Maternal tobacco use during pregnancy. Psychosocial stress — is not a cause for the onset of tics, but influences severity.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Most patients prior to seizures premonitor hissi (somatic urge, tension) is noted; the tic temporarily relieves this sensation. Tics can be voluntarily suppressed for a short period (seconds to minutes), but this suppression creates internal tension and discomfort — the subsequent tic episode is usually more intense. tics "Waxing and waning" — Violence period varies; one tick type replaced by anot

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Multiple motor tics and one or several vocal tics (occurring at different times, not required to be present simultaneously) Tics at least 1 il permanent (may vary over time). Onset Up to 18 years . Exceptions — not fully explained by substance (e.g., cocaine) or medical condition (Huntington's, postviral encephalitis). Parent and patient interview — onset age, course, triggers, premonitory feeling

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: No specific laboratory indication for routine tics. Based on clinical suspicion: Thyroid function (TSH) — in hyperthyroidism context. Ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urine copper — suspicion of Wilson's disease (especially hepatic or movement adjunct). HTT — suspected Huntington (family history). Streptococcal antibodies (ASO, anti-DNase B) - only if classic PANDAS clinical picture present; not recommended

title: 8. Treatment
text: First-line: α2 adrenergic agonists. — Clonidine, guanfasin (ER) — side effect profile superior to antipsychotics; particularly beneficial in comorbid ADHD. Second-order: atipik antipsixotik Aripiprazole, risperidone, ziprasidone. Stronger anti-tic effect, but metabolic and EPS side effects. Third row: tipik antipsixotik — haloperidol, pimozide (FDA approval for Tourette, but EPS and QTc prolongati

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Typical tic disorder (distinct from rapid functional tic-like behavior). Mild-moderate YGTSS severity. Active management of comorbidity (ADHD, OCD). Early psychoeducation and family-school support. Severe comorbidity (severe OCD, ADHD, anxiety, depression). Self-injurious tics (e.g., head banging). Social and academic limited adaptation.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Schizophrenia — a psychotic disorder characterized by significant disturbance in thought, perception, affect, and behavior, accompanied by at least one month of active-phase symptoms and typically a chronic course. In ICD-11 classification 6A20 Code: Located in the group 'Schizophrenia and other primary psychotic disorders' (6A2x). Nosologically, primary psychotic disturbance — meaning psychotic s

title: 2. History
text: The modern concept of schizophrenia has passed through four main stages:

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Geographic and cultural variability exists, but the core incident rate remains within a certain range. Male : female ratio ~1.4 : 1; onset age in males 18–25, in females 25–35 (Aleman A. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003). In women, a second incidence peak is observed after menopause (associated with the reduction of the neuroprotective effect of estrogen).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability 60–80% (Sullivan P.F. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003, twin meta-analysis). Monozygotic twin concordance ~50%, dizygotic ~15%, general population risk ~1%. One parent with schizophrenia — child risk ~10%; both parents — ~40%. Polygenic nature: GWAS studies have identified over 200 risk loci (Trubetskoy V. et al. Nature 2022, n>76,000 cases). There is no single "schizophrenia gene" — r

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: 1/3 patients — good functional recovery. ~1/3 — residual symptoms, partial functional recovery. ~1/3 — severe chronic course, requires continuous support.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Formal thought disorder / disorganized speech Grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior Negative symptoms (affective flattening, avolition, alogia) Clinical interview (patient + at least one informed relative). Structured diagnostic interview (SCID-5 or MINI). Severity assessment (initial PANSS or BPRS score).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Brain MRI (T1, T2, FLAIR) — recommended at first episode (NICE CG178). EKG — before starting antipsychotic (baseline QTc). Brain CT — when MRI is unavailable.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Mechanism, dose, evidence, and side effect profile for each medication. Agranulocytosis (~0.8%, mortality 5%) — blood count monitoring. Myocarditis — especially within the first 4 weeks; troponin, ECG. Constipation — risk of ileus and perforation; prophylactic laxative. Hypersalivation, sedation — dose-dependent. Seizures — risk increases at doses > 600 mg/day; EEG monitoring.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Brief untreated period (DUP < 6 ay) Acute onset, clear stressor Premorbid good function Predominance of positive symptoms (negative few) Presence of an affective component Good social support, family cooperation Early intervention and adherence Gradual onset, long prodrome

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Schizoaffective disorder (ICD-11: 6A21 Schizoaffective Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 295.70) — psychotic symptoms (delusion, hallucination, disorganized speech) and affective episode (manic or depressive) observed together during the same clinical episode, but additionally Continuation of psychotic symptoms even outside affective episodes. ilkin psixotik pozuntudur.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Approximately half the prevalence of schizophrenia (~0.5–0.7%).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Family studies — relatives of schizoaffective patients have increased risk of both schizophrenia and mood disorders (Maier W. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 1993). Overlapping models with schizophrenia — dopamine dysregulation, cortical volume changes, glutamate NMDA dysfunction. Related to the affective component — limbic and monoaminergic (serotonin, noradrenaline) system dysfunction.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Course is typically episodic — affective episodes and psychotic components of varying intensity. Better functional recovery compared to schizophrenia, but significant social and occupational impairment exists. Manic and depressive cycles in bipolar type; persistent depressive components in depressive type.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview + information from relatives — chronological order of affective episodes and psychotic symptoms. Structured interview (SCID-5 or MINI). PANSS or BPRS — severity of psychotic symptoms; HAM-D, MADRS — depressive; YMRS — manic. Physical and neurological examination. Toksikoloji skrining (sidik). Laboratory (complete blood count, thyroid, liver, kidney, glucose, HbA1c, lipid, prolac

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Schizophrenia-like (see 6A20 §7.2): Absolute: complete blood count, liver/kidney, glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile, TSH, B12, folate, toxicology screening, HIV, syphilis, pregnancy test, prolactin baseline. Selective: EEG, anti-NMDA-R antibodies, ceruloplasmin (Wilson). Before starting lithium — creatinine, TSH, calcium, ECG. Before initiating valproate — liver and blood tests.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Severe psychotic depressive episode. Suicide risk distinct from, or comorbid with, eating disorder. During pregnancy (safe alternative). Non-response to pharmacotherapy.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Bipolar type (better prognosis than depressive type). Rapid onset, clear affective component. Premorbid good functional level. Early intervention and adherence. Comorbid substance use management.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Schizotypal disorder (ICD-11: 6A22 Schizotypal Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 301.22 Schizotypal Personality Disorder) — characterized by persistent thought and perception disturbances, bizarre (eccentric) behavior, social deficits, and psychotic-like experiences, but Criteria for psychotic episode not met. Clinical condition.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Polygenic risk architecture similar to schizophrenia, but lesser 'load'; presence of some protective alleles. Observed milder variants of structural changes in schizophrenia — volume reduction of temporal cortex, but preservation of frontal cortex (Hazlett E.A. et al. Schizophr Bull 2011 review). Dopaminergic dysregulation — lower than in schizophrenia; psychotic decompensation less likely.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Referential delusions (ideas of reference) — not at full delusional level; patient may sometimes accept these thoughts as unreal. Odd beliefs or magical thinking – telepathy, 'sixth sense,' superstitions inappropriate to age and culture. Unusual perceptual experiences – impersonal, transient illusions; full hallucination absent. Odd thinking and speech – vague, metaphorical, indirect, stereotyped.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Relationship thoughts (excluding referential delusions); Beliefs or magical thinking inconsistent with age and culture; Unusual perceptual experiences (including illusions); Odd thinking and speech (vague, indirect, metaphorical, stereotyped); Doubtfulness and paranoid ideas; Inappropriate or restricted affect; Odd, eccentric, or peculiar behavior or appearance.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: No specific routine indication. Standard psychiatric initial indicators (complete blood count, thyroid, liver, kidney, glucose); toxicological screening. Not routinely required; neuroimaging in clinical suspicion.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Low-dose antipsychotic (risperidone 0.5–2 mg, olanzapine 2.5–10 mg, aripiprazole 2–10 mg) — for psychotic-like symptoms; intermittent, not continuous. Soviet Union — for social anxiety and comorbid depression. Benzodiazepine short-term — acute anxiety.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Mild symptom intensity. Management of comorbidity (depression, anxiety, substance). Social support and sustained relationships. Psychotherapy participation. Schizophrenia in family history. Cannabis or other psychoactive substance use. Severe cognitive deficits.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Acute and transient psychotic disorder (ICD-11: 6A23 Acute and Transient Psychotic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 298.8 Brief Psychotic Disorder) — a disorder characterized by rapid onset (a few days to weeks), psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, grossly disorganized behavior), short duration, and full recovery to premorbid functional level.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Schizophrenia and bipolar disorder partially share genetic risk. Risk increases when psychotic or affective disorder is present in family history. Acute psychosocial stress (death, displacement, natural disaster) — identified in a significant proportion of patients; indicated as a stress qualifier in DSM-5-TR. Postpartum period — hormonal and sleep disturbances.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Rapid onset — from premorbid normal function to full psychotic picture within days-weeks. Polymorphic (variable) symptoms — delusions (persecution, relationship, religious), hallucinations (auditory, visual, cenesthetic), disorganized speech. Emotional fluctuation — acute fear, ecstasy, panic, bewilderment. No alterations in consciousness (except delirium).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior. (At least one of 1, 2, or 3 must be present.) Clinical interview + information from relatives — onset dynamics, premorbid function.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PANSS, BPRS — psychotic severity. HAM-D, YMRS — assessment of affective components. C-SSRS — suicide risk. GAF/PSP — functional level. According to first episode psychosis protocol (see 6A20 §7.2): Absolute: complete blood count, liver/kidney, glucose, electrolytes, TSH, B12, folate, toxicology screening, HIV, syphilis, pregnancy test, prolactin. If organic psychosis is suspected — anti-NMDA-R ant

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Very rapid onset and short duration. Clear stress trigger. Premorbid good functional level. Presence of an affective component. Polymorphic symptomatology (without schizophrenia symptoms). Absence of family psychotic history. No cannabis or other substance use. Schizophrenia-like symptoms (Schneider first-rank).

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Delusional disorder (ICD-11: 6A24 Delusional Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 297.1) — duration of at least one month one or several delusions Substitutive psychotic disorder organized around hallucinatory experiences that do not meet the full criteria for schizophrenia. The patient's general behavior and functional level remain relatively intact except for the delusional state.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: ~0.2% (Perälä 2007). Onset: middle age (35–55 years), significantly later than schizophrenia. Gender: approximately equal; differences in some subtypes (erotomania in women). Comorbidity: depression, anxiety, social isolation; high suicide risk.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Genetic load is less than in schizophrenia; however, family history shows increased density of paranoid personality and psychotic disorder. Sensory deprivation (hearing impairment) — increases risk of paranoid delirium in elderly. Social isolation, migration, language barriers — risk factors. Neurobiological — dopaminergic dysregulation, but more localized than in schizophrenia; increased striatal

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Outside the delusion, the patient's general behavior, affect, cognitive functions are relatively preserved; work and relationships can continue as long as they do not touch on delusion content.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview (general and especially delusional content). Relative/friend interview – premorbid function, onset of delirium. SCID-5 / MINI; PANSS / BPRS Positive Symptom Subscale. Medical and neurological examination. Toxicology screening; sensor (hearing, vision) test — mandatory in elderly. Beyin MRT (ilk epizodda). Comorbid depression and suicide risk assessment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PANSS / BPRS positive symptom subscale; structured documentation of concrete delusion content. Hearing and vision test (mandatory in the elderly). Laboratory: complete blood count, thyroid, B12, liver, glucose, toxicology screening, syphilis, HIV, ANA (lupus), ceruloplasmin (Wilson). Brain MRI — first episode, atypical clinical picture, elderly patient.

title: 8. Treatment
text: NICE CG178 — no separate algorithm for delirium; general psychosis principles. APA 2021 — antipsychotic first-line; insight and adherence difficulty emphasized. Munro A. reviews (Cambridge Univ Press 1999) — subtype-specific clinical guidance.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Prognosis is better than schizophrenia — functional preservation, late onset. Adherence is the main limitation. Jealousy and somatic delusions relatively more difficult to treat. In older adults, improvement with sensory correction is possible. Monitoring — antipsychotic side effects, delusion severity, violence and suicide risk.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: The basis of the concept — to describe the heterogeneous clinical presentation of psychotic disorders using a more valid and dimensional measure instead of classic subtypes (paranoid, hebephrenic, catatonic).
sub: SYMPTOM DOMAINS OF PRIMARY PSYCHOTIC DISORDERS

title: 2. History
text: Bleuler's "4A" symptoms (1911) and Schneider's "first-rank symptoms" (1959) — categorical approach. Crow T.J. (1980) — Concept of Type I (positive symptom dominant) and Type II (negative dominant) — the basis of the dimensional approach. Liddle P.F. (1987) — three-dimensional factor — reality distortion, disorganization, psychomotor poverty.
sub: SYMPTOM DOMAINS OF PRIMARY PSYCHOTIC DISORDERS

Kay S.R., Fiszbein A., Opler L.A. PANSS. Schiz

title: 5. Six symptom domains
text: Delusion, hallucination — "excessive/added" symptoms in perception and thought processes. Severity: from presence to severe level (according to degree of impact on patient's daily activities). Affective flattening, avolition (loss of motivation), alogia (poverty of speech), anhedonia, social withdrawal. A major factor in functional impairment; responds poorly to pharmacotherapy.
sub: SYMPTOM DOMAINS OF PRIMARY PSYCHOTIC DISORDERS

title: 6. Clinical use
text: PANSS (positive, negative, general psychopathology subscales). SANS / SAPS — negative and positive symptoms separately. HAM-D, MADRS — depressive component. YMRS — manic component. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale — psychomotor component. MATRICS Consensus Cognitive Battery (MCCB) — cognitive component.
sub: SYMPTOM DOMAINS OF PRIMARY PSYCHOTIC DISORDERS

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Katatoniya (XBT-11: 6A40 Catatonia Associated with Another Mental Disorder; 6A41 Catatonia Induced by Substances or Medications; 6E69 Secondary Catatonia Syndrome; DSM-5-TR: 293.89 Catatonia) — syndrome of complex disturbance in psychomotor, behavioral and autonomic functions. Not an independent disorder — clinical syndrome on background of another psychiatric or medical condition.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 7–15% of patients admitted to psychiatric hospital have catatonic symptoms (Fink M., Taylor M.A. Catatonia 2003). Catatonia is most prevalent in affective disorders (especially bipolar manic and depressive episodes). In schizophrenia, 10–20% of patients exhibit catatonic features during the course. Organic catatonia — autoimmune encephalitis (especially anti-NMDA-R, anti-LGI1), electrolyte disturb

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Complete blood count, liver/renal, electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, Mg, P), glucose, ammonia, CPK, troponin, thyroid, B12. Toksikoloji skrining. EEG — rule out non-convulsive status epilepticus. Lumbar puncture — suspected encephalitis / autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDA-R, anti-LGI1 antibodies).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: BFCRS — baseline ratings and monitoring. The above laboratory and instrumental examinations — an etiological search. Autonomic function (blood pressure, pulse, temperature, urine output) — especially in malignant form. Comorbid mood disorder and psychotic symptom assessment (later, when patient can speak).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Lorazepam and/or ECT response — most patients achieve full recovery within days to weeks. High relapse if etiological disorder is untreated. Complications — aspiration pneumonia from prolonged stupor, DVT, dehydration, skin ulcers. Malignant catatonia — mortality 10–20% (if untreated). Monitoring — in etiological disorder context; recognition of relapse markers.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6A40 Catatonia. 2024. Rogers J.P., Pollak T.A., Begum N. et al. Catatonia: demographic, clinical and laboratory associations. Psychol Med 2023 (BAP Guidelines). Fink M., Taylor M.A. Catatonia: A Clinician's Guide to Diagnosis and Treatment. Cambridge Univ Press; 2003. Bush G., Fink M., Petrides G. et al. Catatonia. I. Rating scale and standardized examination. Acta Psychiatr Scand 199

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Bipolyar pozuntu Tip I (XBT-11: 6A60 DSM-5-TR: 296.4x–296.6x) — at least one manik epizod It is characterized as a recurrent disorder often alternating with depressive and/or hypomanic episodes. The presence of a manic episode is sufficient for diagnosis — a depressive episode is not required.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence Type I: ~0.6–1% (Merikangas K.R. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2011, WMH). Gender: approximately equal. Onset: mid-20s to 25 years; earlier with a family history. Suicide risk: lifetime 6–7%; highest in youth and in the first years. Comorbidity: anxiety disorders 60%, substance use 40%, ADHD, personality disorders. Mortality is 2 times higher compared to the population — cardiovas

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: GWAS — 60+ risk loci (Mullins N. et al. Nat Genet 2021). Schizophrenia with extensive genetic overlap (Cross-Disorder Group 2013). Neurobiological — monoaminergic (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine) dysregulation; circadian rhythm disruption; second messenger (PI/cAMP) system impairment; mitochondrial dysfunction. Risk factors — childhood trauma, stress, sleep disturbance, cannabis, antidepressa

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Abnormally elevated or irritable mood + increased energy ≥ 7 days (or any duration requiring hospitalization). ≥ 3 symptoms from the following (≥ 4 in irritable mood): inflated self-esteem, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, increase in goal-directed activity, risky behaviors. Functional impairment or requirement for hospitalization or psychotic features.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Depressive and hypomanic episodes are not required for Type I diagnosis, but are typically present Clinical interview + relative/friend information — manic and hypomanic episodes are more easily recognized by family members. Structured interview (SCID-5, MINI). YMRS (Young Mania Rating Scale), HAM-D / MADRS. MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire) or HCL-32 — bipolar screening (self-report).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: YMRS, HAM-D / MADRS, MDQ, HCL-32, C-SSRS. Pre-lithium: complete blood count, creatinine, TSH, calcium, ECG, pregnancy test. Pre-valproate: complete blood count, liver function, pregnancy test (for women of childbearing age — mandatory recommendation). Before carbamazepine: complete blood count, liver, sodium. Brain MRI — first psychotic episode.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Episodic course; relapse 80–90% (untreated); significant reduction with maintenance pharmacotherapy. Suicide risk high (6–7% lifetime); particularly in the first years. Increased cardiovascular mortality (metabolic syndrome). Functional recovery moderate — the majority of patients experience significant social and occupational impairment. Monitoring — mood scales, suicide risk, metabolic indicator

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6A60 Bipolar type I disorder. 2024. NICE CG185. Bipolar disorder: assessment and management. 2014/2020. Yatham L.N., Kennedy S.H., Parikh S.V. et al. CANMAT and ISBD 2018 guidelines for the management of patients with bipolar disorder. Bipolar Disord 2018;20(2):97–170. Cipriani A., Hawton K., Stockton S., Geddes J.R. Lithium in the prevention of suicide in mood disorders. BMJ 2013;346

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Bipolyar pozuntu Tip II (XBT-11: 6A61 DSM-5-TR: 296.89) — at least one hypomanic Episode and at least one major depresiv Characterized by episodes, but never having a full manic episode It is a form of bipolar disorder. It is not a 'milder' form than Type I—patients spend more lifetime time in depression; functional impairment and suicide risk are comparable to Type I.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: ~0.4–1.1% (Merikangas K.R. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2011 WMH). Sex: relatively higher in females (~1.5:1 — different from Type I). Onset: mid-20s to 25 years; multiple depressive episodes usually precede a hypomanic episode. Suicide risk comparable to Type I (lifetime ~5–7%); more often due to depressive episodes. Comorbidity: anxiety 60%+, substance use 30–40%, borderline personal

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Genetic basis overlaps with Type I — heritability 60–80%; GWAS loci parallel. Genetic independence of the clinical phenotype from bipolar Type I is debated — some studies assess both types as the same spectrum. Circadian rhythm disturbance and family history.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Abnormally elevated or irritable mood + increased energy ≥ 4 days (≥ 7 in Type I). Same sub-symptoms as manic episode (decreased need for sleep, distractibility, increase in goal-directed activity, etc.); ≥ 3 symptoms (≥ 4 if irritable mood). ≥ 5 symptoms for 2 weeks (including low mood and/or anhedonia). Bipolar Type II depressive episodes often with atypical features — hypersomnia, hyperphagia,

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Structured inquiry for hypomanic episodes in every depressive patient (SCID-5 mood module, MDQ, HCL-32). Information from family members — observation of hypomanic episodes is important. Clinical interview + YMRS, HAM-D / MADRS. Laboratory: thyroid, liver, kidney, toxicology, pregnancy test. Comorbidity and suicide risk assessment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: SCID-5 mood module — the gold standard. MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire) and HCL-32 (Hypomania Checklist) — screening. YMRS, HAM-D / MADRS, C-SSRS. Pre-lithium: creatinine, TSH, calcium, ECG.

title: 8. Treatment
text: First-line — quetiapine (FDA approved), lurasidone, or quetiapine + lithium combination. Lamotrigine — first-line in maintenance and depressive prophylaxis (particularly effective in Type II). Lithium — maintenance and suicide reduction.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Episodic course; relapse significantly reduced with maintenance pharmacotherapy. Type II is not 'milder' than Type I — high functional impairment and depressive burden. Quetiapine and lamotrigine in maintenance as main. Monitoring — depressive symptoms, hypomanic triggers, metabolic indicators.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6A61 Bipolar type II disorder. 2024. NICE CG185. 2014/2020. Yatham L.N. et al. CANMAT and ISBD 2018 guidelines. Bipolar Disord 2018;20(2):97–170. Judd L.L., Akiskal H.S., Schettler P.J. et al. A prospective investigation of the natural history of the long-term weekly symptomatic status of bipolar II disorder. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003;60(3):261–269.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Sikletimik pozuntu (XBT-11: 6A62 DSM-5-TR: 301.13 Cyclothymic Disorder) — over a period of at least 2 years (1 year in children/adolescents) subthreshold chronic affective disorder characterized by persistent fluctuation between hypomanic and depressive symptoms. Does not meet criteria for full major depressive, manic, or hypomanic episodes.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 0.4–1% (DSM-5-TR; broad range, precise epidemiological data limited). Gender: approximately equal. Onset: adolescence and early adulthood. Type I or II conversion to bipolar: ~15–50% (Akiskal H.S. reviews). Comorbidity: anxiety, substance use, ADHD, borderline personality disorder.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Shared genetic load with the bipolar spectrum (Akiskal). Increased density of bipolar and major depressive disorder in family history. Neurobiological — monoaminergic and circadian dysregulation similar to bipolar disorder, but less pronounced.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Multiple subthreshold hypomanic episodes — brief, low-intensity elevations in energy and mood. Multiple subthreshold depressive episodes — low-intensity depressive symptoms. "Stable period" less than 2 months. Mood changes over days or weeks; patient may explain themselves as 'this is just my nature.' Interpersonal and occupational impairment is present, but not disruptive.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview (mood diary recommended for 4–6 weeks — documentation of patient's own patterns). Comorbidity and family history assessment. Medical and laboratory (thyroid absolute).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MDQ, HCL-32, YMRS, HAM-D.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Lithium or valproate — considered adequate in the context of bipolar spectrum. Lamotrigine — when depressive component is dominant. Atypical antipsychotic (quetiapine) — in moderate-severe cases. NICE CG185, CANMAT 2018 — separate algorithm for cyclothymic disorder is limited; bipolar spectrum principles apply; psychotherapy first-line.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic; spontaneous remission is rare. Risk of conversion to Bipolar Type I/II — annual clinical assessment. Comorbid substance use and BPD risk factors. Functional level improves with adequate treatment.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6A62 Cyclothymic disorder. 2024. NICE CG185. 2014/2020. Yatham L.N. et al. CANMAT/ISBD 2018. Bipolar Disord 2018;20(2):97–170. Akiskal H.S., Khani M.K., Scott-Strauss A. Cyclothymic temperamental disorders. Psychiatr Clin North Am 1979;2(3):527–554.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Single episode depressive disorder (ICD-11: 6A70 Single Episode Depressive Disorder; DSM-5-TR: Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode — 296.2x) — an affective disorder causing functional impairment, accompanied by significant cognitive, somatic, and psychomotor symptoms along with depressed mood and/or anhedonia for at least two weeks. Patient's history only one A major depressive episode is ob

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 15–20% (Kessler R.C. NESARC-III, Hasin D.S. et al. JAMA Psychiatry 2018). Annual prevalence: ~6–8%. Sex: 1.5–2 times higher in females. Onset: mid-20s to 30 years; but possible at any age. Suicide: depressive disorder associated with ~50% of suicide deaths. Comorbidity: anxiety disorders 60%, substance use 25%, pain, cardiovascular, diabetes.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: GWAS — 100+ risk loci (Howard D.M. et al. Nat Neurosci 2019, n>800,000). Neurobiological — monoaminergic (serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine) hypothesis; HPA axis dysregulation, elevated cortisol; hippocampal volume reduction; inflammatory hypothesis (CRP, IL-6).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Anhedonia — loss of interest and pleasure. Weight or appetite changes. Sleep disturbance (insomnia or hypersomnia). Psychomotor agitation or retardation (observable by others). Fatigue or loss of energy. Worthlessness or excessive guilt. Difficulty concentrating and impaired decision-making.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Structured interview (SCID-5, MINI). Scales - PHQ-9 (screening and monitoring), HAM-D, MADRS, BDI-II. Suisid riski (C-SSRS). Bipolar screening (MDQ, HCL-32) — to rule out bipolar episodes. Medical and laboratory (thyroid, B12, folate, ferritin, liver, kidney, pregnancy) — to exclude somatic causes. Toxicology screening (suspect substance use).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PHQ-9 — standard in primary care; ≥10 score indicates moderate-severe depression. HAM-D-17 (Hamilton), MADRS — clinical and research. BDI-II — self-assessment. C-SSRS — suicide risk. Laboratory: complete blood count, thyroid, B12, folate, ferritin, liver/kidney, glucose. EKG — before starting TCA or QT-prolonging medications.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Mild (PHQ-9 5–9) — guided self-help, CBT-based internet interventions, physical activity, observation. Moderate (PHQ-9 10–14) — CBT or IPT, or pharmacotherapy (SSRI); patient choice. Severe (PHQ-9 ≥ 20 or psychotic features) — combination of pharmacotherapy + psychotherapy; severe psychotic/suicidal risk — inpatient admission and ECT.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Spontaneous remission occurs in 50% within one year (though most remissions occur without antidepressant treatment); relapse risk is high. The risk of a subsequent episode increases after each episode. Suicide risk is particularly high in the first weeks. Monitoring — PHQ-9 / MADRS, suicide risk, side effects, compliance; more frequent in first 12 weeks.

title: 11. Sources

Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients with Major Depressive Disorder. 2010 (with 2019 update). Kennedy S.H., Lam R.W., McIntyre R.S. et al. title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Repeated depressive disorder (ICD-11: 6A71 Recurrent Depressive Disorder; DSM-5-TR: Major Depressive Disorder, Recurrent — 296.3x) — presence of at least two major depressive episodes, with partial or full remission lasting less than 2 months between episodes. No history of manic or hypomanic episodes (if present, diagnosis is bipolar disorder). Differs from single episode depressive disorder (6A7

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Major depressive disorder lifetime prevalence 15–20%; of which ~50–80% have a recurrent course. The risk of a subsequent episode increases after each episode (Solomon D.A. et al. Am J Psychiatry 2000) — ~50% after the 1st episode, ~70% after the 2nd, ~90% after the 3rd. Suicide risk higher than in single episodes, particularly in untreated episodes.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Same as single episode (6A70) — heritability ~35–40% (Sullivan 2000); GWAS loci. Relapse predisposition — predominantly cognitive predisposition, neurobiological "kindling" (Post R.M.) concept — threshold decreases for the next episode after each one. Risk factors — childhood trauma, family history, chronic stress, comorbid anxiety, somatic illness.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Same as a single episode (see 6A70 §5). In recurrent patients, additional features: Episode presentation may change from previous episodes (atypical features, seasonal pattern). Recurrent episodes typically begin more rapidly than previous ones (kindling concept — Post R.M. Am J Psychiatry 1992). Comorbid anxiety disorders with increased frequency. Chronic cognitive deficits may be observed (espec

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: History — number of previous episodes, duration, triggers, quality of remission. Bipolar screening (MDQ, HCL-32) — to rule out hypomanic episodes. PHQ-9 / HAM-D / MADRS — severity of current episode. C-SSRS — suicide risk (high in recurrent patients). Laboratory (thyroid, B12, folate, ferritin); somatic comorbidity assessment. Same as 6A70 (see single episode). Special attention — exclude bipolar

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Same as 6A70. Special attention — assessment of cognitive deficits (after multiple episodes).

title: 8. Treatment
text: After 2 episodes — continue for 1–2 years after remission. ≥3 episodes — long-term (years) or lifelong maintenance. Effective antidepressant is maintained at the same dose after remission (dose reduction increases relapse risk).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Relapse risk increases after each episode (Solomon 2000). Relapse risk significantly reduced with adequate maintenance treatment (NNT 5). Comorbid anxiety, somatic illnesses, compliance disturbance — poor prognosis. Monitoring — PHQ-9 monitoring; relapse markers; antidepressant levels and side effects; suicide risk.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6A71 Recurrent depressive disorder. 2024. APA. Practice Guideline for MDD. 2010/2019. Kennedy S.H. et al. CANMAT 2016. Can J Psychiatry 2016;61(9):540–560. Solomon D.A. et al. Multiple recurrences of major depressive disorder. Am J Psychiatry 2000;157(2):229–233. Post R.M. Transduction of psychosocial stress into the neurobiology of recurrent affective disorder. Am J Psychiatry 1992;1

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Distimik pozuntu (XBT-11: 6A72 Dysthymic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: Persistent Depressive Disorder — 300.4) — chronic depressive symptoms lasting at least 2 years (in adults; 1 year in children/adolescents) that do not meet the severity of a major depressive episode. The patient may describe their state as 'I've always been this way'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 0.5–6% (broad range; method-dependent). Sex: 2 times higher in females. Onset: early (childhood/adolescence — early-onset; ≥ 21 years — late-onset). "Double depression" — major depressive episode on a background of dysthymic disorder — occurs in 75% of patients (Klein D.N. et al. J Clin Psychiatry 2006). Comorbidity: anxiety, BPD, substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Shared genetic and neurobiological predisposition with major depressive disorder. Childhood trauma and chronic psychosocial stress are key environmental risk factors. The frequency of depressive disorder or dysthymia is increased in close relatives.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Persistent low mood — present most of the time (≥ half the time, no symptom-free period > 2 months over 2 years). At least 2 of the following: low or increased appetite, insomnia or hypersomnia, low energy, low self-esteem, concentration difficulty, hopelessness. The patient has adapted to numerous functional compromises—the explanation "I have always been like this" is typical; they often present

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview + long-term history (patient may not recall exact onset). SCID-5; PHQ-9, HAM-D, MADRS. Personality assessment – differential with BPD. Medical and laboratory (thyroid, anemia, B12).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Cornell Dysthymia Rating Scale — dysthymia-specific. Medical and laboratory.

title: 8. Treatment
text: NICE NG222 — long-term treatment for persistent depression; CBASP is noted. APA 2019 — combination superior. Keller M.B. et al. NEJM 2000 — sertraline + CBASP combination significantly superior to monotherapy.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course; spontaneous remission is rare. "Double depression" — develops in 75% of patients. Functional level improves with long-term treatment. Monitoring — periodic scales, compliance, comorbidity.

title: 11. Sources

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Premenstrual Disforik Pozuntu (XBT-11: 6A73 ; DSM-5-TR: 625.4 / N94.3) — a recurrent disorder with significant emotional and behavioral symptoms in the last week before the menstrual cycle (luteal phase), fully remitting within a few days after menses onset. Distinguished from Premenstrual Syndrome (PMS) by clinical severity and significant functional impairment.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence in reproductive-age women: 3–8% (Yonkers K.A. et al. Lancet 2008 review). PMS — milder form — in 30–80% of women. Comorbidity: major depressive disorder, anxiety, bipolar disorder — increases risk. Suicidal thoughts significantly increase in the luteal phase.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Affective lability (rapidly changing mood, tearfulness, rejection sensitivity). Irritability and angry outbursts (interpersonal conflict). Low mood, hopelessness, self-criticism. Decreased interest in usual activities. Difficulty concentrating. Lethargy and fatigue. Appetite changes (especially carbohydrate craving), excess.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Irritability or anger; Low mood or self-criticism; Difficulty concentrating; Appetite changes, excess;

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: DRSP or PRISM mood diary. PHQ-9, GAD-7 — comorbidity. Pelvic examination (gynecologist) and TSH — somatic causes excluded.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: SSRI or hormonal treatment leads to significant improvement in most patients. Persists during reproductive period; remission after menopause. Monitoring — cycle symptoms, compliance, side effects, comorbidity.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6A73 Premenstrual dysphoric disorder. 2024. Yonkers K.A., O'Brien P.M., Eriksson E. Premenstrual syndrome. Lancet 2008;371(9619):1200–1210. NEJM 1998;338(4):209–216.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Generalized anxiety disorder (ICD-11: 6B00 Generalised Anxiety Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 300.02) — a disorder characterized by excessive anxiety that persists for at least several months (≥ 6 months in DSM-5-TR), encompasses multiple life domains (work, family, health, daily topics), is uncontrollable, and lacks sufficient triggers. It is accompanied by significant somatic symptoms (tension, fatigue, sl

title: 2. History
text: 1920s–1960s — term 'anxiety neurosis', linked to Freud's influence.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 3–9% (Kessler R.C. et al. WMH summaries). Annual prevalence: ~2–3%. Sex: 2 times higher in females. Onset — bimodal — adolescence and middle age; often chronic course. Comorbidity: MDD ~60%, social anxiety, panic, substance use, somatic (irritable bowel, cardiac complaints).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — amygdala hyperresponsivity, impaired prefrontal-amygdala regulation; GABAergic and serotonergic dysregulation. Personality traits (neuroticism, behavioral inhibition) are high-risk factors. Environment — childhood trauma, chronic stress, parental overprotection.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Uncontrollable worry covering multiple areas. "Worry about worry" — meta-worry (Wells A. concept). Difficulty concentrating, mind going blank. Sleep disturbance (difficulty falling asleep, difficulty staying asleep). Autonomic symptoms (tachycardia, sweating, dry mouth, headache).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Restlessness, irritability, feeling of wandering off; Difficulty concentrating; Clinical interview (areas of concern, triggers, somatic symptoms, avoidance).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: GAD-7, HAM-A, PSWQ (Penn State Worry Questionnaire). Medical and laboratory (TSH, complete blood count, caffeine and alcohol use assessment). EKG (if cardiac complaint present).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Step 1: psychoeducation, observation. Step 2: guided self-help (internet-based CBT), psychoeducational groups. Step 3: High-intensity CBT or pharmacotherapy. Step 4: Specialized service (combination, refractory). Buspirone — partial effect; alternative to benzodiazepine. Mirtazapine — adjunct, if sleep disturbance present.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course; spontaneous remission is low. Significant symptom reduction and functional improvement with adequate treatment. Komorbid MDD pis proqnoz markeri. Monitoring — GAD-7 monitoring, side effects, compliance, comorbidity.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Panik pozuntu (XBT-11: 6B01 DSM-5-TR: 300.01) — a disorder characterized by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks (acute episodes of fear or anxiety peaking within 10 minutes) and persistent worry about recurrence of these attacks, with behavioral changes.

title: 2. History
text: Da Costa J.M. (1871) 'irritable heart' — in American Civil War soldiers. Freud (1894) "anxiety neurosis". Klein D.F. (1964) — distinguishing antidepressant response of panic attacks; basis for splitting 'anxiety neurosis'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 2–5%; annual ~2.7% (Kessler R.C. WMH). Sex: 2 times higher in females. Onset: young to middle adulthood (20–30 years); second peak in middle age. Comorbidity: agoraphobia 30–50%, MDD 50–60%, other anxiety disorders, substance use, mitral valve prolapse.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability ~40% (Hettema 2001 meta-analysis). Neurobiological — amygdala-locus coeruleus-prefrontal circuit hyperreactivity; serotonergic, noradrenergic, GABAergic dysregulation. CO₂ sensitivity hypothesis (Klein D.F. 'suffocation false alarm') — patients show sensitivity to hyperventilation and CO₂ accumulation. Personality - anxiety sensitivity (Reiss S.); childhood history of separation anxie

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Palpitations, fluttering, tachycardia. Cold sweat, trembling. Shortness of breath, feeling of air insufficiency. Feeling of suffocation. Chest pain or discomfort. Nausea, abdominal discomfort. Dizziness, unreal feeling. Cold or hot sensations; paresthesia.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Persistent worry about next attack; Fear of consequences of attacks (heart attack, going crazy); Significant behavioral change associated with attacks (avoidance, safety behaviors). NICE CG113 — diagnosis after exclusion of somatic cause. Clinical interview + attack history.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PDSS, ACQ (Agoraphobic Cognitions), BSQ (Body Sensations Questionnaire). EKG, TSH, complete blood count, glucose. Holter ECG (suspected arrhythmia).

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT for panic — psychoeducation, interoceptive exposure (tolerance to bodily signals without judgment), in vivo exposure (to avoided situations), cognitive restructuring. 12–16 sessions. Evidence: Cuijpers P. et al. reviews — large effect. Pharmacotherapy: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, paroxetine — FDA) or SNRIs (venlafaxine) — first-line. Start at low dose (jittery, 'activation' effect may wor

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Remission with treatment in most patients. Comorbid agoraphobia or MDD poor prognosis. Early intervention prevents crystallization of avoidance behavior.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B01 Panic disorder. 2024. NICE CG113. 2011/2020. Katzman M.A. et al. CANMAT 2014. BMC Psychiatry 2014;14(Suppl 1):S1. Barlow D.H. et al. Cognitive-behavioral therapy, imipramine, or their combination for panic disorder. JAMA 2000;283(19):2529–2536. Shear M.K. et al. Multicenter collaborative panic disorder severity scale. title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Aqorafobiya (XBT-11: 6B02 DSM-5-TR: 300.22) — excessive fear and avoidance of situations from which escape is difficult or help may not be available (public transportation, open spaces, enclosed spaces, crowds, leaving home alone). The patient fears developing panic attacks or other incapacitating symptoms.

title: 2. History
text: Westphal C. (1871) "agoraphobia" — Greek "agora" (square) + fear.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 1–2.5%. Sex: 2 times higher in females. Onset: young to middle adulthood. Comorbidity: panic disorder 50%, MDD, other anxiety, substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability ~60% (Mosing M.A. et al. PLoS One 2009). Classical conditioning — fear transfer to a specific situation when a panic attack occurs in that situation. Cognitive factors — catastrophizing ("help won't come").

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Typical avoided situations: Public transportation (bus, train, airplane). Open areas (square, river). Closed spaces (store, cinema theater). The patient displays avoidance behaviors, "safety" behaviors (carrying a companion, mobile phone, medication), partial or complete home confinement. In severe cases, housebound.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Public transportation; NICE CG113 — agoraphobia with panic disorder: similar intervention. Clinical interview — avoided situations, panic attack history, functional burden.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: GAD-7, PHQ-9, PDSS komorbidlik.

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT — exposure-based; graded in vivo exposure to avoided situations; cognitive restructuring. First-line; large effect. SSRI or SNRI — first-line pharmacotherapy (such as panic disorder). Benzodiazepine — only short-term. Virtual reality exposure — alternative means; evidence base developing Treatment duration — ≥ 12 months after remission. NICE CG113 — CBT and SSRIs.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Exposure CBT — most patients achieve functional recovery. Chronicity without early intervention (housebound). Komorbid MDD pis proqnoz.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B02 Agoraphobia. 2024. NICE CG113. 2011/2020. Sánchez-Meca J. et al. Psychological treatment of panic disorder with or without agoraphobia: a meta-analysis. Virtual reality exposure in the treatment of panic disor

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Spesifik fobiya (XBT-11: 6B03 DSM-5-TR: 300.29) — marked, irrational fear and avoidance of a specific object or situation (animals, blood-injection, heights, flying, enclosed spaces). The patient usually recognizes the fear as excessive.

title: 2. History
text: Hippocrates (5th century BC) — descriptions of phobia. Westphal C. (1872) — systematic classification. Marks I.M. (1969) — systematic desensitization and exposure studies.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence 7–12% — one of the most common anxiety disorders. Sex: 2–3 times higher in females (blood-injection subtype exception — difference small). Onset: childhood (animal, blood-injection), young adulthood (situational).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability ~30% (Hettema 2001). Classical conditioning (Watson J.B. 'Little Albert', 1920), vicarious learning, information transmission. Biological preparedness (Seligman M.E. "preparedness") — genetic predisposition to certain objects (snakes, spiders). In the blood-injection subtype, the vasovagal response (bradycardia, hypotension, loss of consciousness) distinguishes it from other phobias.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE — no separate specific phobia guideline; general anxiety principles. FSS (Fear Survey Schedule), specific phobia subscales. Functional burden assessment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: FSS, specific phobia scale. BAT (Behavioral Avoidance Test) — the degree to which the patient approaches the phobic object.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Stepped in vivo exposure — adapted to patient's pace. Virtual Reality Exposure — particularly suitable for flight, height Pharmacotherapy has no primary role; benzodiazepines only for specific events (e.g., pre-flight) as short-term adjunct; may sometimes reduce efficacy of CBT exposure. D-cycloserine adjunct — may enhance exposure efficacy (evidence base developing).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Exposure CBT — majority of patients achieve remission. Without treatment, chronicity.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B03 Specific phobia. 2024. Öst L.G. One-session treatment for specific phobias. Behav Res Ther 1989;27(1):1–7. Wolitzky-Taylor K.B., Horowitz J.D., Powers M.B., Telch M.J. Psychological approaches in the treatment of specific phobias: a meta-analysis. Clin Psychol Rev 2008;28(6):1021–1037. Hettema J.M. et al. Am J Psychiatry 2001;158(10):1568–1578.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Social anxiety disorder (ICD-11: 6B04 Social Anxiety Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 300.23) — intense and persistent fear of negative evaluation or humiliation by others in social situations. The patient avoids situations such as speaking, interaction, and being observed.

title: 2. History
text: Janet P. (1903) — 'phobie des situations sociales'. Marks I.M., Gelder M.G. (1966) — separation of social phobia from agoraphobia.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 7–13% (Kessler WMH; cultural variability). Sex: relatively higher in females, but males seek clinical help more often. Onset: adolescence (mean 13 years) — one of the earliest onset anxiety disorders. Comorbidity: MDD 60%, other anxiety, alcohol use (as self-medication).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability ~30–40%. Behavioral inhibition (Kagan J.) — childhood temperament as a strong risk factor. Neurobiological — amygdala hyperreactivity to social stimuli; serotonergic dysregulation. Cognitive — self-focused attention, negative self-image, catastrophizing (Clark D.M., Wells A. model). Environment — childhood peer victimization, parental overprotection.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Feared situations – exit, asking questions, sharing meals, writing or eating in front of others, public toilet, speaking on the phone. Somatic — flushing, tremor, sweating, tachycardia, dry mouth, voice tremor. Cognitive — negative thoughts about oneself, "post-event processing" (prolonged self-criticism after a situation). Avoidance and 'safety' behaviors (avoiding eye contact, speaking less, alc

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE CG159 (Social anxiety disorder: recognition, assessment and treatment, 2013) — individual CBT first-line. Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS), SPIN (Social Phobia Inventory) — scale and monitoring. Comorbidity (MDD, other anxiety, alcohol use).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: LSAS — gold standard scale (24 items). SPIN — short screening (17 items). BFNE (Brief Fear of Negative Evaluation).

title: 8. Treatment
text: NICE CG159 (2013) — individual CBT (Clark) first-line; SSRIs alternative; group CBT or self-help may be suitable for some patients. CANMAT 2014 — first-line escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, venlafaxine. Mayo-Wilson E. et al. Lancet Psychiatry 2014 network meta-analysis — individual CBT has the greatest effect; SSRIs second.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course, high relapse rate without treatment. CBT and SSRIs result in significant improvement in most patients. Early onset and avoidant personality comorbidity as a poor prognosis marker.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B04 Social anxiety disorder. 2024. NICE CG159. Social anxiety disorder: recognition, assessment and treatment. 2013. Katzman M.A. et al. CANMAT 2014. BMC Psychiatry 2014;14(Suppl 1):S1. Mayo-Wilson E. et al. Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis. Lancet Psychiatry 2014;1(5):368–376.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Separation anxiety disorder (ICD-11: 6B05 DSM-5-TR: 309.21) — developmentally inappropriate excessive fear and anxiety related to separation from an attachment figure (typically parent) or home. First diagnosable in adults in DSM-5 (limited to childhood in DSM-IV).

title: 2. History
text: Bowlby J. (1969–1980) — attachment theory.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Spread in children: ~4%; in adolescents ~1.6%; in adults ~1.9% (Kessler R.C. NCS-R). Sex: relatively higher in females. Onset: typical childhood (6–9 years); may recur or persist in adult form. Comorbidity: specific phobia, panic, GAD, MDD, school avoidance.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Attachment styles (insecure attachment), parental overprotection. History of loss or illness in relatives. Stressful events (moving, school change, parental separation).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Excessive worry about harm or loss to the attachment figure. Avoidance of leaving home or separating from attachment figure. Not sleeping in bed (bedroom, unfamiliar house). Nightmares about migration or separation. Somatic complaints in anticipation of separation (abdominal pain, headache, nausea). In children — school avoidance. In adults — fear of separation from partner or children, excessive

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Excessive anxiety about separation from the attachment figure or anticipation thereof; Persistent worry about loss or harm to the attachment figure; Fear of an event that would cause separation from the attachment figure; Avoidance of home, school, or work due to fear of separation; Fear of being alone without the attachment figure; Not sleeping in a bed without the attached person;

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: SAAS, K-SADS, MASC (Multidimensional Anxiety Scale for Children). Comorbidity (depression, other anxiety).

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT (Coping Cat, FRIENDS programs for children; standard CBT for adults) — exposure-based, parental involvement critical in children. First-line. USSR — moderate to severe cases or in cases of CBT failure; sertraline, fluoxetine (in children). School return programs (school refusal) — graded exposure. Family involvement — parental psychoeducation, avoidance of frequent phone calls/visits (accommod

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Remission in most children with early CBT. Without treatment, continuation in adulthood leads to risk of other anxiety disorders and depression.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B05 Separation anxiety disorder. 2024. Connolly S.D., Bernstein G.A. AACAP Practice Parameter for the Assessment and Treatment of Children and Adolescents With Anxiety Disorders. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2007;46(2):267–283. Walkup J.T. et al. CAMS: Cognitive behavioral therapy, sertraline, or a combination in childhood anxiety. NEJM 2008;359(26):2753–2766.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Selektiv mutizm (XBT-11: 6B06 ; DSM-5-TR: 313.23) — consistent failure to speak in specific social situations (e.g., school) despite mastery of language, with normal speech in other contexts (e.g., home). A typical childhood anxiety disorder, sometimes misperceived as "extreme shyness."

title: 2. History
text: Kussmaul A. (1877) — "aphasia voluntaria". Tramer M. (1934) — "elective mutism" — patient's voluntary choice expression.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 0.7–1% in school-aged children (Bergman R.L. et al. JAACAP 2002). Gender: relatively higher in girls (1.5–2:1). Onset: 2–5 years; typically detected at school entry. Comorbidity: social anxiety disorder ~70%, GAD, other anxiety, speech or language disorder.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability — anxiety in family history prevalent (~70% families have anxiety disorder). Behavioral inhibition (Kagan) — temperamental predisposition is a key factor. Comorbid developmental speech and language disorders (especially in bilingual environment) — risk factor. Environment — in bilingual families, sometimes mistakenly perceived as a language switch.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Failure to speak in specific social situations (school, public places). Normal speech at home with close ones. Sometimes the use of whispering or gestures. Academic and social impairment (answering questions, peer relationships). Comorbid shyness, social withdrawal, somatic complaints.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Parent, teacher interview. Home video recordings of speech — diagnostic confirmation. SMQ (Selective Mutism Questionnaire). Speech-language assessment (SLT) — communication disorder exclusion. Comorbid anxiety, autism, language disorder screening.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: SMQ — gold standard; SAS (School Anxiety Scale). Speech-language assessment.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Integrated Behavioral Therapy (Bergman R.L.) — manualized, with family + school involvement. School-based intervention — gradual increase in communication demands, teacher coordination. SSRI (fluoxetine, sertraline) – in moderate to severe or non-responsive to behavioral intervention cases. Black B. et al. JAACAP 1994 RCT. Reducing accommodation — stopping the parent from speaking for the child.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Speech recovers in most children with early intervention. Without treatment, chronicity and transition to social anxiety disorder.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B06 Selective mutism. 2024. Bergman R.L. et al. Prevalence and description of selective mutism in a school-based sample. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2002;41(8):938–946. Bergman R.L., Gonzalez A., Piacentini J., Keller M.L. Integrated Behavior Therapy for Selective Mutism: a randomized controlled pilot study. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2013;52(7):680–689.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD; ICD-11: 6B20 DSM-5-TR: 300.3) — recurrent, ego-dystonic obsessiyalar (unwanted, intrusive thoughts, images, impulses) or kompulsiyalar (repetitive behaviors or mental acts) characterized by the disorder. Usually both are present. The patient performs the compulsion to reduce anxiety or prevent a feared outcome.

title: 2. History
text: Esquirol J.E.D. (1838) — "monomanie raisonnante". Janet P. (1903) — 'psychasthénie'. Freud (1909) — "Rat Man" case. Skinner B.F. and Mowrer O.H. (1947) — two-factor learning theory (classical conditioning + operant avoidance); basis of ERP. Meyer V. (1966) — first clinical application of Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 1.6–2.3% (Ruscio A.M. et al. Mol Psychiatry 2010). Gender: approximately equal; relatively more common in women in adulthood, early onset in childhood in men. Onset: bimodal — prepubertal (10–12 years, males) and early adulthood (20–25 years). Comorbidity: MDD ~60%, anxiety disorders, tic disorders, autism, hoarding, BDD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability 40–50% (Mataix-Cols D. et al. JAMA Psychiatry 2013). Neurobiological — hyperactivity of the cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuit (orbitofrontal, cingulate, caudate); serotonergic dysregulation (SSRI effect); glutamatergic dysregulation. Genetic research — SLC1A1 (glutamate transporter), serotonin genes. PANDAS — rare onset of OCD following streptococcal infection (NIMH 2017

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: The patient accepts that obsessions are their own thoughts, but unwanted, irrational, or unacceptable (insight varies — DSM-5-TR insight qualifiers: good / poor / absent).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE CG31 (2005, 2019 surveillance) — diagnostics via clinical interview + Y-BOCS. SCID-5, Y-BOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) — the gold standard severity scale; CY-BOCS for children. OCI-R (Obsessive Compulsive Inventory-Revised) — self-assessment. Comorbidity (depression, anxiety, tic, autism, BDD, hoarding).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Y-BOCS / CY-BOCS — gold standard. OCI-R — self-assessment. Comorbidity (depression, anxiety).

title: 8. Treatment
text: NICE CG31 (2005, 2019 surveillance) — stepped approach by severity; ERP first-line; SSRIs for moderate-severe. APA Practice Guideline for OCD (2007) — same; clomipramine strong based on evidence, but second-line due to side effects. WFSBP (Bandelow B. et al. 2008) — SSRI dose ranges and titration NICE CG31 PANDAS — routine antibiotic prophylaxis not recommended.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course, but significant improvement with adequate treatment. Komorbid depressiya, tikli, autizm pis proqnoz markeri. Early onset and poor insight — more difficult treatment.
