title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD; ICD-11: 6B21 DSM-5-TR: 300.7) — obsessive preoccupation with an imagined or very slight defect in appearance; this preoccupation leads to repetitive behaviors (mirror checking, skin picking, seeking cosmetic procedures) or mental acts (comparing).

title: 2. History
text: Morselli E. (1891) — 'dysmorphophobia'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence in general population: 1.7–2.4% (Buhlmann U. et al. Psychiatry Res 2010). 9–15% in cosmetic dermatology and plastic surgery clinics. Gender: approximately equal. Onset: mean 15–16 years. Comorbidity: MDD ~75%, OCD ~30%, social anxiety ~37%, substance use. Suicide risk high: attempt ~24–28%, completion rate 45 times higher than population (based on reviews by Phillips K.A.).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Genetic overlap with OCD. Neurobiological — orbitofrontal-striatal circuit hyperactivity (similar to OCD); impaired visual processing (Feusner J.D. et al. reviews — patient overfocuses on details, fails to integrate the global image). Social-cultural — idealized appearance in mass media. Childhood peer victimization (humiliation, "bullying").

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Obsessive focus on a specific part of appearance (face, skin, hair, nose, body shape). No actual defect or invisible/unimportant to others. Repeated behaviors: mirror checking, skin picking, comparison, seeking cosmetic procedures, clothing/makeup/camouflage. Cognitive actions: comparing oneself to others. Insight often poor; sometimes at delusional level.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Qualifiers: with muscle dysmorphia; insight (good / moderate — poor / absent / delusional). NICE CG31 — BDD and OCD joint recommendations. Clinical interview (patient often does not speak openly due to shame). BDDQ (Body Dysmorphic Disorder Questionnaire) — screening. BDD-YBOCS — severity scale. Comorbidity (depression, OCD, social anxiety, suicide risk).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: BDDQ — screening (Phillips K.A.). BDD-YBOCS — modified OCD scale, 12 items. C-SSRS — high suicide risk.

title: 8. Treatment
text: First-line psychotherapy: CBT (adapted for BDD, Wilhelm S., Phillips K.A.) — cognitive restructuring + exposure + mirror retraining + behavioral experiments. 12–22 sessions. First-line pharmacotherapy: SSRIs at high dose — similar to OCD; fluoxetine (40–80 mg), escitalopram, sertraline. Clomipramine — alternative. Augmentation — atypical antipsychotic (especially insight at delusional level).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with adequate treatment; remission is possible but requires long-term maintenance. Suicide risk monitored.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Pathological Body Odor Disturbance (BOD) (ICD-11: 6B22 Olfactory Reference Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 300.3 specified within OCD spectrum or related to 300.7 BDD) — disorder characterized by obsessive belief about real or exaggerated unpleasant odor emanating from patient's body. Patient believes others condemn or embarrass him/her, exhibits compulsive washing, deodorant use, social avoidance behavior.

title: 2. History
text: Pryse-Phillips W. (1971) — term 'olfactory reference syndrome'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: precise epidemiological data limited; based on clinical and review reports rare, but underdiagnosed (Phillips K.A., Menard W. CNS Spectr 2011). Onset: early adulthood (mean 20–25 years). Gender: approximately equal. Comorbidity: MDD, social anxiety, OCD, BDD, high suicide risk (attempt ~50%).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Genetic and neurobiological overlap with OCD and BDD. Insight is variable — sometimes at delusional level. Social-cultural factors — social stigma regarding body odor.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Obsessive preoccupation with body odor (mouth, feet, armpits, genitals, general body) or breath odor. 'Misreading' others' reactions (referential ideas) — coughing, scratching nose, opening a window interpreted as 'result of smell'. Compulsive behaviors — frequent washing, changing clothes, deodorant, perfume, oral hygiene, gum chewing, vigorous tooth brushing.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Insight qualifier: good / moderate — poor / absent level. Medical and otolaryngological examination — excluding genuine halitosis, sinusitis, hyperhidrosis, gastrointestinal diseases. Trimethylaminuria (TMAU) — urine trimethylamine test (rare genetic metabolic disorder, 'fish odor syndrome'). Insight level assessment. Comorbidity and suicide risk (high!).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Medical examination — dental, ENT, gastrointestinal. TMAU testi (sidik trimetilamin). Scale - not specific; adapted use of Y-BOCS. C-SSRS (high suicide risk).

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT — approach tailored to OCD/BDD; exposure (cessation of checking behavior, return to social situations), cognitive restructuring. SSRI high dose — similar to OCD. Augmentation — atypical antipsychotic (insight at delusional level). Sosial reabilitasiya. Suicide risk monitoring.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Evidence base is limited. Improvement with SSRIs + CBT; suicide risk monitored.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B22 Olfactory reference disorder. 2024. Phillips K.A., Menard W. Olfactory reference syndrome. CNS Spectr 2011;16(11):245–251. Pryse-Phillips W. An olfactory reference syndrome. Acta Psychiatr Scand 1971;47(4):484–509.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Hipoxondriya (XBT-11: 6B23 Hypochondriasis; DSM-5-TR: 300.7 Illness Anxiety Disorder — new name, separated from somatic symptom disorder) — persistent preoccupation and anxiety about having or developing a serious medical illness. Patient shows repeated medical checks (clinic visits, internet "Dr. Google" searches, advice from friends) or conversely medical avoidance behavior.

title: 2. History
text: Hypochondriasis – ancient humoral theory.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 1–6% (Bleichhardt G., Hiller W. Compr Psychiatry 2007). In primary care patients: ~3–10%. Gender: approximately equal. Onset: early to middle adulthood. Komorbidlik: MDD, GAD, OKP, panik pozuntu.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Genetic overlap with OCD and anxiety disorders. Experience with childhood trauma or medical illnesses. Cognitive — catastrophizing of bodily sensations; selective attention; "medical information seeking" (cyberchondria). Modeling — high familial vulnerability to illness.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Fear of developing a serious illness (cancer, heart attack, various chronic diseases). Interpretation of normal bodily sensations (palpitations, headache, sluggish bowel) as markers of serious illness. Behavioral models (two polar): Care-seeking — repeated medical check-ups, multiple doctor consultations ('doctor shopping'), internet searches (cyberchondria), body checks.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Medical evaluation — exclusion of genuine illness (however excessive testing should be avoided). Scale - Whiteley Index, SHAI (Short Health Anxiety Inventory). Komorbidlik skrining.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Whiteley Index (14 items), SHAI. Medical examination — once complete, not repeated. PHQ-9, GAD-7 komorbidlik.

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT first-line — cognitive restructuring (catastrophizing), prevention of body checking and reassurance seeking (exposure + ritual prevention), attention training. Effect: Cooper K. et al. Behav Res Ther 2017 meta-analysis. USSR — moderate-severe condition or comorbid depression/GAD. Single physician model (single physician) — patient works only with one primary care physician; avoids multiple che

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: CBT or SSRIs result in significant improvement in most patients. Chronic without treatment; persistent use of medical resources.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Toplama pozuntusu (XBT-11: 6B24 DSM-5-TR: 300.3 Hoarding Disorder) — persistent difficulty acquiring or discarding possessions and a need to save them, leading to clutter of living spaces (to the extent that rooms cannot be used for their intended purpose). Categorized under the OCD spectrum.

title: 2. History
text: Frost R.O., Hartl T.L. (1996) — cognitive-behavioral model of hoarding.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 2–6% (Samuels J.F. et al. Behav Res Ther 2008). In older adults, prevalence is higher (~6%). Gender: approximately equal; women predominate in clinical samples. Onset: adolescence (mean 11–15 years); severity increases with age. Comorbidity: MDD ~50%, OCD ~20%, GAD, social anxiety, ADHD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — frontostriatal circuit disruption; activation patterns distinct from OCD (Tolin D.F. et al. reviews). Cognitive deficits — categorization, decision-making, attention (Frost & Hartl model). Emotional attachment to objects; loss trauma. In older adults — trigger for cognitive decline.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Persistent difficulty acquiring objects (purchasing, obtaining free, gift) or storing them. The thought of discarding or releasing objects causes significant distress. Hoarding items and occupying the living space — rooms cannot be used for their intended functional purposes (the bedroom for sleeping, the kitchen for eating, etc.). Sometimes "animal hoarding" — the mass keeping of animals.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Kvalifikator: excessive acquisition with; insight (good / moderate — poor / absent). "Animal hoarding" — although not a separate specifier, it is a clinically recognized variant; collaboration with veterinary and public health authorities is required. Home visit or photographic evidence — condition of living space (Clutter Image Rating). Scale - Saving Inventory-Revised (SI-R).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: SI-R (Saving Inventory-Revised), CIR (Clutter Image Rating). Medical and cognitive examination (MMSE/MoCA for elderly).

title: 8. Treatment
text: SSRIs — evidence base is limited; SSRIs are less effective in OCD than in depression. As adjunctive. Cognitive remediation — attention, categorization, decision-making training. Buoyancy approach — supporting the patient's internal motivation; risk of recurrent blockage with forced cleaning. Community intervention — public health, social service, fire safety.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course, severity increases with age. Significant improvement with CBT, but full remission rare. Komorbid demensiya pis proqnoz.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B24 Hoarding disorder. 2024. Frost R.O., Hartl T.L. A cognitive-behavioral model of compulsive hoarding. Behav Res Ther 1996;34(4):341–350. Steketee G., Frost R.O. Treatment for Hoarding Disorder: Therapist Guide. Oxford Univ Press; 2014. Tolin D.F., Frost R.O., Steketee G. et al. Cognitive behavioral therapy for hoarding disorder: a meta-analysis. Depress Anxiety 2015;32(3):158–166.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Body-focused repetitive behaviors (ICD-11: 6B25 Body-focused Repetitive Behaviour Disorders) — a group of disorders characterized by the patient's repetitive interference with their own body (hair, skin, nails). Main subtypes:

title: 2. History
text: Hallopeau F.H. (1889) — term "trichotillomania".

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence of trichotillomania: 0.5–2%; in clinical form, females 4:1. Excoriation: 1.4–5.4%; higher in females. Onset: adolescence (10–13 years). Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, OCD, BDD, ADHD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability 30–40%; family history prevalent. Genetic — SAPAP3 (SLITRK1) — hypergrooming phenotype in mice. Neurobiological — OCD-like cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical dysregulation, but with a different profile. Behavioral mechanisms: stress response, 'automatic' (unconscious) and 'focused' (purposeful, with intense attention) subtypes.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Hair pulling — usually from head, eyebrows, eyelashes; sometimes from other body parts. "Pre-pulling tension" or "post-pulling relief" — may not be present in all patients. Trichophagia (eating of pulled hair) — in 10–20% of patients; 'trichobezoar' — gastrointestinal obstruction may require surgery. Skin picking — face, arm, damaged skin areas ("acne", insect bite site).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview (patient often does not speak openly due to shame). Dermatological examination — exclusion of other causes of hair loss and skin lesions (alopecia areata, tinea capitis, eczema). Scale — Massachusetts General Hospital Hairpulling Scale (MGH-HPS), Skin Picking Scale. Komorbidlik skrining. Trichophagia screening — if gastrointestinal symptoms present.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MGH-HPS (trikotilomaniya), Skin Picking Scale-Revised. Dermatological examination. Abdominal ultrasound in case of trichophagia.

title: 8. Treatment
text: ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy) — targets emotional component. Comprehensive Behavioral Treatment (ComB, Mansueto) — HRT + multimodal behavioral intervention. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — evidence base developing; Grant J.E. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2009 RCT — efficacy in trichotillomania. SSRI — evidence base is limited; effective in OCD, SSRI has weak response in trichotillomania; clomipra

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course, "waxing and waning". Significant improvement with HRT; treatment of comorbidity is essential.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B25 Body-focused repetitive behaviour disorders. 2024. Bloch M.H. et al. Systematic review: pharmacological and behavioral treatment for trichotillomania. Biol Psychiatry 2007;62(8):839–846. Grant J.E. et al. N-acetylcysteine, a glutamate modulator, in the treatment of trichotillomania: a double-blind, placebo-controlled study. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2009;66(7):756–763.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD; ICD-11: 6B40 DSM-5-TR: 309.81) — a disorder developing after direct or witnessed exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence, characterized by three main symptom domains: Re-experiencing (intrusions), Avoidance , persistent apprehension (XBT-11) / Cognitive-emotional changes and hyperarousal (DSM-5-TR).

title: 2. History
text: "Shell shock" (World War I), "combat fatigue" (World War II).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 3.9% (WHO World Mental Health), in USA ~6.8%. Sex: 2 times higher in females (due to risk of sexual violence exposure). Risk by trauma type: sexual violence ~50% PTSD, physical assault ~25%, accident ~10%, natural disaster ~5%. Comorbidity: MDD ~50%, GAD, panic, substance use, BPD, complex PTSD. Suicide risk significantly high.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — amygdala hyperreactivity, impaired prefrontal-amygdala regulation, hippocampal volume reduction, HPA axis dysregulation (paradoxically low cortisol). Trauma characteristics — severity, duration, perpetrated by others (intentional), occurrence in childhood. Risk factors — previous trauma, previous psychiatric history, lack of social support, female gender, peritraumatic dissociati

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Negative beliefs (about self, others, the world). Anhedonia, social isolation. Numbing of feelings, persistent negative mood. Symptoms ≥ 1 month; onset may be delayed after trauma (delayed onset > 6 months).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview + trauma history. CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale) — gold standard structured interview. PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist) — self-assessment screening, 20 items. Comorbidity (MDD, GAD, substance, BPD, complex PTSD). Suicid riski (C-SSRS). Except for medical and traumatic brain injury (TBI).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: CAPS-5 — gold standard. PCL-5 (20 items) — screening. PHQ-9, GAD-7, C-SSRS — comorbidity. Trauma history (LEC-5 — Life Events Checklist).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Treatment duration — psychotherapy 12–16 sessions; pharmacotherapy ≥ 12 months after remission.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Trauma-focused psychotherapy yields significant improvement in most patients. Chronicity without early intervention. Comorbid MDD, substance use poor prognosis markers.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B40 Post traumatic stress disorder. 2024. Department of Veterans Affairs / Department of Defense. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder and Acute Stress Disorder. 2023. APA. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of PTSD. 2017. NICE NG116. Post-traumatic stress disorder. 2018. Bisson J.I., Roberts N.P., Andrew M. et al. Psychological t

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Kompleks PTSP (XBT-11: 6B41 Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder) — a disorder that develops in response to prolonged or repeated trauma from which escape is difficult or impossible (prolonged abuse, childhood physical or sexual violence, war, torture, human trafficking). It includes the three symptom domains of PTSD (re-experiencing, avoidance, persistent sense of threat). "Disturbance of self-

title: 2. History
text: Herman J.L. (1992) — "Complex PTSD" concept (book "Trauma and Recovery"); "Disorder of Extreme Stress Not Otherwise Specified" (DESNOS) — not included in DSM-IV.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 1–8% among trauma-exposed patients in primary care (summary by Brewin C.R. et al.). Particularly high in patients with a history of childhood abuse. Comorbidity: MDD, GAD, dissociative disorders, BPD (clinical overlap), substance use, eating disorders.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Trauma type — prolonged, repeated, perpetrated by others (interpersonal), difficult escape. Trauma occurring in childhood — broad impact on personality development. Neurobiological — similar to PTSD but with more widespread HPA dysregulation, dissociation, and impaired affect regulation.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Re-experiencing (intrusives). Persistent sense of threat.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Affect Dysregulation; Negative self-esteem; Difficulty in relationships. Clinical interview + trauma history (long-term, repetitive). ITQ (International Trauma Questionnaire — Cloitre M.) — designed for differentiating PTSD and cPTSD. Comorbidity (especially BPD, dissociative disorders). Assessment of suicide and self-harm risk.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: ITQ (International Trauma Questionnaire) — gold standard. PCL-5 + DSO additional scales. BPD skrining (McLean Skrinninq Instrument for BPD). Suicide and self-harm (C-SSRS).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Skills Training in Affective and Interpersonal Regulation (STAIR — Cloitre M.) + or pre-trauma phase 1 intervention. Trauma-focused CBT and EMDR — adapted format. DBT-PTSD (Bohus M.) — in comorbid BPD cases. SSRI adjunct — comorbid depression and anxiety. Duration of treatment is long (1–2 years or more). Clinician continuity and therapeutic alliance are critically important.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course; but significant improvement with phased approach. Comorbid BPD, dissociative disorders, substance use poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B41 Complex post traumatic stress disorder. 2024. Cloitre M., Garvert D.W., Brewin C.R. et al. Evidence for proposed ICD-11 PTSD and complex PTSD: a latent profile analysis. Eur J Psychotraumatol 2013;4:20706. ISTSS Complex PTSD Treatment Guidelines. International Society for Traumatic Stress Studies; 2019. Herman J.L. Trauma and Recovery. Basic Books; 1992.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Prolonged grief disorder (ICD-11: 6B42 Prolonged Grief Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 309.89 — added in 2022) — a disorder of grief and preoccupation lasting longer than cultural and religious expectations following the loss of a close person, causing significant functional impairment. Difference from normal grief: duration, intensity, and functional burden; clinical diagnosis based on specific duration (ICD

title: 2. History
text: Lindemann E. (1944) — studies on acute grief dynamics. Bowlby J. (1980) — attachment and loss. Prigerson H.G. et al. (1995–2009) — "Complicated Grief" / "Prolonged Grief" concept; development of diagnostic criteria.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Development after close loss: 7–10% in general population, 49% in case of sudden/violent death (Lundorff M. et al. J Affect Disord 2017 meta-analysis). Gender: higher in women. Risk factors: early age loss, sudden or violent death, child loss, anxious/ambivalent attachment. COVID-19 pandemic — significantly increased the prevalence of prolonged grief (Eisma M.C. et al.).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Attachment theory — impairment in cognitive processing of separation from a close attachment figure. Neurobiological — nostalgia-related activation of the reward circuit in response to compensated attachment (O'Connor M.F. fMRI studies). Cognitive — "stuck" thoughts, lack of healthy interpretation.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Persistent, intense 'nostalgia' or preoccupation with the lost person. Persistent symptoms from the following: Disturbance of the sense of self ("a part of me has died") Difficulty in impulse acceptance; Emotional pain related to loss (grief, anger, guilt); Loss of meaning in life; Avoidance of social activities; Difficulty maintaining relationships.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Disturbance of sense of self; Difficulty accepting loss; Avoidance of reminder stimuli; Intense emotional pain; Difficulty returning to social or other activities. Loss of meaning in life; Feelings of loneliness and isolation.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PG-13 (Prolonged Grief Disorder-13, Prigerson H.G.) and PG-13-R update. ICG (Inventory of Complicated Grief) — 19 items. PHQ-9, C-SSRS komorbidlik.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Family and group support. Suicid riski monitor. Shear M.K. reviews and RCTs — CBT gold standard. Wittouck C. et al. Clin Psychol Rev 2011 meta-analysis — grief-specific interventions superior to universal grief support in prolonged grief.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement in most patients with CGT. Without treatment, chronicity and development of comorbid MDD/PTSD.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B42 Prolonged grief disorder. 2024. Shear M.K., Frank E., Houck P.R., Reynolds C.F. Treatment of complicated grief: a randomized controlled trial. JAMA 2005;293(21):2601–2608. Shear M.K. et al. Optimizing treatment of complicated grief: a randomized clinical trial. JAMA Psychiatry 2016;73(7):685–694. Prigerson H.G. et al. title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Adaptasiya pozuntusu (XBT-11: 6B43 Adjustment Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 309.x) — clinically significant emotional or behavioral symptoms that have developed in response to an identifiable stressor (acute or persistent, individual or collective). Symptoms do not meet full criteria for another mental disorder (MDD, GAD, etc.).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Among the most frequent psychiatric diagnoses in primary care; in clinical populations 5–20%. Sex and age — universal; more intense at young age and during new stress periods. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, substance use, suicide risk (especially in short-term acute form).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Stress factor - a single event (divorce, job loss, illness diagnosis, grief) or multiple/persistent stressors. The role of personality and coping resources — the same stress elicits different responses in different people. Social support — protective factor.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Preoccupation with the stress factor - recurrent thoughts, worry. Difficulty adapting to new situations. Emotional symptoms — depressed mood, anxiety, irritability. Behavioral symptoms — social withdrawal, decreased work/school performance, risky behavior With depressive mood (309.0); With anxiety (309.24); With mixed depressive mood and anxiety (309.28);

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview — stressor, symptom onset, prior psychiatric history. Scale - PHQ-9, GAD-7 symptom severity. MDD, GAD, PTSD, complex PTSD exclusion. Suicide risk (C-SSRS) — acutely particularly high.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PHQ-9, GAD-7, C-SSRS. Documentation of the stress factor.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Stress management, strengthening social support, development of coping strategies. Pharmacotherapy — only in severe cases or comorbid; SSRIs short-term; benzodiazepines for acute anxiety for 1–2 weeks. Suicid riski monitor. Removal or modification of the stress factor (if possible). NICE — no separate guideline for adjustment disorder; general anxiety and depression principles.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: In most cases remission within 6 months. Some persist or transition to MDD/PTSD — follow-up recommended.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B43 Adjustment disorder. 2024. Maercker A. et al. ICD-11 stress-related disorders. World Psychiatry 2013;12(3):198–206. Casey P. Adjustment disorder: epidemiology, diagnosis and treatment. CNS Drugs 2009;23(11):927–938.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Reactive attachment disorder (ICD-11: 6B44 ; DSM-5-TR: 313.89 Reactive Attachment Disorder) — a disturbance of attachment behavior developing in early childhood due to severe psychosocial deprivation or inadequate care; the child does not seek comfort from the caregiver when distressed, or this seeking is markedly reduced.

title: 2. History
text: Bowlby J. (1951) — maternal deprivation report (WHO). Spitz R.A. (1945) — "hospitalism" in institutionalized children. Nelson C.A. et al. Science 2007 — Bucharest Early Intervention Project — effect of institutional deprivation and early fostering.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Rare in the general population; in at-risk populations (institutionalized children, severe neglect) ~10–40%. Comorbidity: cognitive deficits, neurodevelopmental disorders, depressive symptoms.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Severe psychosocial deprivation — institutionalism, multiple placements, neglect. Disruption of caregiver-child interaction in the critical early period up to 5 years. Neurobiology of the attachment system — oxytocin, prefrontal-amygdala circuit.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Not seeking help from caregiver or minimal attempt while distressed Difficulty accepting comfort. Reduction of social and emotional reactions (withdrawal, irritability, sadness). Reduction of positive affect. Withdrawn, hypovigilant behavior.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Interview with caregiver or social worker — care history (institutionalization, placement changes, neglect). Structured interview (DAI — Disturbances of Attachment Interview, Smyke A.T., Zeanah C.H.). Clinical observation of child-caregiver interaction. Autism spectrum exclusion (M-CHAT-R/F, ADOS). Cognitive and developmental assessment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: DAI (Disturbances of Attachment Interview). Strange Situation Procedure (Ainsworth M.) - within research context. Developmental assessment (Bayley, Mullen).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Interventions focused on carer-child interaction – Attachment and Biobehavioral Catch-up (ABC, Dozier M.), Child-Parent Psychotherapy (CPP, Lieberman A.). Trauma-focused approach — TF-CBT added in trauma context. Comorbid disorders (depression, anxiety, ADHD) treatment. Pharmacotherapy — no specific approval for RAD itself; for comorbid symptoms. "Holding therapy", "rebirthing" — dangerous , is co

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with early stable care and intervention. Long-term institutional deprivation — residual cognitive and emotional deficits.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Social Participation Restriction Disorder (ICD-11: 6B45 ; DSM-5-TR: 313.89 Disinhibited Social Engagement Disorder, DSED) — excessive and inappropriate friendliness toward strangers in a child, with approach or departure behavior without caution — resulting from early psychosocial deprivation (institutionalization, multiple placements). It shares the same etiological root as RAD but presents with

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Rare in the general population; in institutionalized children 20%, in foster children 10–15%. Significant reduction with early fostering (Bucharest Early Intervention Project), but unlike RAD, DSED sometimes persists even in stable care environments.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Severe psychosocial deprivation — same as RAD. Institutional environment with multiple caregiver rotation — formation of 'promiscuous attachment'.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Open approach to strangers, going with them without caution. Reckless physical closeness (hugging, sitting). Carer monitoring reduces behavior in interactions with strangers. Absence of stranger danger perception. Academic and social impairment.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Reduced or no caution in approaching strangers; Physical or verbal intimacy inappropriate for culture or age; Reduced checking-seeking behavior for caregiver in unfamiliar place; Minimal or no caution about going with strangers. Developmental level by age must be considered — normal social interest in young children should not be confused with DSED. Interview and observation — child's interaction

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Observation in unfamiliar environment. Williams syndrome genetic test (FISH 7q11.23) — if suspected.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Consistent service environment. Parent training — developing a "safe base" concept for the child; ABC programs. Teaching appropriate distance with strangers. Comorbidity treatment (ADHD). Community and school staff coordination — child safety. There is no specific pharmacotherapy; it is for comorbid symptoms. AACAP 2016 — same guidance as for RAD. DSED, unlike RAD, can persist even in a stable env

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With stable care and intervention, improvement occurs; however, DSED sometimes persists long-term. Impulsive behaviors and risks of abuse can persist into adulthood.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID; ICD-11: 6B60 Dissociative Identity Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 300.14) — a severe dissociative disorder characterized by transitions between two or more distinct personality states ("identities", "alters"). Each identity has a relatively enduring sense of self, memory, behavior, affect, perception, and cognition patterns. Concurrently, persistent dissociative amnesias

title: 2. History
text: 1791 — Eberhardt Gmelin first case description. Janet P. (1880s) — concept of 'dissociation'. 1980s–1990s — 'False Memory' controversy; allegations of 'iatrogenic induction' in some diagnostic cases. ISSTD Treatment Guidelines (Adult Treatment, 3rd Revision 2011) — standardized the phase-oriented approach.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence in clinical populations: 1–3%; in general population ~1.5% (Sar V. ISRN Psychiatry 2011 review). Sex: 5–9 times higher in females in clinical populations. Onset: symptoms develop in childhood, clinical presentation often at 20–40 years. Comorbidity: MDD, PTSD, complex PTSD, BPD, somatic symptom, eating disorders, substance use, very high suicide risk.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — hippocampal and amygdala volume reduction; default mode and salience network dysregulation (Reinders A.A. et al. reviews). Attachment — disorganized attachment style in childhood. Iatrogenesis / supporters of the social-cognitive model — some diagnoses may be the result of therapeutic induction (critique by Lilienfeld S.O.); however, most research supports the trauma-based model.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Two or more distinct identity states — some patients document overt transitions, others appear only with "internal voices," sense of "loss of control." Persistent amnesias — daily events, personal information, traumatic episodes. Depersonalization and derealization Identity transitions as a response to stress, triggers. Self-harm, suicidal attempts, somatic symptoms.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: ISSTD Guidelines 2011 — diagnosis must be confirmed by structured interview (SCID-D). Clinical interview — trauma history, dissociative symptoms. SCID-D (Structured Clinical Interview for DSM Dissociative Disorders) is the gold standard. Scale — DES (Dissociative Experiences Scale, ≥30 cut-off, ≥45 DID indicator); MID (Multidimensional Inventory of Dissociation).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: SCID-D-R is the gold-standard structured interview. C-SSRS suicide; self-harm assessment. EEG rule out seizure.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Specific psychotherapeutic approaches — excerpt from Internal Family Systems (Schwartz R.), modified EMDR. Pharmacotherapy — no specific approval for DID; SSRIs, prazosin (for nightmares) for comorbid PTSD, MDD, anxiety symptoms. Hospitalize — in acute self-harm or suicide risk. Multidisciplinary approach — psychiatrist, psychologist, social support. Long-term treatment (years); clinician continui

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Functional recovery with long-term phase-oriented therapy. High suicide and self-harm risk monitored. Comorbidity treatment is important.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Partial Dissociative Identity Disorder (ICD-11: 6B61 Partial Dissociative Identity Disorder) — similar to DID (6B60), but Non-dominant identifications do not consistently completely occupy the patient's behavior or conscious function differs from. Instead, non-dominant identifications interfere, but do not fully seize "executive control." In ICD-11, added for the first time as an official diagnost

title: 2. History
text: New diagnostic framework for conditions recognized in clinical practice as 'DDNOS' or 'DID-NOS'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence, precise data limited — believed to be more common than DIP. Develops on trauma basis. Comorbidity resembling DID.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Same as DID — severe childhood trauma, disorganized attachment, neurobiological dissociative processes.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Two or more distinct identity states. Qeyri-dominant identifikasiyalar disrupting – internal voices, thoughts, feelings, actions – but do not fully occupy patient's behavior and consciousness. Dominant identity controls daily functioning. Less amnesia compared to DID. Distress and functional impairment.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Same as DIP (see 6B60 §6.3).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Same as DID (SCID-D-R, DES, MID).

title: 8. Treatment
text: The phase-oriented approach is adapted for DID (ISSTD 2011). For treatment principles, phases, and specific methodologies, see section 6B60 "Treatment" (including "Treatment methodologies").

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Functional recovery with phase-oriented therapy; similar to DIP.

title: 11. Sources

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Depersonalizasiya-Derealizasiya Pozuntusu (XBT-11: 6B62 DSM-5-TR: 300.6) — a dissociative disorder characterized by persistent or recurrent depersonalization (feeling detached from oneself, disconnected from body and thoughts) and/or derealization (surroundings appearing unreal, like a 'foggy film'). Reality testing is preserved (the patient knows the experience is unreal).

title: 2. History
text: Krishaber M. (1872) — first description. Dugas L. (1898) — term 'dépersonnalisation'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 1–2% in clinical form; subclinical experiences at least once in 50% of the population. Gender: approximately equal. Onset: adolescence and early adulthood (mean 16 years). Komorbidlik: MDD, panik, GAD, PTSP, BPD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Trauma and stress trigger (especially cannabis, hallucinogen use). Neurobiological — impaired prefrontal-limbic regulation; sensory processing anomalies (Simeon D. reviews). Comorbid anxiety and panic attack frequency high.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Reality testing preserved — patient knows the experience is unreal. Significant distress and functional impairment. Typical course — continuous or recurring episodes.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: CDS (Cambridge Depersonalisation Scale, Sierra M., Berrios G.E.) — 29 items. Medical and neurological examination (excluding seizures and brain injury). Toksikoloji skrining.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: CDS is the gold standard. EEG if seizure is to be ruled out.

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT — adapted for depersonalization; cognitive restructuring + exposure. Hunter E.C. et al. Behav Res Ther 2005. SSRI — effective for comorbid depression and anxiety; evidence base for disorder itself limited. Lamotrigine adjunct — effective in some studies. Against trauma background — trauma-focused approach. Abstinence from cannabis and hallucinogen use is critical.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course, but significant improvement with CBT.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B62 Depersonalization-derealization disorder. 2024. Sierra M. Depersonalization: A New Look at a Neglected Syndrome. Cambridge Univ Press; 2009. Hunter E.C.M. et al. Cognitive-behaviour therapy for depersonalisation disorder: an open study. Behav Res Ther 2005;43(9):1121–1130. Simeon D. Depersonalisation disorder: a contemporary overview. CNS Drugs 2004;18(6):343–354.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Dissosiativ Nevroloji Simptom Pozuntusu (XBT-11: 6B63 Dissociative Neurological Symptom Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 300.11 Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder / Conversion Disorder) — impairment of motor, sensory, or cognitive functions under voluntary control, not fully explained by a neurological or medical illness. Previously termed "conversion disorder" or "hysteria".

title: 2. History
text: Charcot J.M. (19th century) — Studies on 'hysteria' at Salpêtrière. Freud S. — psychoanalytic "conversion" model.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: In primary care and neurological clinics — 5–10% (reviews by Carson A.J. et al.). Sex: 2–3 times higher in females. Onset: adulthood, but also in children and the elderly. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, trauma history, somatic illnesses.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Trauma or stress trigger (but in 25% no explicit trigger identified). Neurobiological — impaired sensorimotor and cognitive prediction processing; predictive coding hypothesis (Edwards M.J.). "Functional" term — not telling the patient it is "made up", but a functional disorder of the nervous system.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Patterns differing from typical neurological pathogenesis (e.g., Hoover sign in motor weakness — positive indicator of functional component). Distractibility — symptom changes when attention is directed elsewhere. Entrainability — tremor adapts to another rhythm.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Neurological examination — functional positive features (Hoover, entrainability, distractibility). Typical neurological condition exception — MRI, EEG, EMG, lumbar puncture (according to clinical presentation). In PNES cases — video-EEG monitoring gold standard. Psychiatric evaluation — comorbid trauma, MDD, anxiety. The patient's diagnosis was explained positively — "not fabricated, but a functio

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Neurological examination (positive features). Video-EEG — in PNES cases MRI, laboratory exclusion of neurological disease. Trauma and psychiatric assessment.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Multidisciplinary approach — neurologist + psychiatrist + physiotherapist. Physiotherapy (for motor symptoms) — evidence-based benefit (Nielsen G. et al. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 2017 RCT). CBT — cognitive restructuring; specific protocols for PNES (LaFrance W.C. JAMA Psychiatry 2014). Against trauma background — trauma-focused approach. Anticonvulsants for PNES. ineffective and harmful — acc

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Improvement with early accurate diagnosis and intervention. Late diagnosis and polypharmacy — chronicity.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B63 Dissociative neurological symptom disorder. 2024. Stone J., Carson A. Functional neurological disorders: the neurological assessment as treatment. Pract Neurol 2016;16(1):7–17. Nielsen G. et al. Physiotherapy for functional motor disorders: a consensus recommendation. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 2015;86(10):1113–1119. LaFrance W.C. et al. Multicenter pilot treatment trial for p

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Dissosiativ Amneziya (XBT-11: 6B64 DSM-5-TR: 300.12) — inability to recall important autobiographical information (usually traumatic or stressful), not explained by ordinary forgetfulness. No medical or neurological cause. Subtypes: localized (specific period), selective (part of the period), generalized (entire life history), systematized (specific category), continuous.

title: 2. History
text: Janet P. — originator of the concept of dissociation. "False Memory" controversy (1990s) — debate over therapeutically induced "recovered memory".

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 1–2%; higher after public misfortunes (war, disaster). Sex: relatively higher in females. Comorbidity: PTSD, cPTSD, DID, BPD, MDD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Trauma — response to severe psychological or physical trauma; hypothesized as 'defense mechanism'. Neurobiological — impaired hippocampal and prefrontal cognitive processing.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Inability to recall the traumatic or stressful event. Localized (e.g., wartime), selective, generalized. Dissociative fugue — unexpected travel, new identity, usually lasts days to weeks. Implicit memory (skills) remains; explicit memory is selective.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Kvalifikator: with dissociative fugue. Clinical interview + trauma history. Medical and neurological examination — TBI, dementia, encephalitis, seizure excluded. MRI, EEG in clinical suspicion.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Neuropsychological test (memory profile).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Safe environment and supportive psychotherapy. Trauma-focused psychotherapy — phased approach. During a fugue episode — reuniting the patient with family and community. Pharmacotherapy for comorbid symptoms; 'amobarbital interview' or hypnosis was historically used — caution due to risk of induced false memory. Long-term psychotherapy — trauma processing.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Most spontaneous remission in acute form; Chronic form of long-term intervention.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B64 Dissociative amnesia. 2024. ISSTD. Guidelines for Treating DID in Adults, 3rd Revision. J Trauma Dissociation 2011;12(2):115–187. Loftus E.F. The reality of repressed memories. Am Psychol 1993;48(5):518–537. Staniloiu A., Markowitsch H.J. Dissociative amnesia. Lancet Psychiatry 2014;1(3):226–241.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Anorexia Nervosa (ICD-11: 6B80 DSM-5-TR: 307.1) — a severe eating disorder characterized by significant low body weight due to restriction of food intake, intense fear of weight gain, and disturbance in body image. Among the highest mortality rates of psychiatric disorders.

title: 2. History
text: Morton R. (1689) — first clinical description. Lasègue C. (1873) and Gull W. (1874) — 'anorexia hysterica' and 'nervous anorexia'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: in females 0.9–4%, in males 0.3% (Smink F.R. reviews). Sex: 10:1 in females; but under-detection in male patients. Onset: adolescence (14–18 years); second peak in middle age. Mortality: lifetime 5–6%; half medical (cachexia, cardiac), half suicide (Arcelus J. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2011 meta-analysis). Comorbidity: MDD, OCD, anxiety, BPD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability 50–60% (Bulik C.M. reviews). Neurobiological — reduced cognitive flexibility, reward circuit dysregulation, atypical response to hunger. Social-cultural factors — thinness ideal; social media influence. Personality - perfectionism, anxiety, family history of eating disorder.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Food restriction – calories, types of food. Body weight significantly low (BMI < 18.5 in adults; children BMI percentile). Marked fear of weight gain or persistent behavior aimed at preventing it. Body image disturbance — "I feel fat" even when underweight. Subtypes: Restriktiv tip — only food restriction. Medical complications — bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, electrolyte imbalance, osteop

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE NG69 (Eating disorders, 2017, update) — diagnosis and treatment algorithms. EDE-Q (Eating Disorder Examination Questionnaire) or EDE — gold standard. Medical examination — BMI, vital signs, electrolytes, ECG, bone densitometry. Laboratory: complete blood count, electrolytes (especially K, P, Mg), liver, kidney, glucose, thyroid. Comorbidity (MDD, OCD, anxiety).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Vital signs, BMI, weight history Laboratory: complete blood count, electrolytes, liver, kidney, thyroid, FSH/LH, prolactin, vitamin D, B12, iron. EKG — bradycardia, QTc.

title: 8. Treatment
text: MANTRA, SSCM adult alternatives. Nutrition — group or individual nutritionist; prophylaxis of refeeding syndrome — initial energy intake is low, phosphorus monitoring. Duration of treatment is long (1–2 years or more). NICE NG69 (2017) — FBT in children and adolescents; CBT-E in adults. APA 2023 Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients with Eating Disorders, 4th ed.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: 50% remission, 30% residual symptoms, 20% chronic course. Mortality high (5–6%). Early intervention (especially FBT in adolescence) significantly improves prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B80 Anorexia Nervosa. 2024. NICE NG69. Eating disorders: recognition and treatment. 2017, 2020 update. APA. Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients With Eating Disorders, 4th ed. 2023. Lock J., Le Grange D., Agras W.S. et al. Randomized clinical trial comparing family-based treatment with adolescent-focused individual therapy for adolescents with anorexia nervosa. Arch Gen P

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Bulimia Nervosa (ICD-11: 6B81 DSM-5-TR: 307.51) — an eating disorder characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating (large amount of food in a short period + sense of loss of control) and compensatory behaviors (vomiting, laxatives, exercise, fasting). Unlike anorexia, weight is typically normal or overweight.

title: 2. History
text: Russell G.F. (1979) — term 'bulimia nervosa' and first clinical description.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: in females 1.5–3%, in males 0.5%. Onset: adolescence and early adulthood. Comorbidity: MDD ~70%, anxiety, substance use, BPD, history of anorexia.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — serotonergic dysregulation, dopamine reward system. Social-cultural — thinness ideal, dietary history. Personality — impulsivity, perfectionism, low self-esteem.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Binge eating episodes — loss of control, secretive. Compensatory behaviors: Purging — self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics; Non-purging — fasting, excessive exercise. Body image and weight preoccupation. Medical complications: hypokalemia (vomiting), Russell's sign (calluses on fingers), dental erosion, parotid hypertrophy, esophagitis, gastrointestinal issues.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE NG69 — diagnosis and treatment. Medical: electrolytes (K, P, Mg, Cl), liver, kidney, ECG (QTc). Dental examination (erosion). Comorbidity and suicide risk.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Medical: electrolytes, ECG, dental examination.

title: 8. Treatment
text: FBT (Lock J., Le Grange D.) — in adolescents. Medical: electrolyte correction, refeeding, dental care. Bupropion Out-of-body (tutma riski). NICE NG69 — CBT-E first-line; FBT in adolescents.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: 50–70% remission with CBT-E; long-term follow-up is essential.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B81 Bulimia Nervosa. 2024. NICE NG69. 2017/2020. APA. Practice Guideline for Eating Disorders, 4th ed. 2023. Fairburn C.G. Cognitive Behavior Therapy and Eating Disorders. Guilford Press; 2008. Russell G. Bulimia nervosa: an ominous variant of anorexia nervosa. Psychol Med 1979;9(3):429–448.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ICD-11: 6B82 DSM-5-TR: 307.51) — recurrent episodes of binge eating (large amount of food in a short period + sense of loss of control) Without compensatory behaviors . Typically, weight is increased and associated with obesity.

title: 2. History
text: Stunkard A.J. (1959) — description of binge eating in the context of obesity.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 1.9–3.5%; most common among eating disorders. Sex: 1.5–2:1 in females; but also significant in males. Strong association with obesity. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, BPD, substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — reward circuit dysregulation; 'food addiction' hypothesis. Emotional eating — stress trigger. History of restrictive diet.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Binge eating episodes — large amount of food in short time, loss of control. Typical features: rapid eating, continuing despite fullness, secretive eating, subsequent shame, guilt. No compensatory behavior. Weight gain and obesity complications (Type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular, OSA).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Eating until physically full; Binge eating without hunger; Secretive eating (due to shame); Following stub or depressed mood.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: BMI, weight, vital signs.

title: 8. Treatment
text: SSRI — for comorbid MDD; modest effect on binge eating frequency. Weight management — diet, physical activity; weight loss is not effective without BED intervention. Bariatric surgery requires long-term follow-up (BED preoperative assessment). NICE NG69 — CBT or guided self-help first-line. McElroy lisdexamfetamine RCTs.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: 50–60% remission with CBT-E. Weight management and metabolic complications require long-term monitoring.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B82 Binge eating disorder. 2024. NICE NG69. 2017/2020. McElroy S.L. et al. Efficacy and safety of lisdexamfetamine for treatment of adults with moderate to severe binge eating disorder. JAMA Psychiatry 2015;72(3):235–246. Wilson G.T., Wilfley D.E., Agras W.S., Bryson S.W. Psychological treatments of binge eating disorder. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2010;67(1):94–101.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Pika (XBT-11: 6B83 DSM-5-TR: 307.52) — persistent (≥ 1 month) ingestion of non-food, non-nutritive substances (clay, hair, paper, soap, metal, stones) — inappropriate for developmental age.

title: 2. History
text: "Pica" — from the Latin word for "magpie" (magpie eats everything).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: children 4–26%, higher in intellectual disability, pregnant women 8–65%, iron deficiency anemia. Comorbidity: autism, intellectual disability, iron deficiency, adult malabsorption.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Nutritional deficiencies (iron, zinc) — may also be reduced due to pica substance ingestion in some patients. Neurodevelopmental disorders (autism, ID). Pregnancy — physiological and micronutrient. Cultural — consumption of clay in some traditions (geophagy).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Geophagy (clay, soil), trichophagy (hair), pagophagy (ice), amylophagy (starch), plumbophagy (paint — lead risk). Complications: intestinal obstruction (trichobezoar), lead poisoning, parasitic infection, dental injury, perforation.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Interview + observation. Laboratory — complete blood count, ferritin, iron, lead level, zinc. Abdominal imaging (obstruction). Neurodevelopmental assessment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Iron, lead, zinc, parasite tests. Abdominal X-ray / USG.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Correction of nutritional deficiency (iron, zinc). Behavioral intervention — habit reversal, environmental modification, alternative stimuli. Intellectual disability comorbid with ASD — functional behavior analysis and ABA-based approach. Medical management of complications (lead poisoning — chelation only with specific indication). During pregnancy — nutritional support.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Remission in children through nutritional correction and behavioral intervention. May persist on an ID/ASD background; long-term follow-up.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B83 Pica. 2024. Young S.L. Pica during pregnancy: novel insights into a longstanding condition. Annu Rev Nutr 2010;30:403–422. Hyman S.L. et al. AAP 2020 Clinical Report (ASD). Pediatrics 2020;145(1):e20193447.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Ruminasiya-Geri Qaytarma Pozuntusu (XBT-11: 6B84 Rumination-Regurgitation Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 307.53 — characterized by voluntary or semi-voluntary regurgitation of swallowed food followed by re-chewing, re-swallowing, or spitting out. Not fully explained by a medical condition (gastroesophageal reflux or other GI disease).

title: 2. History
text: Term 'mericism' in ancient times.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: rare in infants and children; 6–10% in patients with intellectual disability; increasing recognition in adults (especially comorbid with eating disorders). Comorbidity: ID, ASD, anxiety, BN, BED.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Behavioral conditioning — 'post-prandial belching' followed by regurgitation as a learned behavior. In ID/ASD, self-stimulation with food.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Regurgitation minutes to hours after food intake. Eating disorder outcomes: weight loss, malnutrition, electrolyte imbalance, dental erosion, halitosis. Social consequences: shame, social isolation.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Medical assessment — exclusion of GERD, pyloric pathology, gastroparesis (upper endoscopy, motility study). Manometry — confirmation of functional regurgitation. Weight and nutritional status. Comorbid eating disorder screening.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Endoskopiya, manometriya, pH-monitorinq. Complete blood count, electrolytes.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Diaphragmatic breathing — first-line behavioral intervention (Chitkara D.K. et al. Am J Gastroenterol 2006; effect 50–70%). Habit reversal — learning alternative behavior. Stress management and CBT. In ID/ASD, functional behavior analysis and ABA. Medical: GERD with proton pump inhibitor initial intervention. Rome Foundation criteria (functional GI). NICE NG69 — general eating disorder principles.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with diaphragmatic breathing and habit reversal.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B84 Rumination-regurgitation disorder. 2024. Chitkara D.K., Van Tilburg M., Whitehead W.E., Talley N.J. Teaching diaphragmatic breathing for rumination syndrome. Am J Gastroenterol 2006;101(11):2449–2452. Rome IV Diagnostic Criteria for Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction. Rome Foundation; 2016.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID; ICD-11: 6B85 DSM-5-TR: 307.59) — significant weight loss, nutritional deficiency, enteral feeding dependence, or psychosocial impairment resulting from avoidance or restriction of food intake. No body image disturbance or fear of weight gain (key difference from anorexia).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 0.3–3.2% (~5–14% in eating disorder clinics). Gender: approximately equal (unlike anorexia). Onset: predominantly in childhood and adolescence. Comorbidity: autism spectrum, anxiety, OCD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Sensory sensitivity (food texture, smell, appearance) — especially on autism background. Fear of choking or vomiting trauma. Low interest in food (low appetite, low food interest).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Results: significant weight loss or insufficient weight gain (in children), nutritional deficiencies (iron, zinc, vitamins), dependence on enteral feeding, social disruption (school meals, eating with peers).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Significant weight loss or failure to achieve adequate weight gain; Significant nutritional deficiency; Enteral feeding or oral nutritional supplement dependence; Significant psychosocial impairment. NICE NG69 — specific intervention rules for ARFID. Clinical interview and dietary history. NIAS (Nine Item ARFID Screen) — screening.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Weight, height, BMI history. Laborator: tam qan, ferritin, sink, vitamin D, B12, folat. Autizm skrining (M-CHAT, ADOS).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Multidisciplinary approach: nutritionist + CBT therapist + feeding-occupational therapist. FBT-ARFID — adapted by Lock J. CBT-AR — Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for ARFID (Thomas J.J.) — manualized. Stepped food exposure — gradual introduction of new food types. Sensory integration elements (on autism background). Medical: nutritional correction; severe case enteral feeding.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With a multidisciplinary approach, improvement in most patients. Long-term follow-up in the context of autism and OCD.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6B85 ARFID. 2024. NICE NG69. 2017/2020. Thomas J.J., Eddy K.T. Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for ARFID. Cambridge University Press; 2018. Zickgraf H.F., Ellis J.M. Initial validation of the Nine Item Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder Screen (NIAS). Appetite 2018;123:32–42.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Enurez (XBT-11: 6C00 DSM-5-TR: 307.6) — involuntary urination persisting when the child's developmental age reaches at least 5 years. Frequency: at least twice a week for ≥ 3 months or significant functional impairment. Subtypes (ICCS — International Children's Continence Society 2014):

title: 2. History
text: In the 1830s, Pinel noted the family phenomenon. Mowrer O.H. (1938) — invented the pad-and-bell alarm. ICCS (1989) — international standardization. NICE CG111 (2010) — nocturnal enuresis clinical guideline.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: Age 5: 15–20%, Age 7: 5–10%, Age 10: 3–5%, Adults: 1–2%. Spontaneous remission occurs in approximately 15% per year. Comorbidity: ADHD ~30%, anxiety, conduct disorders, constipation.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Monosymptomatic nocturnal Immaturity of nocturnal vasopressin (ADH) secretion (nocturnal polyuria); Elevation of arousal threshold; Bladder capacity below age norm. Non-monosymptomatic form — bladder overactivity, constipation association. Secondary enuresis — stress, urinary infection (UTI), diabetes, psychiatric trigger.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Daytime symptoms (non-monosymptomatic): urgency, holding maneuvers (Vincent's curtsy, leg crossing), increased frequency, incontinence. Social-emotional: shame, decreased self-esteem, avoidance of social activities (camping, overnight companionship).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Qualifiers: only nocturnal / only diurnal / nocturnal and diurnal; primary / secondary; monosymptomatic / non-monosymptomatic. ICCS 2014 — subtype terminology standard. NICE CG111 — diagnosis and algorithm. AAP — pediatric assessment. Clinical interview — onset, frequency, daytime symptoms, family history. Urinalysis and culture — rule out UTI. Voiding diary 3 days — for daytime symptoms

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Sidik analizi, kultura. Voiding and constipation diary USG urinary (atipik klinik). Comorbid ADHD, anxiety screening.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Conservative recommendations — reducing fluid intake before bedtime, adequate water intake during the day, regular urination, resolving constipation. Positive reinforcement (star chart) — for dry nights. Alarm + desmopressin combination; Anticholinergic (oxybutynin) — in the context of non-monosymptomatic overactive bladder. ICCS 2020 — alarm and desmopressin first-line; "wait and see" until age 5

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Spontaneous remission occurs in approximately 15% per year; with intervention, remission occurs in most patients. Constipation management is important. Comorbid ADHD, anxiety — parallel intervention.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C00 Enuresis. 2024. NICE CG111. Nocturnal enuresis in children and young people under 19. 2010. Nevéus T. et al. Management and treatment of nocturnal enuresis — an updated standardization document from the International Children's Continence Society. J Pediatr Urol 2020;16(1):10–19. Caldwell P.H., Codarini M., Stewart F. et al. Alarm interventions for nocturnal enuresis in children.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Enkoprez (XBT-11: 6C01 DSM-5-TR: 307.7) — voluntary or involuntary defecation into inappropriate places (clothing, floor) persisting when the child's developmental age reaches at least 4 years. Frequency: at least once a month for ≥ 3 months.

title: 2. History
text: Rome IV — consensus within the functional GI framework. NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN functional constipation guideline (Tabbers M.M. et al. 2014).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 1–4% among ages 4–17; 3–6 times higher in boys. ~80% are primarily constipation-related (retentive). Comorbidity: enuresis, ADHD, anxiety, autism spectrum.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Trauma, fear, discipline in toilet training process. Diet (low fiber, dehydration). Behavioral and emotional factors — oppositional, anxiety In rare cases — Hirschsprung disease (rectal aganglionosis), spinal pathology.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Fecal soiling of clothing. Retentive — fecal mass on abdominal palpation; tense rectum; large (massive) defecation episodes. Social-emotional: shame, social isolation, school avoidance.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Qualifier: with/without constipation and overflow. NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN 2014 — functional constipation guideline (Rome IV). NICE CG99 (2010) — constipation in children and adolescents. Clinical interview — defecation frequency, pain, toilet training history. Abdominal palpation — fecal mass. Rectal examination — fecal impaction (or if clinically suspected final condition).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Abdominal and rectal examination. Bristol Stool, defecation diary. TSH, calcium, anti-TTG selectively. USG, manometriya atipik halda.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Regular toilet time (15–20 minutes after meals, gastrocolic reflex); Correct foot support (defecation position); Positive reinforcement (no punishment). NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN 2014 — PEG first-line; enema traumatic and second-line. Cochrane (Gordon M. et al. 2016) — PEG superior to lactulose.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: PEG and behavioral intervention lead to remission in most patients (50–80% within 1 year). Long-term retention is recommended; early discontinuation creates relapse risk.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C01 Encopresis. 2024. Tabbers M.M. et al. Evaluation and treatment of functional constipation in infants and children: evidence-based recommendations from ESPGHAN and NASPGHAN. J Pediatr Gastroenterol Nutr 2014;58(2):258–274. NICE CG99. Constipation in children and young people. 2010. Gordon M. et al. Osmotic and stimulant laxatives for the management of childhood constipation. Cochr

title: 2. History
text: Briquet P. (1859) — "hysteria" with multiple somatic symptoms.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: In primary care: 5–15%. Sex: 2–3 times higher in females. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, panic, factitious, real medical illnesses. High healthcare utilization and costs.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Biopsychosocial model — biological vulnerability + cognitive-affective emotional components + social reinforcement. Trauma and childhood illness history. Cognitive — catastrophizing and hypervigilance to bodily sensations.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Persistent somatic symptoms (pain, fatigue, gastrointestinal, cardiopulmonary). ≥ 1 of the following: Excessive and persistent thoughts about the severity of symptoms; High anxiety about health; Excessive time and energy devoted to symptoms. "Doctor shopping", repeated checking, avoidance behavior.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Excessive thoughts about the severity of symptoms; High anxiety about health; Excessive time/energy on symptoms. German S3 Guideline (Funktionelle Körperbeschwerden, 2018) — biopsychosocial approach. PHQ-15 (somatic symptom scale), SSS-8.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PHQ-15, SSS-8, Whiteley Index (overlap). One-time comprehensive medical examination; then minimal repetition.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Single physician model (single physician approach) — all complaints are managed through that physician. CBT — cognitive restructuring, reduction of catastrophic interpretation of bodily sensations, behavioral activation. Evidence: Kleinstäuber M. Cochrane 2014. SSRI — for comorbid MDD/anxiety; duloxetine, amitriptyline when pain is dominant. Structured limited checking — once comprehensive examina

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: CBT and a multidisciplinary approach lead to significant improvement; chronic course.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C20 Bodily distress disorder. 2024. Kleinstäuber M. et al. Pharmacological interventions for somatoform disorders in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2014;(11):CD010628. title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Alcohol Use Disorders (ICD-11: 6C40 DSM-5-TR: 303.x Alcohol Use Disorder, AUD) — a group of disorders characterized by impaired control over alcohol use, continued use despite physical or psychological harm. ICD-11 subtypes: harmful pattern of use (6C40.1), alcohol dependence (6C40.2), intoxication (6C40.3), withdrawal syndrome (6C40.4), alcohol-induced delirium, psychotic, mood, anxiety disorders

title: 2. History
text: Jellinek E.M. (1960) — 'The Disease Concept of Alcoholism' — alcoholism as a disease.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 14–29% (NIAAA NESARC); annual 5–14%. Sex: 2–3 times higher in males, but increasing in females. Mortality: 3 million deaths per year worldwide (WHO). Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, PTSD, BPD, other substance use, liver disease, cardiovascular.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Heritability 50–60% (Verhulst B. et al. Psychol Med 2015). Neurobiological — dopamine reward circuit, GABA, glutamate, opioid systems. Environment — peers, stress, alcohol availability, cultural norms. Genetic markers — ADH1B, ALDH2 (protective in Asian population).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Attempts to reduce are unsuccessful; Most time spent obtaining, using, recovering from substance Work/school disruption; Continues despite social-interpersonal problem. Refusal of important activities; Use in a dangerous situation;

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NIAAA AUDIT — 10-item screening. NICE CG115 — diagnosis and treatment of alcohol disorders. SAMHSA TIP — international protocols. AUDIT, AUDIT-C screening at primary care level. Clinical interview — TLFB (Timeline Followback) use pattern. Laborator: GGT, AST/ALT, MCV, CDT (carbohydrate-deficient transferrin), tam qan. Comorbidity (MDD, anxiety, PTSD, somatic illnesses).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: AUDIT, CIWA-Ar (withdrawal severity). GGT, MCV, CDT, transaminazlar. EKG, cardiac USG (cardiomyopathy). Komorbidlik skrining.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Mild (CIWA-Ar <10) — outpatient; Moderate-severe — bedridden; Benzodiazepine (lorazepam, diazepam) — gold standard for withdrawal treatment; Thiamine 100–300 mg IV/IM × 3–5 days (Wernicke prophylaxis — BEFORE glucose). Hydration, electrolyte correction. Topiramate, gabapentin — off-label. SAMHSA — co-occurring substance use disorder and psychiatric comorbidity (dual diagnosis).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With multimodal approach, 40–50% long-term remission. Comorbid MDD, BPD, other substance — poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C40 Disorders due to use of alcohol. 2024. NICE CG115. Alcohol-use disorders: diagnosis, assessment and management. 2011. APA. Practice Guideline for the Pharmacological Treatment of Patients with AUD. 2018. SAMHSA. TIP 49: Incorporating Alcohol Pharmacotherapies Into Medical Practice. 2009. Anton R.F. et al. COMBINE trial. JAMA 2006;295(17):2003–2017.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Opioid Use Disorders (ICD-11: 6C41 DSM-5-TR: 304.x Opioid Use Disorder, OUD) — impaired control over opioid use (heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, etc.) or harmful pattern of use. High mortality (overdose) related to medical-criminal context.

title: 2. History
text: Dole V.P., Nyswander M. (1965) — initial studies of methadone maintenance therapy. 2010+ US opioid epidemic — starting with prescription opioids, leading to fentanyl overdose crisis. WHO, SAMHSA, NICE — opioid agonist maintenance therapy (OAT) is the gold standard.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Global: 40 mln+ patients (UNODC); US annual 80,000+ overdose deaths. Sex: 2 times higher in males. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, PTSD, BPD, HIV/HCV, cardiac. Mortality: in treated OUD patients, 10–20 times higher compared to the population.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — effect of the μ-opioid receptor system on the dopaminergic reward circuit. Environment — prescription opioid availability, socioeconomic factors. Pain syndromes — transition from prescription initiation to dependence.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Dependence syndrome (same criteria as AUD). Intoxication — miosis, sedation, respiratory depression, hypotension, coma. Overdose — respiratory depression is main cause of death; naloxone antidote. Withdrawal — onset 8–24 hours (short-acting), 36–72 hours (long-acting methadone); rhinorrhea, lacrimation, mydriasis, anxiety, tachycardia, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, gas-pain.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: SAMHSA TIP 63 — Medications for Opioid Use Disorder. NICE CG52 — opioid dependence. Toksikoloji skrining (sidik). HIV, HCV, syphilis, pregnancy test. Comorbidity and overdose history.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Toksikoloji skrining. HIV, HCV, HBV, sifilis. EKG (before starting methadone — QTc).

title: 8. Treatment
text: OAT significantly reduces mortality and HIV transmission (MacArthur G.J. et al. BMJ 2012 meta-analysis). OAT is superior to psychosocial intervention in mortality reduction; however, combination with psychosocial intervention (MI, CBT, contingency management, peer support, 12-step — see "Treatment methodologies") is optimal. Methadone or buprenorphine — OAT is continued during pregnancy; detox is

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Long-term stabilization and mortality reduction with OAT. Comorbidity intervention important.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C41 Disorders due to use of opioids. 2024. SAMHSA. TIP 63: Medications for Opioid Use Disorder. 2021. MacArthur G.J. et al. Opioid substitution treatment and HIV transmission in people who inject drugs. BMJ 2012;345:e5945. Walley A.Y. et al. Opioid overdose rates and implementation of overdose education and nasal naloxone distribution. BMJ 2013;346:f174.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Cannabis Use Disorders (ICD-11: 6C42 DSM-5-TR: 304.30) — impaired control over use of THC-containing cannabis or synthetic cannabinoids and/or harmful use.

title: 2. History
text: 2010+ — high-potency cannabis and synthetic cannabinoids ("Spice", "K2") epidemic.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 7–9% cannabis users develop addiction; risk 17% in those who start in adolescence. Sex: higher in males. Comorbidity: psychosis risk, anxiety, MDD, ADHD, other substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Endokannabinoid sistemi. Social and cultural factors; accessibility.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Dependence syndrome (same criteria as AUD). Intoxication — flushed face, tachycardia, increased appetite ("munchies"), time perception impairment, paranoid feelings. Severe intoxication — cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome (vomiting syndrome with chronic use), panic, psychosis. Withdrawal (DSM-5 new): irritability, anxiety, sleep disturbance, decreased appetite, mood lowering, somatic complaints; on

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NIDA — principles of cannabis use disorder. WHO mhGAP — assessment at first aid level. CUDIT-R (Cannabis Use Disorders Identification Test-Revised). Toksikoloji skrining (sidik THC). Comorbid psychosis, anxiety, MDD.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Comorbidity (psychosis, anxiety, MDD).

title: 8. Treatment
text: First-line psychosocial intervention — MI, CBT, contingency management. Evidence: Davis M.L. et al. Drug Alcohol Depend 2015 meta-analysis. No specific FDA-approved pharmacotherapy exists. Off-label: N-acetylcysteine (in adolescents Gray K.M. JAMA 2017 small effect RCT), gabapentin. Comorbid psychosis — antipsychotic; cannabis abstinence critical. Synthetic cannabinoid intoxication — supportive, a

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with psychosocial intervention. Early onset of use — poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C42 Disorders due to use of cannabis. 2024. NIDA. Research Report on Cannabis. 2020. Davis M.L. et al. Behavioral therapies for treatment-seeking cannabis users: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. J Addict Dis 2015;34(4):298–312. Gray K.M. et al. A randomized placebo-controlled trial of N-acetylcysteine for cannabis use disorder in adults. JAMA 2017.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Sedative/hypnotic use disorders (ICD-11: 6C43 DSM-5-TR: 304.x Sedative, Hypnotic, or Anxiolytic Use Disorder) — impaired control over use of benzodiazepines, z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone), barbiturates, and other anxiolytics. Often begins with medical prescription in clinical practice; 'iatrogenic dependence' is common.

title: 2. History
text: 1960s — clinical introduction of benzodiazepines (chlordiazepoxide 1960, diazepam 1963). 1980s — recognition of the dependence potential of long-term use. NICE and APA — short-term use recommendation (2–4 weeks).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Long-term benzodiazepine use in the elderly: 10–20%; problem identification is low. Sex: higher in females prescription. Comorbidity: alcohol and opioid use (dangerous combination — overdose risk).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Iatrogenesis — long-term prescriptions. Adaptation to the GABA-A receptor system — tolerance and discontinuation. Self-medication pattern in patients with anxiety, sleep disturbance.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Dependence syndrome — loss of control, tolerance, withdrawal. Intoxication — sedation, ataxia, dysarthria, disinhibition, anterograde amnesia. Overdose — life-threatening respiratory depression with opioids.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE — benzodiazepine prescription 2–4 weeks; long-term use should be reassessed. Beers Criteria — avoid benzodiazepines in the elderly. Clinical interview + prescription history. Toksikoloji skrining. Comorbid alcohol and opioid use (especially overdose risk). Clinical examination — withdrawal signs.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Toksikoloji skrining. Vital signs (discontinuation)

title: 8. Treatment
text: Bedwetting — severe disruption or comorbid substance use. Psychosocial intervention — CBT for anxiety or sleep disorder; this reduces need for pharmacotherapy. Comorbid disorders treatment (anxiety, depression, sleep). Prescription management — at the primary healthcare level. NICE, BNF — taper protocols. Ashton Manual — practical clinical guide by Ashton C.H. Heather (1999).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Gradual taper and significant improvement with CBT. In older adults, discontinuation is both medically and psychologically difficult.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C43 Disorders due to sedative, hypnotic, anxiolytic. 2024. NICE Quick Reference Guide. Benzodiazepines. Ashton C.H. Benzodiazepines: How They Work and How to Withdraw (Ashton Manual). 2002. FDA Drug Safety Communication. Opioid pain or cough medicines combined with benzodiazepines: serious side effects. 2016.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Cocaine use disorders (ICD-11: 6C44 DSM-5-TR: 304.20) — impaired control over cocaine use or harmful use. Includes intoxication, withdrawal, harmful pattern of use, dependence, induced psychotic and mood disorders.

title: 2. History
text: Coca leaves have been used in the Andes for thousands of years. Niemann A. (1860) — cocaine isolation. Freud (1884) — "Über Coca" — later criticized the clinical effect. 1970s–1980s — 'crack' epidemic in the USA.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Annual global users 21 mln (UNODC). Sex: 2 times higher in males. Komorbidlik: alkohol, opioid, MDD, BPD, psixoz, kardiovaskulyar mortalite.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — blockade of dopamine reuptake → increased mesolimbic reward; tolerance and down-regulation. Social-cultural — peer, accessibility, socio-economic.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Dependence syndrome (DSM-5-TR criteria). Intoxication - euphoria, hypervigilance, mydriasis, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia; in severe cases, paranoid delirium, hallucinations, agitation, myocardial infarction, stroke, seizures. Withdrawal — "crash" — depressive mood, anhedonia, fatigue, hypersomnia, increased appetite, intense cravings; high suicide risk.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: SAMHSA TIP 33 — stimulant use. Clinical interview + toxicology. EKG, troponin (especially if chest pain). Comorbidity and suicide risk (high during discontinuation).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: EKG, troponin, cardiac assessment.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Psychosocial — first line: Contingency management — strongest evidence base (Petry N.M. reviews). Community Reinforcement Approach (CRA). Pharmacotherapy — No specific FDA-approved medication exists. . Off-label studies — disulfiram, modafinil, topiramate — mixed results. Comorbidity treatment. Acute intoxication — benzodiazepine (lorazepam); cardiac monitoring; antipsychotic in psychosis.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with contingency management. Comorbid substance use and psychiatric disorder poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
