title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Stimulant use disorders (ICD-11: 6C45 DSM-5-TR: 304.4 Stimulant Use Disorder) — disorders developing from use of amphetamine, methamphetamine, MDMA, mephedrone, or other synthetic cathinones. Cocaine is coded separately (6C44).

title: 2. History
text: Amphetamine synthesis by Edeleano L. (1887); widespread use in World War II. 1950s–1960s — pharmacy-prescription epidemic. Methamphetamine epidemic since the 1990s. "Krokodil" (desomorphine), "bath salts" (synthetic cathinones) — epidemics of 2010+.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Global users: amphetamine 30 mln+ (UNODC). Sex: higher in males. Comorbidity: psychosis, MDD, anxiety, cardiovascular, skin lesions (self-excoriation, 'meth mouth').

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — blockade of dopamine and norepinephrine release and reuptake; severe neurotoxicity (especially methamphetamine — to dopamine neurons).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Intoxication — euphoria, hypervigilance, mydriasis, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia; in severe cases: paranoid psychosis, agitation, myocardial infarction, stroke, seizures, rhabdomyolysis. Withdrawal — "crash" — depressive mood, anhedonia, fatigue, hypersomnia, appetite, intense cravings; high suicide risk. Chronic use — psychosis (paranoid delusions, hallucinations), cognitive deficit, "

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: SAMHSA TIP 33 — stimulant use. NIDA — methamphetamine research reports. Komorbidlik (psixoz, MDD, kardiovaskulyar).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: EKG, troponin, CPK (rabdomioliz). Dental and skin examination.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Psychosocial — first line: Contingency management; Matrix Model — manualized comprehensive program for amphetamine dependence. Pharmacotherapy — No specific FDA-approved medication exists. ; off-label: mirtazapine, naltrexone + bupropion (Trivedi M.H. NEJM 2021 — ADAPT-2 trial combination for methamphetamine — modest effect). Acute intoxication — benzodiazepine for agitation; antipsychotic in psyc

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With multimodal approach, remission 30–50%. Komorbid psixoz pis proqnoz.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C45 Disorders due to use of stimulants. 2024. SAMHSA. TIP 33. 2021. NIDA. Methamphetamine Research Report. 2019. Trivedi M.H. et al. Bupropion and Naltrexone in Methamphetamine Use Disorder. NEJM 2021;384(2):140–153. Rawson R.A. et al. Matrix Model. SAMHSA.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Hallucinogen use disorders (ICD-11: 6C46 DSM-5-TR: 304.5x Hallucinogen Use Disorder) — disorders related to use of LSD, psilocybin, mescaline, DMT, ayahuasca, ketamine (dissociative hallucinogen), and similar substances.

title: 2. History
text: Hofmann A. (1943) — accidental discovery of LSD's hallucinogenic effect. 1950s–1960s — clinical research (depression, schizophrenia). 1971 — Controlled Substances Act in the USA, Schedule I. 2020+ — psilocybin and ketamine "psychedelic therapy" research renaissance.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Dependence is relatively rare (unlike other substances); typical use is episodic. Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder (HPPD) — flashback-like experiences.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Effect on serotonergic (5-HT2A) or NMDA (ketamine, PCP) receptors. Environment, peers, experimentation.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Intoxication — visual hallucinations, synesthesia, time distortion, depersonalization, derealization, "bad trip" (panic, paranoia). In severe cases — psychosis, fatal accidents. "Persisting Perception Disorder" (HPPD) — persistent visual phenomena (flashbacks) after use. Ketamine — dissociation, "K-hole"; chronic use — urinary bladder syndrome. PCP — aggressive behavior, analgesia, disorganized be

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Toxicology (LSD not in standard panels). Comorbid psychosis, anxiety, MDD.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Acute intoxication ("bad trip") — calm environment, benzodiazepine (lorazepam); antipsychotic (haloperidol) only in severe psychotic state; caution. Dependence rare; psychosocial intervention (MI, CBT). HPPD — antipsychotics usually ineffective; clonazepam helps some patients; long-term monitoring. NIDA — psychedelic research renaissance; clinical recommendation still pending.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Most users do not develop dependence. Chronic course in patients with severe psychotic or HPPD complications.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C46 Disorders due to use of hallucinogens. 2024. NIDA. Hallucinogens DrugFacts. 2019. Halpern J.H., Lerner A.G., Passie T. A review of Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder (HPPD). Curr Top Behav Neurosci 2018;36:333–360. Carhart-Harris R.L. et al. Trial of psilocybin versus escitalopram for depression. NEJM 2021;384(15):1402–1411.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Nicotine use disorders (ICD-11: 6C47 DSM-5-TR: 305.1 Tobacco Use Disorder) — impaired control over use of cigarettes, tobacco products, e-cigarettes, or other nicotine substances. Leading preventable cause of mortality worldwide.

title: 2. History
text: 1964 — Surgeon General Report (USA) — recognition of tobacco as a cause of disease. 2003 — WHO FCTC (Framework Convention on Tobacco Control). 2010+ — e-cigarette epidemic, "EVALI" (e-cigarette/vaping product use-associated lung injury).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Global: 1.3 bln users; annual 8 mln+ deaths (WHO). Sex: higher in males (but decreasing); increase observed in females in some regions. Comorbidity: MDD, schizophrenia (~60–80% of patients smoke), anxiety, other substance use. Tobacco — cancer, COPD, cardiovascular, stroke, perinatal complications.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — nicotine α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors; increased dopamine reward. Social-cultural, peer, marketing.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Withdrawal — irritability, anxiety, concentration difficulty, appetite, sleep disturbance, depressive mood; onset within 24 hours, peak 2–3 days, duration 2–4 weeks. E-cigarette — nicotine dependence + EVALI risk.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: USPSTF — screening all adult patients for tobacco use; 5A approach (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange) for smokers. NICE NG209 (Tobacco: preventing uptake, promoting quitting and treating dependence, 2021). Clinical interview (5A). Fagerström Test for Nicotine Dependence (FTND). CO ekspirator monitorinq (motivasiya). Comorbid cardiac and respiratory assessment.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Spirometriya (KOAH skrining).

title: 8. Treatment
text: 5A modeli (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange). Behavioral intervention — CBT, MI, telephone 'quitline' service; intensity increases effect. Combination — pharmacotherapy + behavioral therapy is superior. E-cigarettes as a smoking cessation aid are controversial — effective in some studies (Hajek P. NEJM 2019), but long-term safety and gateway risk are being investigated; NRT is preferred.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With multimodal intervention, 6-month abstinence is 25–35%. Withdrawal reduces mortality and illness risk significantly.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C47 Disorders due to use of nicotine. 2024. USPSTF. Interventions for tobacco smoking cessation in adults. JAMA 2021;325(3):265–279. NICE NG209. Tobacco. 2021. Cahill K., Stevens S., Perera R., Lancaster T. Pharmacological interventions for smoking cessation: an overview and network meta-analysis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2013;(5):CD009329.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Behavioural addictions (ICD-11: 6C50 Gambling Disorder, 6C51 Gaming Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 312.31 Gambling Disorder) — a persistent or recurrent pattern of gambling or video-gaming behaviour with impaired control; it causes significant functional impairment.

title: 3. Epidemiology
text: Gambling Disorder lifetime prevalence: 0.2–5.3% (wide range). Gaming Disorder: 1–10% (Europe) among young people. Sex: gambling is more prevalent in men; the sex difference narrows in online gambling; gaming predominates in men. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, other substance use, ADHD, BPD, suicide risk.

title: 4. Aetiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — dysregulation of the reward circuit (dopamine), similar to substance use disorders. Personality — impulsivity, sensation-seeking.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Persistent/recurrent behaviour (gambling, gaming). Occupational, family, social, and academic impairment. "Chasing losses" (in gambling) — attempts to win back losses. Lying, financial disruption (gambling), online preoccupation (gaming). Comorbid MDD and high suicide risk.

title: 6. Diagnosis
text: Gambling with increasing amounts — tolerance; Irritability or restlessness when cutting down gambling — withdrawal; Unsuccessful attempts to cut down; Preoccupation with gambling; Gambling when distressed; "Chasing" after losses; Loss of relationships/job;

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: SOGS, PGSI, IGDS9-SF.

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT — first line; trigger management, behavioural activation, cognitive restructuring ("near miss" and "gambler's fallacy"). Cowlishaw S. Cochrane 2012 meta-analysis. Naltrexone, nalmefene — the evidence base is evolving (reviews by Grant J.E.). SSRIs — for comorbid MDD/anxiety. Self-help (Gamblers Anonymous, Gam-Anon) — adjunct. Financial counselling — critical in gambling.

title: 9. Prognosis
text: Remission with CBT 30–50%. Comorbid MDD predicts a poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C50 Gambling disorder. 2024. WHO. ICD-11. 6C51 Gaming disorder. 2024. Cowlishaw S. et al. Psychological therapies for pathological and problem gambling. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2012;(11):CD008937. Grant J.E. et al. Pharmacotherapy for gambling disorder. Curr Top Behav Neurosci 2016;26:135–149.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Pyromaniya (XBT-11: 6C70 ; DSM-5-TR: 312.33) — deliberate, purposeful fire-setting behavior; tension or emotional arousal before the act, relief or pleasure after the act. Without financial gain, revenge, or ideological motive; a rare impulse control disorder.

title: 2. History
text: In the 1830s, Marc — 'monomanie incendiaire'.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Rare — only ~3–10% of arson incidents meet criteria for pyromania (most arson has financial, revenge, or other motivation). Sex: higher in males. Comorbidity: conduct disorder, ASPD, ID, ADHD, other substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Multiple factors — neurobiological impulse control, cognitive deficit, family dysfunction. Komorbid pozuntular tipik.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Repeated deliberate fire-setting. Pre-act tension/emotional arousal. Admiration for the fire, its attributes (flame, tools). Post-act pleasure, relief. No financial gain, revenge, ideological goal, or psychotic compulsion (delusion).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Rare in clinical practice; most firesetting is motivation-based. Clinical interview + family/witness information. Motivational history — compatibility with pyromania criteria. Comorbidity (ASPD, conduct disorder, manic, psychosis, ID, ADHD). Legal context — forensic psychiatry consultation.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Psychiatric interview. Intellectual assessment. Impulsivity scales (Barratt Impulsiveness Scale). Structured risk assessment in forensic context (HCR-20, VRAG).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Evidence base is limited (rare disorder). CBT — impulse control, trigger management, exposure to alternative behavior. Treatment of comorbid disorders (therapeutic limit in ASPD, behavioral intervention in ID, stimulant in ADHD). SSRI — off-label for impulse control. Legal-psychiatric coordination (hospitalization, court order).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Evidence base is limited; comorbid disorder and psychosocial support determine prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C70 Pyromania. 2024. Burton P.R., McNiel D.E., Binder R.L. Firesetting, arson, pyromania, and the forensic mental health expert. J Am Acad Psychiatry Law 2012;40(3):355–365.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Kleptomaniya (XBT-11: 6C71 ; DSM-5-TR: 312.32) — a recurrent impulse to steal objects that are not needed for personal use or monetary value; tension before the act, relief or pleasure after the act.

title: 2. History
text: Esquirol (1838) — "kleptomanie".

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Only ~5% of shoplifting incidents meet criteria for kleptomania (most theft is motivation-based). Sex: higher in females (in clinical populations). Comorbidity: MDD ~80%, anxiety, OCD, eating disorders, substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Hypothesis of serotonin and opioid system dysregulation (Grant J.E.). Comorbid affective and impulsivity disorders.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Failure to resist stealing act — items unnecessary for personal or financial value. Pre-act increase in tension. Post-act relief, pleasure. Stealing is not motivated by anger, revenge, or psychotic motivation. Objects are typically discarded later, returned, hidden; accidentally given back.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: K-SAS (Kleptomania Symptom Assessment Scale) — Grant J.E. Comorbid MDD, anxiety, OCD, eating disorders.

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT — cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, exposure and ritual prevention. SSRI — for comorbid MDD. Multidisciplinary approach — legal context. Grant J.E. — naltrexone evidence base.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with naltrexone and CBT. Komorbid MDD pis proqnoz.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C71 Kleptomania. 2024. Grant J.E. et al. A double-blind, placebo-controlled study of the opiate antagonist naltrexone in the treatment of kleptomania. Biol Psychiatry 2009;65(7):600–606.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Compulsive Sexual Behavior Disorder (ICD-11: 6C72 Compulsive Sexual Behaviour Disorder) — a persistent pattern of failure to control intense, repetitive sexual impulses and behaviors, causing significant distress or functional impairment. Included in the impulse control disorders category in ICD-11.

title: 2. History
text: Krafft-Ebing (1886) — "hyperaesthesia sexualis". 1980s–1990s — concept of 'sexual addiction' (Carnes P.) became popular, but remained controversial within the scientific community.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 1–6% (broad range, depending on assessment tools). Sex: higher in males (5:1 in clinical populations). Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, BPD, substance use, OCD, ADHD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Multiple factors—impulsivity, compulsivity, cognitive-emotional dysregulation, trauma. Neurobiological similarities with substance use disorders and OCD. Social-cultural — accessibility of internet pornography.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Excessive time spent on sexual fantasies, urges, or behaviors. Failure of attempts to reduce behavior despite negative consequences. Use of sexual behavior to reduce distress or achieve emotional acceptance. Significant functional impairment due to impaired control of sexual behavior (relationships, work, health, legal). Typical forms — compulsive masturbation, multiple sexual partners, patronizin

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Repeated sexual activity leads to impairment in other areas of the individual's life (health, relationships, responsibility); The patient has made repeated and significant efforts to regain control — unsuccessfully. The behavior continues despite negative consequences; Behavior persists even when it does not produce significant pleasure Diagnosis should not be based solely on spiritual-religious d

title: 8. Treatment
text: Naltrexone off-label — opioid antagonist; modest effect in some studies. SSRI — in comorbid MDD/anxiety. Group support (S-Anon, SAA) — adjunct. Relationship/couple therapy — in comorbid context. There is no specific FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. WHO — ICD-11 official category. APA — DSM-5-TR does not have a separate diagnosis; controversial area.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with CBT and ACT. Comorbidity treatment important.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C72 Compulsive sexual behaviour disorder. 2024. Kraus S.W. et al. Compulsive sexual behaviour disorder in the ICD-11. World Psychiatry 2018;17(1):109–110. Grubbs J.B., Hoagland K.C., Lee B.N. et al. Sexual addiction 25 years on. Clin Psychol Rev 2020;82:101925.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Intermittent Explosive Disorder (IED; ICD-11: 6C73 Intermittent Explosive Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 312.34) — recurrent, uncontrolled verbal or physical aggressive outbursts; disproportionately intense relative to triggering provocation. Remorse or shame after the act.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: 4–7% (Kessler R.C. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2006). Sex: higher in males. Onset: mid-adolescence. Comorbidity: MDD ~70%, anxiety, substance use, BPD, ASPD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Serotonin dysregulation — SSRI effect. Trauma and family history. Disruption of cortex-amygdala regulation.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Recurrent aggression outbursts — verbal (screaming, threat) or physical (object damage, harm to others). Explosion out of control — patient unsuccessful in restraining. Trigger minimal or minor. Post-act remorse, shame, guilt. Relationship, work, legal consequences.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Verbal or physical aggression outbursts, without destructive harm, ≥2 times per week, ≥3 months Three or more disruptive/damaging outbursts within a 12-month period. Clinical interview + family information. Scale — OAS (Overt Aggression Scale), BPAQ (Buss-Perry Aggression Questionnaire). Comorbidity and exclusion of medical causes (frontal lobe injury, epilepsy, hypoglycemia).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Medical and neurological.

title: 8. Treatment
text: CBT — anger management, cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, relaxation. McCloskey M.S. et al. J Consult Clin Psychol 2008. Mood stabilizers — valproate, lithium (off-label in refractory cases). Comorbid disorder treatment. Legal coordination when needed. Coccaro JAMA Psychiatry 2009 — fluoxetine evidence-based. McCloskey 2008 — CBT efficacy.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with fluoxetine and CBT. Comorbid ASPD, substance use poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C73 Intermittent explosive disorder. 2024. Coccaro E.F. et al. A double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial of fluoxetine in patients with intermittent explosive disorder. J Clin Psychiatry 2009;70(5):653–662. McCloskey M.S. et al. Cognitive-behavioral therapy for intermittent explosive disorder. J Consult Clin Psychol 2008;76(5):876–886.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Conduct Disorder (ICD-11: 6C90 Conduct-Dissocial Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 312.81 Conduct Disorder) — a persistent and repetitive pattern of violating the rights of others or age-appropriate social norms. Typically begins in childhood/adolescence; may transition to ASPD in adulthood.

title: 2. History
text: NICE NG158 (2017) — child and adolescent antisocial behavior.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: boys 6–16%, girls 2–9%. Sex: 3 times higher in males. Onset — childhood (before age 10, poor prognosis) or adolescence (age 10+, good prognosis). Comorbidity: ADHD 50%, ODD, MDD, anxiety, substance use, learning disorders.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — amygdala, prefrontal dysregulation, low sympathetic arousal. Environment — childhood trauma, harsh parenting, family dysfunction, peers. Prenatal — tobacco, alcohol.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Lack of empathy or remorse; Lack of concern about performance; Callous lack of emotion;

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Onset: childhood type (≥1 symptom by age 10), adolescent type. NICE NG158 — diagnosis and intervention in children and adolescents. Clinical interview + parent, teacher. Structured interview (K-SADS). CBCL (Child Behavior Checklist), TRF (Teacher Report Form).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Komorbidlik skrining. Intellectual and academic assessment.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Incredible Years (Webster-Stratton); PCIT (Parent-Child Interaction Therapy); MST — Multisystemic Therapy (Henggeler S.W.) — encompassing family, peer, school, community; FFT — Functional Family Therapy; MTFC — Multidimensional Treatment Foster Care. Comorbid ADHD — stimulant (also reduces aggression). Antipsychotic (risperidone) — off-label in severe aggression, with caution; no FDA approval (spe

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With childhood-type and psychopathic traits — risk of ASPD in adulthood ~40–50%. Adolescent-type good prognosis. Early parent training and multisystem intervention significantly improve outcomes.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C90 Conduct-dissocial disorder. 2024. NICE NG158. Antisocial behaviour and conduct disorders in children and young people: recognition and management. 2017. Steiner H., Remsing L. AACAP Practice Parameter for the Assessment and Treatment of Conduct Disorder. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2007;46(1):126–141. Petrosino A. et al. title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: ODD (XBT-11: 6C91 Oppositional Defiant Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 313.81) — pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behavior, or vindictive behavior. Unlike Conduct Disorder (CD), there is no interference with the fundamental rights of others.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 3–11% in preschool or school-aged children. Sex: higher in prepubertal boys; postpubertal difference decreases. Comorbidity: ADHD ~40–60%, MDD, anxiety, transition to CD in 30% (severe childhood type).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Environment — harsh parenting, family dysfunction. Neurobiological — affective regulation, frontal-amygdala circuitry. Temperamental traits (irritability).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Age qualifier: symptoms on most days for 6 months until age 5; age ≥5 — ≥1 per week for 6 months. NICE NG158 — guidance for child antisocial behavior. Clinical interview + parent, teacher. CBCL, TRF, ECBI (Eyberg Child Behavior Inventory). Komorbid ADHD skrining. Difference between Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) and depressive disorder.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Comorbid ADHD, anxiety, MDD.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Incredible Years — ages 3–8; Parent Management Training (Barkley) — age 6+; Comorbid ADHD — stimulant. SSRI — comorbid anxiety/depression. School-based intervention. Intimate partner violence suspicion - social service coordination.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Most patients improve with early parent training. Comorbid ADHD, severe variant — risk of CD/ASPD.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6C91 Oppositional defiant disorder. 2024. AACAP Practice Parameter (Steiner 2007). Eyberg S.M. et al. Evidence-based psychosocial treatments for children and adolescents with disruptive behavior. J Clin Child Adolesc Psychol 2008;37(1):215–237.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Personality Disorder (ICD-11: 6D10 Personality Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 301.x 10 categories) — persistent and pervasive pattern in personal and social functioning; thoughts, feelings, and behaviors regarding self and others. ICD-11 fundamentally single category + severity degrees + characteristic domains has been modeled; classical 10 distinct personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, borderline, anti

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: ~9% (Lenzenweger M.F. et al. Biol Psychiatry 2007); BPD ~1.4%, ASPD ~1%, schizoid 3%. Sex: BPD higher in females in clinical populations; ASPD higher in males. Onset: adolescence and early adulthood. Mortality: in BPD, suicide 8–10% lifetime; in ASPD, violent death. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, substance use, eating disorders.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Early trauma, childhood abuse (especially BPD). Disorganized attachment. Neurobiological — frontal-limbic regulation, serotonergic dysregulation.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: "Borderline model" — a separate qualifier in ICD-11 (corresponding to BPD, instead of 10 categorical separate diagnoses). Cluster A (odd/eccentric): Paranoid, Schizoid, Schizotypal. Cluster B (dramatik/erratik): Antisocial (ASPD), Borderline (BPD), Histrionic, Narcissistic. Cluster C (narahat/qorxulu): Avoidant, Dependent, Obsessive-Compulsive (OCPD).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview + long history. SCID-5-PD (DSM categorical) or ICD-11 PD assessment. Personality scales — PID-5 (DSM-5 dimensional), MMPI-2-RF. Comorbidity (MDD, substance, trauma). Suicide and violence risk.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Comorbidity and suicide.

title: 8. Treatment
text: For ASPD — therapeutic limitation; CBT in structured, motivated patient. Pharmacotherapy — no specific approval for personality disorder itself; for comorbid symptoms (impulsivity, depression, anxiety, psychosis-like) — SSRIs, mood stabilizers, low-dose atypical antipsychotics. Crisis management — DBT skills, short-term inpatient stay. Long-term treatment (years).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: BPD — 50%+ remission at 10-year follow-up (Zanarini M.C. reviews). ASPD — worst prognosis; but aggression decreases with age. Treatment prognosis significantly improves.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Personality Difficulty (XBT-11): 6D11 Personality Difficulty — ICD-11 subclinical form of personality disorder (6D10). Personality traits cause functional difficulties but do not fully meet Personality Disorder criteria. It is a diagnostic code but not evaluated as a full disorder.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Precise epidemiological data are limited; subclinical personality traits are widespread in the population.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Same as 6D10 — genetic and environmental interaction.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Non-trivial expression of 5 trait domains (negative affectivity, detachment, dissociality, disinhibition, anankastia). Functional difficulties (relationships, work), but no significant functional impairment. Problems increase in stress context.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Exclusion of full PD diagnosis.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Personality inventories (PID-5).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Psychotherapy — short-to-medium term; problem-focused CBT or supportive. Stress management and coping skills. Specific intervention for comorbid symptoms.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Improvement with treatment; risk of progression to stable or full PD.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D11 Personality difficulty. 2024. Tyrer P. et al. Lancet 2015;385(9969):717–726.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Ekshibisionizm (XBT-11: 6D30 Exhibitionistic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 302.4) — persistent or intense sexual arousal from exposing genitals to non-consenting persons; the patient experiences significant distress or functional impairment in response to these fantasies, urges, or behaviors, and/or acts on them with actual non-consenting individuals.

title: 2. History
text: Lasègue C. (1877) — term 'exhibitionnistes'. WFSBP 2020 (Thibaut F. et al.) — international recommendation for paraphilic disorders

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: males 2–4%; rare in females. Onset: adolescence and early adulthood. Comorbidity: other paraphilia (50–80% of paraphilic disorder patients), ASPD, substance use, MDD.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Numerous factors — biological, behavioral conditioning, psychosocial. Comorbid impulsivity and hypersexuality.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Repeated and intense sexual arousal for exposing genitals to nonconsenting persons. The patient acts on fantasy, impulse, or behavior or experiences distress. Typical context — street, public transportation. Legal and social consequences (sexual crime).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: WFSBP 2020 — step-by-step therapy for paraphilic disorders. Comorbidity (ASPD, substance, other paraphilic). Risk assessment (forensic context).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Risk scales (Static-99R for sexual recidivism).

title: 8. Treatment
text: In refractory cases / severe cases — antiandrogen (CPA cyproterone, MPA medroxyprogesterone). In most severe case — GnRH agonist (leuprolide, triptorelin) — chemical castration; continuous monitoring. Legal-medical coordination. WFSBP 2020 — step-by-step algorithm.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Reduction of relapse with evidence-based treatment; comorbid ASPD and substance abuse poor prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D30 Exhibitionistic disorder. 2024. Thibaut F. et al. WFSBP guidelines for the biological treatment of paraphilic disorders. World J Biol Psychiatry 2020.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Voyerizm (XBT-11: 6D31 Voyeuristic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 302.82) — persistent and intense sexual arousal from secretly observing undressing, nakedness, sexual activity of non‑consenting persons; patient experiences distress from these fantasies or acts upon them with non‑consenting individuals.

title: 2. History
text: 6D30 develops in parallel with exhibitionism.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: males 5–10% subclinical fantasies; clinical voyeuristic disorder ~1–3%. Comorbid other paraphilic disorders. Internet voyeurism (hidden camera, "upskirt") is developing.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: General etiology for paraphilic disorders.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Observing unsuspecting individuals naked or during sexual activity without their consent. Typical context — window, dressing rooms, public places. "Upskirting" — in synthetic form.

title: 8. Treatment
text: WFSBP 2020 step‑by‑step algorithm (same as 6D30): CBT for relapse prevention; GnRH in the most severe case. Legal-medical coordination.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Reduction of relapse with evidence-based treatment.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D31 Voyeuristic disorder. 2024. Thibaut F. et al. WFSBP. World J Biol Psychiatry 2020.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Pedofilik Pozuntu (XBT-11: 6D32 Pedophilic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 302.2) — persistent and intense sexual arousal toward prepubescent children (typically <13 years) for ≥6 months; patient has acted on fantasies involving children or experiences significant distress/functional impairment. Patient is at least 16 years and victim at least 5 years younger.

title: 2. History
text: WFSBP 2020 — international paraphilic indication.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence — precise data is difficult (stigmatic category); in men 1–5%; clinical impairment ~1%. Sex: almost always in males. Comorbidity: other paraphilic, ASPD, MDD, anxiety, substance use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Biological — neurodevelopmental anomalies (Cantor J.M. reviews — IQ, neuroanatomical differences). History of childhood trauma or sexual abuse (but most individuals with pedophilic disorder were not victims themselves; the "abused-abuser" concept is semi-scholarly). Comorbid impulsivity and hypersexuality.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Persistent and intense sexual arousal toward prepubertal children. The patient has acted on fantasy (sexual abuse of a child) or experiences distress/functional impairment. Subtypes (DSM-5-TR): exclusive type (attracted only to children), nonexclusive type (attracted to both children and adults); male/female/both. Legal consequences (crime).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Structured risk assessment in forensic psychiatry context. Clinical interview (difficult to confess; patient may conceal). Penile plethysmography — investigation and forensic context. Forensic risk assessment (Static-99R, STABLE-2007).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Forensic clinical interview. Static-99R, STABLE-2007. Penile plethysmography (investigation).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Forensic intervention and community monitoring parallel. Multidisciplinary approach — psychiatrist, therapist, social service, legal. WFSBP 2020 — Thibaut et al. — step‑by‑step pharmacotherapy STABLE-2007 – dynamic risk factors.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Behavior management improves with evidence-based treatment. Comorbid ASPD and substance use — poor prognosis. Continuous monitoring critical.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Sadistik Cinsi Pozuntu (XBT-11: 6D33 Coercive Sexual Sadism Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 302.84 Sexual Sadism Disorder) — persistent and intense sexual arousal from inflicting physical or psychological suffering on a non-consenting person. The patient has acted on these fantasies or experiences significant distress/functional impairment.

title: 2. History
text: Krafft-Ebing (1886) — term sadism from Marquis de Sade.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Rare in clinical populations; 5–15% in forensic contexts of sexual offense samples. Almost always in males High comorbidity ASPD, other paraphilic disorders.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Biological and psychosocial factors; childhood trauma, antisocial traits. Hypersexuality and impulsivity are frequently comorbid.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Non-consensual sexual arousal without causing harm to the victim. Typical context — criminal offense (compulsion-sadistic). In severe cases — sadistic murders, serious physical injury.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Consensual BDSM is not a clinical disorder. Forensic clinical interview. Risk assessment (Static-99R, SVR-20). Comorbid disorders (ASPD, psychopathy — PCL-R).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Forensic clinical interview. PCL-R (Psychopathy Checklist), Static-99R, SVR-20.

title: 8. Treatment
text: WFSBP 2020 step‑by‑step algorithm: CBT and prevention of relapse; GnRH agonist in the most severe case. Forensic psychiatric supervision; in hospital or correctional setting. Psixopatiya komorbid hallarda standart psixoterapiya az effektivdir. WFSBP 2020 — in severe cases combination (CBT + antiandrogen + GnRH).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: In severe cases, high risk of relapse; multimodal intervention and continuous monitoring are essential.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D33 Coercive sexual sadism disorder. 2024. Thibaut F. et al. WFSBP. World J Biol Psychiatry 2020. Hare R.D. Psychopathy Checklist-Revised (PCL-R). 2nd ed. 2003.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Frotteurizm (XBT-11: 6D34 Frotteuristic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 302.89) — persistent and intense sexual arousal from touching or rubbing one's genitals against a non-consenting person. The patient has acted on the fantasies or experiences significant distress/functional impairment. Typical context — crowded public transport and crowds.

title: 2. History
text: "Frotteur" — from the French word for "to rub".

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: males 7–8% (including subclinical fantasies); clinical disorder low. Comorbid other paraphilic disorders.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: General etiology for paraphilia; comorbid hypersexuality and impulsivity.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Touching an unwilling person in crowded public places (bus, metro, crowd) Patient escapes — victim cannot complain immediately. Legal consequences (sexual harassment, sexual crime).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Forensic clinical interview.

title: 8. Treatment
text: WFSBP 2020 step‑by‑step: CBT and prevention of relapse; Antiandrogen / GnRH in severe cases. Legal-medical coordination.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Reduction of relapse with WFSBP intervention.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D34 Frotteuristic disorder. 2024. Thibaut F. et al. WFSBP. World J Biol Psychiatry 2020.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Self-applied factitious disorder (ICD-11: 6D50 DSM-5-TR: 300.19 Factitious Disorder Imposed on Self) — intentional production of physical or psychological symptoms or injury in oneself, presentation of these symptoms to others; no obvious external incentive (distinguishing it from malingering). The patient's primary motivation is to assume the sick role or be perceived as a patient.

title: 2. History
text: Asher R. (1951) — term "Munchausen syndrome" (from Baron Munchausen's fantastic stories). Eisendrath S.J. reviews — diagnosis and clinical approach.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Rare in clinical populations (1–2% of hospital admissions); under-detection in clinical approach. Sex: relatively higher in females (in the context of medical profession). Komorbidlik: BPD, depresiv pozuntular, travma anamnezi.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Psychological — deep emotional need in medical role; childhood trauma, neglect. Medical system proximity (being a healthcare professional). BPD is frequently comorbid.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Multiple medical consultations and examinations; numerous clinic visits ("hospital shopping"). Multiple surgical procedures. Intentional creation of symptom or injury (bleeding, creating infection, poisoning with medication, fabricating somatic complaint). Clinical presentation 'impossible' or atypical; laboratory results contradict patient. The patient may lie in interview; medical history inaccu

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Diagnosis is difficult — requires evidence (insurance documents, information from other clinics, video). Clinical suspicion — atypical clinical presentations, multiple clinics, "impossible" symptom. Obtain medical history from other clinics (with patient consent). Insurance and pharmacy records Observation — method of creating laboratory samples. Komorbid BPD, MDD, travma anamnezi.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Medical history (from other clinics). Psychiatric interview for comorbid disorders.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Single physician model — all complaints are managed through that physician. Limitation of invasive procedures and unnecessary examinations. Psychotherapy — supportive, CBT; long-term follow-up. Comorbid BPD, MDD, trauma treatment. Confrontation abruptly 'burns' the patient; better in a gentle and caring context. Eisendrath S.J. — soft confrontation and therapeutic alliance.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic course; "hospital shopping" continues. Long-term therapeutic alliance and treatment of comorbidities improve prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D50 Factitious disorder imposed on self. 2024. Eisendrath S.J. Factitious physical disorders: treatment with the soft confrontation. Psychosomatics 1989;30(4):383–387. Levenson J.L. (ed.). APA Publishing Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine and Consultation-Liaison Psychiatry. 3rd ed. 2018. Asher R. Munchausen's syndrome. Lancet 1951;1(6650):339–341.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Factitious disorder imposed on another (ICD-11: 6D51 Factitious Disorder Imposed on Another; DSM-5-TR: 300.19) — the patient creates or feigns physical or psychological symptoms in another person (typically their own child, elderly or disabled dependent); they bring that person to the hospital as a victim. Originally termed 'Munchausen by proxy' (Meadow R. 1977).
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 2. History
text: Meadow R. (1977) — 'Munchausen syndrome by proxy' Lancet. APSAC, AAP — child protection protocols.
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Rare — annual incidence ~2/100,000 children (Sheridan M.S. Child Abuse Negl 2003 review); under-detection. Typical perpetrator — biological mother (>90%); victim — child (typically <5 years). Victims' mortality 6–9% (Sheridan); morbidity high.
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Psychological needs of the patient — attention in the medical role, relationships, 'hero' image. Occupational burnout. History of trauma or neglect in the patient.
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Child's symptoms occur only with mother; improves when placed in bed and mother is separate. Multiple clinics, atypical symptoms, "impossible" medical history. Mother — a medical professional or rich in medical information. The mother appears overly polite and an "ideal caregiver." Typical induction methods: drug poisoning, mechanical obstruction of food or airway, blood collection, infection crea
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Diagnosis is placed on the file, not on the victim. APSAC, AAP — child protection protocols; this clinical condition also category of child abuse . Clinical suspicion — atypical symptom pattern, related to mother. Pediatrician, psychiatrist, social worker multidisciplinary team. Obtaining medical documentation from other clinics. Separation test — monitoring symptoms when the patient is separated
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Pediatric clinical assessment. Medical history gathering. Social service and child protection referral.
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 8. Treatment
text: Child protection referral — legal protocols. The patient's (patient's) psychiatric treatment: Psychotherapy (long-term); Comorbid disorders (BPD, MDD, trauma); Medical occupational context - occupational prohibitions may be considered. Family intervention and sibling protection assessment. Victim child's psychotherapy – trauma intervention. APSAC — child protection protocols.
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: For the victim – high mortality (6–9%) and morbidity without intervention. For the patient — improvement with psychotherapy; long-term follow-up.
sub: FACTITIOUS DISORDER IMPOSED ON ANOTHER (MUNCHAUSEN BY PROXY)

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Delirium (XBT-11: 6D70 DSM-5-TR: 293.0) — an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and consciousness, cognitive deficit (memory, orientation, language, visuospatial) and perceptual disturbances (illusions, hallucinations) characterized neuropsychiatric syndrome. Develops in response to a typical medical cause (infection, medication, metabolic, neurological).

title: 2. History
text: Hippocrates "phrenitis" — classic description of delirium. Lipowski Z.J. (1980-90s) — modern conceptualization. CAM (Confusion Assessment Method, Inouye S.K. 1990) — gold standard screening.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence in hospitalized elderly 15–30%; in ICU 50–80%; post-surgery 15–50%. Komorbidlik: demensiya (delirium superimposed on demensiya), infeksiya, polifarmasiya. Mortality and morbidity high; risk of long-term cognitive decline.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Multiple factors: Infections — sepsis, pneumonia, UTI; Medication — anticholinergics, opioids, benzodiazepines, steroids; Substance withdrawal (alcohol, benzodiazepine); Metabolic — electrolyte, glucose, ammonia, uremia; Hypoxia, hypoperfusion; Neurological — seizure, stroke, brain tumor, encephalitis; Pain, constipation, urinary retention; Environment — sensory deprivation, sleep disruption (espe

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Acute onset (hours-days) and fluctuation. Attention impairment. Cognitive deficits — memory, orientation (time, place, person). Perceptual disturbances – visual hallucinations typical. Sleep-wake cycle disruption. Sundowning — worsening in evening hours.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NICE CG103 — Delirium: prevention, diagnosis, management (2010, 2019 update). CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) — gold standard screening. CAM-ICU — for intensive care. Medical cause investigation: complete blood count, electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, Mg, P), glucose, liver, kidney, TSH, B12, troponin, ECG, urine analysis and culture, blood culture, chest X-ray, brain MRI/CT, EEG (seizure suspicion), lum

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MMSE or MoCA (initial indicators of cognitive function). From the above medical panel. EEG — rule out non-convulsive status.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Environmental modification — sensory (glasses, hearing aid), daylight, nighttime darkness, quiet environment, presence of loved ones. Bedside care — hydration, nutrition, urination and defecation, movement. Benzodiazepine — only in alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal delirium; otherwise Out-of-body (exacerbates delirium). Prevention — ABCDEF bundle in ICU (Awakening, Breathing, Coordination, Deli

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Hospital mortalitesi 25–33%. In survivors — functional decline, long-term cognitive impairment, increased dementia risk. Early etiological intervention improves prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D70 Delirium. 2024. NICE CG103. Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management. 2010, 2019 update. Inouye S.K. et al. Clarifying confusion: the confusion assessment method. Ann Intern Med 1990;113(12):941–948. Burry L. et al. Antipsychotics for treatment of delirium in hospitalised non-ICU patients. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2018;(6):CD005594.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Mild Neurocognitive Impairment (ICD-11: 6D71 Mild Neurocognitive Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 331.83) — mild decline in one or more cognitive domains (memory, complex attention, executive function, language, visuospatial, social cognition); Daily activity is maintained (though compensation may be needed). Typical name — "Mild Cognitive Impairment" (MCI).

title: 2. History
text: Petersen R.C. (1999) — clinical conceptualization of MCI. AAN (2018) — Practice Guideline for MCI (Petersen).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence 10–20% in those aged 65+. Annual 10–15% of MCI transition to Alzheimer dementia. Risk factors: age, ApoE4, cardiovascular.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Alzheimer's prodromal (amnestic MCI); Lewy bodies prodromal; Medical (depression, thyroid, B12, medication effects).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: The patient or a close person complains of cognitive decline. Mild deficit on standardized tests (typically -1 to -2 SD). Daily functioning is preserved (minimal compensation in complex activities). Subtypes: amnestic (memory dominant — high Alzheimer's risk), non-amnestic (executive or other domain).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: AAN 2018 — etiological investigation (Alzheimer, vascular) and biomarkers in developmental stage. Clinical interview + informant. MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment), MMSE. Laboratory — B12, folate, TSH, liver, kidney, glucose, HIV/syphilis. Brain MRI — vascular, atrophy, other pathology. Biomarkers (if clinical suspicion) — CSF amyloid-β/tau, PET imaging (Alzheimer's prodrome).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MoCA (≤ 25 pathological); MMSE (24–28 MCI). Neuropsychological battery (memory, executive, language, visuospatial). MRI (routine), biomarkers (selective).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Comorbid depression, anxiety treatment (SSRIs). Monitoring risk of transition to dementia AAN 2018 — modifiable factors and lifestyle. FINGER trial (Ngandu Lancet 2015) — multimodal intervention. Lecanemab — CLARITY-AD (van Dyck NEJM 2023); donanemab — TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 (Sims JAMA 2023).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Stability 30%, transition to dementia 10–15%/year, remission 15–20%. Amnestic MCI as Alzheimer's prodrome — high conversion rate.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D71 Mild neurocognitive disorder. 2024. Petersen R.C. et al. Practice guideline update summary: Mild cognitive impairment. Neurology 2018;90(3):126–135. Ngandu T. et al. A 2-year multidomain intervention of diet, exercise, cognitive training, and vascular risk monitoring (FINGER): a randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2015;385(9984):2255–2263.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Amnestik Pozuntu (XBT-11: 6D72 Amnestic Disorder; DSM-5-TR: 294.0 and similar) — significant impairment in learning and recalling new information; other cognitive domains relatively preserved. Due to a medical condition (Wernicke-Korsakoff, brain injury, encephalitis, anoxia), substance effect, or other causes.

title: 2. History
text: Korsakoff S. (1887) — description of alcohol-related amnesia. Scoville W.B., Milner B. (1957) — H.M. case (amnesia after bilateral hippocampotomy).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Korsakoff syndrome — in 12–25% of chronic heavy alcohol users. Amnesia after traumatic brain injury — in 30–50% of TBI patients. Transient global amnesia (TGA) — prevalence in age 65+ 5–25/100,000/year.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Wernicke-Korsakoff — thiamine (B1) deficiency (alcohol, malnutrition). Traumatic brain injury (TBI). Hypoxia/anoxia — cardiac arrest, carbon monoxide. Herpes simpleks ensefaliti. Autoimmun ensefalit (anti-NMDA, anti-LGI1). Transient global amnesia (TGA) — suspected vascular etiology. Substance (alcohol blackout, benzodiazepine). Stroke (especially bilateral thalamic or hippocampal).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Anterograde amnesia — impairment in learning new information (core feature). Retrograde amnesia — memory for the past (before injury first; "Ribot's law" — recent memories are lost earlier than remote ones). Confabulation — in Korsakoff syndrome — patient fills memory gaps with fabrications. Other cognitive domains are relatively preserved (intellect, language, visuospatial).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical interview + informant. Neuropsychological test — memory (WMS-IV, RAVLT, Wechsler Logical Memory). Brain MRI — bilateral hippocampal, medial temporal, thalamic pathology. Vitamin B1 level or empirical thiamine (suspected Korsakoff syndrome). Autoimmun ensefalit panel (atipik halda).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: WMS-IV (Wechsler Memory Scale), RAVLT.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Etiological — thiamine (Wernicke-Korsakoff — 500 mg IV × 3 times/day × 3 days, then 250 mg IM/day × 3–5 days; long-term oral thiamine in alcohol withdrawal). Autoimmune encephalitis — immunotherapy (steroids, IVIG, plasmapheresis, rituximab). Herpes encephalitis — acyclovir. Compensatory strategies (external memory aids, daily structuring). Treatment of substance use (alcohol).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Korsakoff — residual deficit typical; acute intervention with thiamine for Wernicke critical. TBI — partial recovery with rehabilitation

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D72 Amnestic disorder. 2024. Royal College of Physicians. Wernicke's encephalopathy and Korsakoff's syndrome. 2014. Graus F. et al. A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis. Lancet Neurol 2016;15(4):391–404. Wilson B.A. Neuropsychological Rehabilitation. Wiley; 2009.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Dementia due to Alzheimer's disease (ICD-11: 6D80 DSM-5-TR: 294.1x Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Alzheimer's Disease) — gradual and persistent cognitive decline, initially episodic memory, then multiple cognitive domains and significant impairment of daily functioning. Most common cause of dementia (60–70%).

title: 2. History
text: Alzheimer A. (1906) — first clinical and pathohistological description. Amiloid hipotezi (Hardy J., Higgins G.A. Science 1992). NICE NG97 (2018) — dementia clinical guideline. FDA — aducanumab (2021, controversial), lecanemab (2023), donanemab (2024) — anti-amyloid antibodies.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Global 55 mln+ dementia patients (WHO 2023); 60–70% Alzheimer's. Prevalence by age: 65 years 5%, 80+ years 25%+. Sex: higher in females (related to life expectancy). Comorbidity: cardiovascular, depression, anxiety, BPSD (behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Familial Alzheimer's (1%): APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 mutations (early onset, dominant). Sporadic (99%) – multiple genes (ApoE4 strongest risk; LOAD GWAS loci). Modifiable factors (Lancet Commission 2024) — alongside age, ApoE4: low education, hearing loss, hypertension, obesity, smoking, depression, physical inactivity, social isolation, diabetes, air pollution, alcohol, TBI, vision loss, high LDL.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Typical onset — episodic memory decline (difficulty retaining new information). Stages: Early — memory, anomia, orientation difficulty; Moderate — visual-spatial, executive function, supports daily activities; Late — language very limited, loss of identification, mobility, food intake, lip-swallowing. Atypical forms: posterior cortical atrophy (visuospatial dominant), logopenic primary progressive

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: NIA-AA Research Framework (Jack C.R. 2018) — biomarker-based diagnosis (ATN — amyloid, tau, neurodegeneration). NICE NG97 — based on clinical diagnosis; biomarkers selective. AAN — diagnostic tools. Clinical interview + informant. Neyropsixoloji battery. Laboratory — B12, folate, TSH, liver, kidney, glucose, HIV/syphilis (medical cause exclusion). Brain MRI (atrophy, vascular changes, other pathol

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MoCA, MMSE, ACE-III, neyropsixoloji. BPSD scales (NPI — Neuropsychiatric Inventory).

title: 8. Treatment
text: First-line — non-pharmacological (environment, social support, physical activity, music therapy); In refractory cases — atypical antipsychotic (risperidone, quetiapine) at low dose, short-term; FDA BLACK BOX — antipsychotics increase mortality in elderly dementia patients (stroke, cardiovascular) . SSRI — comorbid depression/anxiety; Trazodone — for sleep disturbance;

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: After diagnosis, median life expectancy is 8–10 years. Rapid course, family history, altered by ApoE4 Komorbid kardiyovaskulyar mortalite.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D80 Dementia due to Alzheimer disease. 2024. NICE NG97. Dementia: assessment, management and support. 2018. Livingston G. et al. Dementia prevention, intervention, and care: 2024 report of the Lancet standing Commission. Lancet 2024;404(10452):572–628. Jack C.R. et al. NIA-AA Research Framework: Toward a biological definition of Alzheimer's disease. Alzheimer's Dement 2018;14(4):535–

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Vascular Dementia (ICD-11: 6D81 DSM-5-TR: 290.4x and similar) — cognitive decline and impairment of daily functioning developing as a result of cerebrovascular disease (stroke, small vessel disease, hypoperfusion). Second most common cause of dementia (~15–20%).

title: 2. History
text: Binswanger O. (1894) — classic description of subcortical vascular dementia. NINDS-AIREN (1993) — international diagnostic criteria. VASCOG (2014) — Updated consensus (Vascular Cognitive Disorders).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 15–20% of dementia cases; often mixed with Alzheimer's Prevalence increases with age. Risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, atrial fibrillation, smoking, history of stroke.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Multi-infarct dementia. Small vessel disease (subcortical leukoaraiosis, lacunar infarcts — Binswanger). Strategik infarkt (talamik, hippokampal). Hipoperfusiya (sistemik hipotenziya, kardiyak arest). CADASIL (autosomal dominant subkortikal demensiya, NOTCH3).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: "Stepwise" decline (as opposed to the gradual decline of Alzheimer's). Executive function, processing speed, dominant attention — memory is relatively less impaired (difference from Alzheimer). Focal neurological signs — hemiparesis, dysarthria, extrapyramidal, visual field defect. Mood disorders (vascular depression), apathy, abulia frequent. Postural disturbances (gait, balance).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: VASCOG 2014 (Sachdev P. et al. Alzheimer Dis Assoc Disord) — Vascular Cognitive Impairment umbrella. NICE NG97 — management of vascular risk factors. Clinical interview + informant. MoCA (attention to executive function subscales). Neurological examination — focal signs. Brain MRI (FLAIR — leukoaraiosis, DWI — acute infarcts, T2/SWI — microhemorrhages). Cardiovascular evaluation — blood pressure,

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MoCA, neyropsixoloji. MRT, kardiyovaskulyar.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Hypertension (target SBP <140, sometimes <130); Diabetes HbA1c management; Antiplatelet (aspirin) or anticoagulant (atrial fibrillation — DOAC); Refusal of cigarettes; Physical activity, diet. AChEI and memantine — evidence base limited for VaD, but may be effective in mixed dementia; NICE NG97 — considered in mixed cases. BPSD management — non-pharmacological first; antipsychotics limited (same F

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Risk factor management reduces the rate of decline. Stroke history determines comorbidity prognosis.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D81 Vascular dementia. 2024. P. Sachdev et al. Diagnostic criteria for vascular cognitive disorders: a VASCOG statement. Alzheimer Dis Assoc Disord 2014;28(3):206–218. McNeil J.J. et al. Effect of Aspirin on Disability-free Survival in the Healthy Elderly (ASPREE). NEJM 2018;379(16):1499–1508. Livingston G. et al. Lancet Commission 2024.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Lewy Body Dementia (DLB; ICD-11: 6D82 ; DSM-5-TR: 331.82) — dementia developing from α-synuclein pathology; classic triad — cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism. 5–15% of dementias. Parkinson's disease dementia (PDD) — same pathology, but dementia develops at least 1 year after parkinsonism; DLB and PDD clinical spectrum.

title: 2. History
text: Lewy F. (1912) — Description of Lewy bodies in Parkinson's disease. McKeith I. (1996, 2017 update) — DLB consensus criteria.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 5–15% of dementia cases; relatively higher in males Prevalence increases with age ≥65. Comorbidity: depression, delusions, REM sleep behavior disorder.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: α-Synuclein aggregation — Lewy bodies in cortical and substantia nigra regions. SNCA, LRRK2, and GBA genes confer risk.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD) — it can begin years before diagnosis. Sensitivity to antipsychotics — severe neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like reaction. DAT-SPECT (123I-FP-CIT) shows reduction in presynaptic dopamine neurons.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: McKeith I. et al. Neurology 2017 — diagnostic consensus. DAT-SPECT or MIBG cardiac scintigraphy — supportive biomarker. Clinical interview + informant (especially RBD). MoCA, MMSE, neuropsychological (visuospatial and executive function dominant). Neurological examination. Brain MRI (hippocampal atrophy less than in Alzheimer's). DAT-SPECT (when indicated).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: MoCA, neyropsixoloji. Polysomnografiya (RBD).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Memantine — for moderate-severe cases. RBD — melatonin (3–12 mg at night) or clonazepam (low dose). BPSD qeyri-farmakoloji birinci. FDA antipsychotic black box in elderly dementia patients. McKeith I. Lancet 2000 — rivastigmine in DLB. NICE NG97 — rivastigmine or donepezil for DLB. Antipsychotic sensitivity — McKeith I. reviews.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Moderate expected lifespan from diagnosis 5–8 years; sometimes shorter than Alzheimer's. Antipsychotic reaction is a critical complication.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6D82 Dementia due to Lewy body disease. 2024. McKeith I.G. et al. Diagnosis and management of dementia with Lewy bodies: Fourth consensus report. Neurology 2017;89(1):88–100. McKeith I. et al. Efficacy of rivastigmine in dementia with Lewy bodies: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled international study. Lancet 2000;356(9247):2031–2036.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD; ICD-11: 6D83 ; DSM-5-TR: 331.19) — dementia developing from atrophy of the frontal and/or temporal lobes; typical onset between ages 45–65 (most common among early-onset dementias). Three clinical variants: bvFTD (behavioral variant) and two forms of PPA (Primary Progressive Aphasia — semantic and non-fluent).

title: 2. History
text: Pick A. (1892) — "Pick's disease" — description of frontotemporal atrophy. Neary D. et al. (1998) — FTD clinical consensus criteria. Rascovsky K. et al. (2011) — updated criteria for bvFTD.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 5–10% of dementia cases before age 65; Alzheimer's dominates after age 65. Comorbidity: ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis) — FTD-ALS spectrum (C9orf72 mutation).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Genetic (40%) — C9orf72 (most common), MAPT, GRN mutations. Pathology — tau (Pick body, MAPT), TDP-43 (TDP-43A, B, C, D, GRN, C9orf72), FUS proteins.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Early personality change — apathy, disinhibition, socially inappropriate behavior, loss of empathy. Stereotypical behavior (ritualistic). Hyperoral behavior (food changes, "sweet tooth"). Executive function significantly impaired; memory and visuo-spatial relatively preserved at onset. Logopenic — associated with Alzheimer's, distinct.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Rascovsky K. et al. Brain 2011 — Behavioral Variant Frontotemporal Dementia. Gorno-Tempini M.L. et al. Neurology 2011 — PPA. Clinical interview + informant (behavioral change). Neuropsychological battery (executive function, language). Brain MRI — frontal and/or temporal atrophy. FDG-PET — hypometabolism. Genetic testing (family history). ALS screening (clinical or electromyography).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: FAB (Frontal Assessment Battery), neyropsixoloji.

title: 8. Treatment
text: AChEI — Ineffective in FTD or harmful (may worsen behavior); differs from Alzheimer. SSRI — for behavioral symptoms (disinhibition, compulsivity, depression) in bvFTD; some evidence. Trazodone — for agitation; studies by Lebert F. et al. Atypical antipsychotic — only in severe behavioral disturbance, short-term, FDA black box rule. Carer support and psychoeducation critical (behavioral symptoms ar

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: After diagnosis, median life expectancy is 6–11 years; FTD-ALS is shorter (3 years). Rapid decline in some mutations

title: 11. Sources

Brain 2011;134(Pt 9):2456–2477. Gorno-Tempini M.L. et al. Classification of primary progressive aphasia and its variants. Neurology 2011;76(11):1006–1014. Lebert F. et al.
