title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Postnatal Depressiya (XBT-11: 6E20 Mental and Behavioural Disorders Associated with Pregnancy, Childbirth or the Puerperium; DSM-5-TR: 296.x Major Depressive Disorder with peripartum onset specifier) — a major depressive episode developing after childbirth (typically first 6 months; some guidelines up to 1 year). Affects 10–15% of women.

title: 2. History
text: Marce L.V. (1858) — first clinical description of postpartum psychosis and depression. EPDS — Cox J.L. et al. (1987) — Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale. NICE CG192 (2014, 2018) — antenatal and postnatal mental health. FDA — brexanolone (2019), zuranolone (2023) — first specifically approved drugs for postpartum depression.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 10–15% (high-income countries), 20–30% (low- and middle-income countries). "Baby blues" — milder, short-term (1–2 weeks) — occurs in 50–80% of women; does not require clinical intervention. Comorbidity: anxiety, OCD, panic. Risk factors: previous depression, family history, depression during pregnancy, lack of partner support, socio-economic stress.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Hormonal — sharp drop in estrogen and progesterone after childbirth; allopregnanolone dysregulation. Sleep disturbance with newborn care. Psychosocial stress, relationships, social support. Genetic and prior history of affective disorder.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Major depressive episode symptoms (sleep disturbance, appetite loss, anhedonia, worthlessness, suicidal thoughts). Specific characteristics: affective distancing towards the infant, difficulties in infant bonding, belief in inability to fulfill the maternal role, intrusive thoughts regarding harming the infant (ego-dystonic — similar to OCD; distinct from postpartum psychosis).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Major depressive episode criteria (DSM-5-TR 5+ symptoms for 2 weeks; ICD-11) + peripartum onset specifier (DSM-5-TR — during pregnancy or within 4 weeks postpartum; ICD-11 — broader, up to 6 months-1 year). NICE CG192 (2014, 2018) — diagnosis and treatment. ACOG Committee Opinion 757 (2018) — perinatal depression screening. USPSTF — screening in pregnant and postpartum women.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: C-SSRS — suicide and risk of harm to infant.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Sertraline (least excreted into milk) and paroxetine — first-line; Fluoxetine — long half-life, use with caution; Citalopram, escitalopram — appropriate. Family support, peer support (e.g., PND helpline). Social service and parent-child bonding support. Sleep hygiene and involvement of family members in night care. NICE CG192 — sertraline and paroxetine first-line in breastfeeding.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: 70-80% remission with adequate treatment. Risk of recurrence in subsequent pregnancies (~25%). Without treatment, chronicity and comorbidity.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6E20 Mental and behavioural disorders associated with pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. 2024. NICE CG192. Antenatal and postnatal mental health. 2014, 2018 update. ACOG Committee Opinion No. 757: Screening for Perinatal Depression. 2018. Cox J.L., Holden J.M., Sagovsky R. Detection of postnatal depression. Development of the 10-item Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale. Br J P

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Postnatal Psychosis (PPP; ICD-11: 6E21 ; DSM-5-TR: Brief Psychotic Disorder or Bipolar I with peripartum onset specifier) — an acute-onset psychotic episode after childbirth (typically within the first 2 weeks); hallucinations, delusions, cognitive disorganization, mood lability. This is a medical emergency — Infanticide (~4%) and maternal suicide risk high.

title: 2. History
text: Marce L.V. (1858) — classic description of postpartum psychosis. Bergink V. et al. (2015) — 4-step treatment algorithm. NICE CG192 — peripartum psychiatric conditions.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Incidence: 1–2/1000 births. 25–50% in women with a history of bipolar disorder; 30% with a history of previous PPP. Comorbidity: mostly bipolar in nature. Mortality: infanticide 4%, suicide up to 5% (untreated).

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Hormonal — sharp postpartum drop in estrogen and progesterone. Bipolar disorder predisposition (most PPP — manifestation of a bipolar episode).

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Rapid onset (24–72 hours, typically within first 2 weeks). Polymorphic psychotic symptoms — delusions (particularly regarding baby — baby being "evil" or "demanded"; mother "must escape"), hallucinations. mood variability — manic or depressive components. Cognitive disorganization, confusion. Sleep disturbance severe. Behavior — bizarre, extreme, impaired infant care

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Clinical diagnosis: acute-onset psychotic episode postpartum; coded under bipolar manic or acute psychotic category + peripartum onset specifier. NICE CG192 — emergency medical condition. Royal College of Psychiatrists perinatal psychiatric service standards. Clinical interview — rapid onset, nature of symptoms. Medical assessment — postpartum medical conditions (thyroiditis, infection, encephalop

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Clinical psychiatric and neurological. The above medical panel. Anti-NMDA-R antibodies (CSF) — in atypical cases. C-SSRS, infant safety.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Step 1: Benzodiazepine (lorazepam) acute stabilization; Step 2: atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone); Step 3: Addition of lithium; Step 4: ECT in refractory or catatonic features. Bipolar primarily — lithium or valproate (valproate with caution in women of childbearing age; but considered in non-lactating women with adequate contraception).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Full recovery in most patients with adequate treatment. Risk of recurrence in subsequent pregnancies is high (~50–80%). Long-term management of bipolar disorder is crucial.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6E21 Puerperal psychosis. 2024. NICE CG192. 2014/2018. Bergink V. et al. Treatment of psychosis and mania in the postpartum period. Am J Psychiatry 2015;172(2):115–123. Bergink V. et al. Prevention of postpartum psychosis and mania in women at high risk. Am J Psychiatry 2012;169(6):609–615. Graus F. et al. Lancet Neurol 2016 (autoimmun ensefalit).

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Distinct from factitious disorder (6D50) and somatic symptom disorder (6C20) — here there is a confirmed medical illness, but psychological factors influence the course.
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 2. History
text: Alexander F. (1950) — conceptualization of 'psychosomatic medicine'. Engel G.L. (1977) — 'biopsychosocial model'. Levenson J.L. (2018) — APA Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine.
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Universal — psychological factors influence all chronic somatic diseases. Depression in cardiovascular disease — 20–40%; diabetes — 25%; cancer — 15–25%. Wide use of consultation-liaison psychiatry service.
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Biopsychosocial interaction — biological predisposition + psychological factors + social context.
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Comorbid depression/anxiety affects illness (e.g., increased mortality after myocardial infarction). Medication non-adherence (compliance/adherence). False beliefs about illness. Stress worsens illness course. Behavioral factors (smoking, alcohol, diet, physical inactivity).
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Reduces treatment response; Increases health risk; The pathophysiology is worsened; Medical intervention alters the necessity. Consultation-liaison psychiatry model. Evaluation of medical illness. Identification of psychological factors — depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), medication adherence (Morisky).
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: PHQ-9, GAD-7, Morisky. Illness-specific scales (DDS — Diabetes Distress Scale, Beck-cardiac).
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 8. Treatment
text: An integrated multidisciplinary approach — somatic specialist + psychiatrist + psychologist. Illness-specific psychosocial intervention: Cardiac CBT (post-MI depression); Diabetes self-management education (DSME); Oncology distress monitoring; Chronic pain CBT and ACT. Antidepressant — disease-specific side effect profile: Cardiac — sertraline, escitalopram;
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Integrative approach improves both somatic and psychological outcomes.
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 10. Myths and misconceptions
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6E40 Psychological or behavioural factors affecting disorders or diseases classified elsewhere. 2024. Levenson J.L. (ed.). APA Publishing Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine. 3rd ed. 2018. NICE NG185. Acute coronary syndromes. 2020. Whooley M.A., Wong J.M. Depression and cardiovascular disorders. Annu Rev Clin Psychol 2013;9:327–354.
sub: PSYCHOLOGICAL FACTORS AFFECTING OTHER MEDICAL CONDITIONS

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Secondary Psychotic Syndrome (ICD-11: 6E60 Secondary Psychotic Syndrome; DSM-5-TR: 293.81/293.82 Psychotic Disorder Due to Another Medical Condition) — psychotic episode developing in the context of another medical condition (autoimmune, infectious, endocrine, metabolic, neurological) or a substance. First-line intervention — treatment directed at the primary etiology .

title: 2. History
text: The term "Symptomatic psychosis" was formulated in the 20th century. Dalmau J. (2007) — discovery of anti-NMDA-R encephalitis; autoimmune psychosis revolution. Graus F. et al. (2016) — autoimmune encephalitis clinical guideline.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 3–8% of all first-episode psychosis cases have secondary etiologies. Anti-NMDA-R encephalitis — rare but reversible psychosis in young women; paraneoplastic with teratoma. Hashimoto encephalopathy, lupus encephalopathy.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Psychotic symptoms (delusion, hallucination, disorganized behavior). Atypical features (characteristic of autoimmune encephalitis): Rapid onset Motor symptoms (catatonic, dystonias, orofacial dyskinesia); Autonomic disturbance (hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia); Impairment of consciousness. Compared to classic psychosis (schizophrenia) — atypical presentation raises suspicion.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Graus F. et al. Lancet Neurol 2016 — autoimmune encephalitis clinical approach. Clinical suspicion — new-onset psychosis, especially with atypical features or in young women. Medical assessment: Complete blood count, liver/renal, electrolytes (Na, Ca), glucose, ammonia; TSH, TPO antibodies (Hashimoto); Cortisol level (Cushing/Addison);

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: The above laboratory panel.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Autoimmune encephalitis — IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 5 days → IVIG and/or plasmapheresis; second-line rituximab, cyclophosphamide; Paraneoplastic — tumor treatment (ovarian teratoma surgery); Endocrine — thyroid, cortisol, calcium correction; Infectious — antibiotic, antiviral; Metabolic — correction of the disorder; Medication-induced — discontinuation of the suspected agent.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Most cases respond to etiological treatment; reversible condition. Anti-NMDA-R encephalitis — 75% good recovery with early immunotherapy.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6E60 Secondary psychotic syndrome. 2024. Graus F. et al. A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis. Lancet Neurol 2016;15(4):391–404. Dalmau J. et al. Anti-NMDA-receptor encephalitis: case series and analysis of the effects of antibodies. Lancet Neurol 2008;7(12):1091–1098. Levenson J.L. (ed.). APA Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine. 3rd ed. 2018.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Secondary Affective Syndrome (ICD-11: 6E61 Secondary Mood Syndrome; DSM-5-TR: 293.83 Mood Disorder Due to Another Medical Condition) — depressive, manic, or mixed mood episodes in the context of another medical condition, substance, or medication effect.

title: 2. History
text: The term "Organic Affective Disorder" belongs to the 20th century; the biopsychosocial model in clinical practice.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Depressive symptoms are prevalent in chronic medical conditions; some meet criteria for clinical depressive episode. Steroid-induced mood disorders—high-dose prednisone 5–18% mania/hypomania, depression. Interferon-induced depression — 20–40% in HCV treatment. Post-stroke depression - 30%.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Endocrine — hypothyroid (depression), hyperthyroid (mania-like), Cushing, Addison. Neurological — stroke (left frontal — depression, right — mania), Parkinson's, MS, brain tumor, dementia, TBI. Substance/medication — steroids, interferon, dopamine agonists, isotretinoin, β-blocker, anticonvulsant. Metabolic — uremia, B12, folate. Infectious — HIV, syphilis, COVID.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Symptoms of depressive, manic, mixed, and hypomanic episodes. Medical condition or substance-related temporal association. Features not characteristic of classic affective disorder (late onset, atypical presentation).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Konsultasiya-liaison protokollar. Clinical interview + medical history. Medical and laboratory assessment (TSH, B12, cortisol, renal, hepatic). Medication list review. Brain imaging (stroke, MS, tumor).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Laboratory: TSH, B12, cortisol, ANA, kidney, liver.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Etiological treatment — correction of medical condition or medication. Symptomatic psychiatric treatment: Depressive — SSRIs (disease-specific selection; cardiac — sertraline, escitalopram; oncology — mirtazapine); Manic — antipsychotic (quetiapine, olanzapine); in steroid-induced mania, taper steroids or use antipsychotic; Mixed or refractory — ECT. Multidisciplinary coordination.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Most remissions with etiological intervention.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6E61 Secondary mood syndrome. 2024. Levenson J.L. APA Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine 2018. Robinson R.G., Jorge R.E. Post-stroke depression: a review. Am J Psychiatry 2016;173(3):221–231.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Secondary Anxiety Syndrome (ICD-11: 6E62 Secondary Anxiety Syndrome; DSM-5-TR: 293.84 Anxiety Disorder Due to Another Medical Condition) — anxiety, panic or obsessive-like symptoms within context of another medical condition or substance effect.

title: 2. History
text: Development of consultation-liaison psychiatry; formalization from DSM-III.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Frequency of anxiety is high in chronic medical conditions (cardiac, COPD, endocrine). In hyperthyroidism, 60–70% have anxiety symptoms. Caffeine, stimulant-induced anxiety.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Endocrine — hyperthyroidism, pheochromocytoma, hypoglycemia. Cardiac — arrhythmia, myocardial infarction, mitral valve prolapse. Respiratory — COPD, asthma, pulmonary embolism. Neurological — TBI, Parkinson's, MS. Substance/medication — caffeine, stimulant, corticosteroid, β-blocker (paradoxical), benzodiazepine/alcohol withdrawal. Vestibular pozuntular.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: GAD, panic attack, OCD-like symptoms in the context of a medical condition. Atypical features (late onset, focal symptoms).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Medical evidence + distress symptoms + temporal congruence. Medical evaluation — TSH, glucose, cardiac, vestibular. Medication and caffeine history. Primary anxiety disorders (6B0x);

title: 8. Treatment
text: Etiological treatment. Symptomatic — SSRIs for residual anxiety; benzodiazepines short-term for acute condition. Caffeine restriction.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Significant improvement with etiological intervention.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 6E62 Secondary anxiety syndrome. 2024. Levenson J.L. APA Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine 2018.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Insomnia (ICD-11: 7A00 ; DSM-5-TR: 780.52 Insomnia Disorder) — difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, and/or early morning awakening; daytime functional impairment. Duration ≥ 3 months, frequency ≥ 3 times per week.

title: 2. History
text: AASM (American Academy of Sleep Medicine) — Clinical Practice Guidelines (2021 CBT-I, 2017 pharmacological). European Insomnia Guideline (Riemann D. 2023).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Lifetime prevalence: chronic insomnia 10–15%; insomnia symptoms 30–35%. Sex: 1.5 times higher in females. Comorbidity: MDD ~50%, anxiety, pain, COPD, circadian disorders.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Spielman 3P Model — Predisposing, Precipitating, Perpetuating. Hyperarousal hypothesis — physiological and cognitive stimulation. Behavioral factors — irregular schedule, caffeine, alcohol, screen use, non-sleep activities in the bedroom.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Sleep difficulty (falling asleep), maintenance, early awakening. Daytime symptoms: fatigue, irritability, concentration difficulties, occupational/academic impairment, somatic (headache, GI). Impact on performance, risk of accident.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: AASM — CBT-I first-line. Clinical interview + sleep diary for 1–2 weeks. ISI (Insomnia Severity Index), PSQI. Differential — obstructive sleep apnea (STOP-BANG), restless legs, circadian. Comorbidity (MDD, anxiety). Polysomnography only in atypical cases or suspicion of another sleep disorder.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: STOP-BANG (OSA), Epworth Sleepiness Scale.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Stimulus control (Bootzin)—bed only for sleep; Sleep restriction (Spielman) — restricting time in bed to equal sleep time; Cognitive therapy — catastrophic thoughts about sleep; Digital CBT-I (Sleepio, SHUTi) — effect close to clinical format; NICE 2025 — cost-effective alternative. Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone, eszopiclone); Benzodiazepine (temazepam) — tolerance, dependence risk;

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: 70–80% remission with CBT-I, sustained long-term. Without treatment, chronic course and comorbidity (MDD, anxiety).

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 7A00 Insomnia disorders. 2024. Edinger J.D. et al. Behavioral and psychological treatments for chronic insomnia disorder in adults: an AASM clinical practice guideline. J Clin Sleep Med 2021;17(2):255–262. Riemann D. et al. The European Insomnia Guideline 2023. J Sleep Res 2023;32(6):e14035. Mitchell M.D. et al. Comparative effectiveness of cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia: a

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA; ICD-11: 7A20 ; DSM-5-TR: 327.23) — recurrent complete (apnea) or partial (hypopnea) obstruction of the upper airway during sleep; daytime sleepiness, cognitive impairment, increased cardiovascular mortality.

title: 2. History
text: Gastaut H. (1965) — classic description of OSA. Sullivan C.E. (1981) — invented CPAP. AASM Practice Parameters (update).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: In adults: approximately 13% in men, approximately 6% in women (moderate-to-severe OSA). Increases with age and BMI. Komorbidlik: hipertenziya (50%), kardiovaskulyar, stroke, atrial fibrilyasiya, diabet.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Anatomical — adipose tissue in the upper airway, small mandible, enlarged tonsils/adenoids. Genetic and family history. Posture — worsens in supine position. Alcohol, sedative — worsens.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Waking up with loud snoring, observed apnea, or choking sensation. Daytime sleepiness (dangerous while driving), fatigue. Morning headache, dry mouth Concentration, memory problems. Cinsi disfunksiya, depresiv simptomlar.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Polysomnography (PSG) — AHI (Apnea-Hypopnea Index) ≥ 5/hour + symptoms; AHI ≥ 15 irrespective of symptoms. AASM Clinical Practice Guideline for Diagnostic Testing (2017). Home Sleep Apnea Test (HSAT) — appropriate for moderate-severe OSA. STOP-BANG skrining (Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, BP, BMI, Age, Neck, Gender). Epworth Sleepiness Scale. Polysomnography or HSAT.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Polysomnography or Home Sleep Apnea Testing. BMI, neck circumference.

title: 8. Treatment
text: BiPAP — in refractory cases or CPAP non-response. Weight loss — mild OSA; bariatric surgery in severe obesity. Mandibular advancement device (MAD) — mild-moderate OSA, CPAP non-responsive. Postural therapy — supine-avoidant. Surgery (UPPP, hypoglossal nerve stimulation — Inspire) — in selected cases. Withdrawal from alcohol and sedatives. AASM CPAP standard, MAD selective.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Cardiovascular mortality decreases with CPAP. Without treatment — stroke, atrial fibrillation, diabetes, increased mortality.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 7A20 Obstructive sleep apnoea. 2024. Kapur V.K. et al. AASM Clinical Practice Guideline for Diagnostic Testing for Adult OSA. J Clin Sleep Med 2017;13(3):479–504. Marin J.M. et al. Long-term cardiovascular outcomes in men with OSA-hypopnoea with or without treatment with CPAP. Lancet 2005;365(9464):1046–1053.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Circadian Rhythm Sleep-Wake Disorders (ICD-11: 7A40 DSM-5-TR: 307.45) — mismatch between the internal circadian rhythm and the external social and light schedule; resulting in insomnia and/or sleepiness. Subtypes: Delayed Sleep-Wake Phase Disorder (DSWPD), Advanced Sleep-Wake Phase Disorder, Irregular Sleep-Wake Rhythm, Non-24-hour Sleep-Wake (in blind individuals), Shift Work Disorder, Jet Lag Di

title: 2. History
text: In the 1960s, Aschoff J. — foundations of circadian biology. AASM (2015) — clinical practice guideline for circadian rhythm disorders.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: DSWPD — 7–16% in adolescents and young adults. Shift Work Disorder — 10–30% of shift workers. Comorbidity: MDD, anxiety, bipolar, cardiovascular.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Endogenous circadian rhythm disorder (clock gene mutations rare — Familial Advanced Sleep Phase). Environment — light exposure, social schedule. In adolescents, DSWPD — pubertal hormonal changes.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: AASM 2015 Clinical Practice Guideline. Clinical interview + sleep diary for 1–2 weeks. Dim Light Melatonin Onset (DLMO) — in research context. Exclusion of comorbid sleep disorders. Insomnia, OSA, depressive disorder, narcolepsy.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Sleep diary, actigraphy. Munich Chronotype Questionnaire.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Chronotherapy — gradual shift of sleep timing. Sleep hygiene and behavioral intervention. Shift work — strategic resting, dark room, caffeine timing. Non-24-hour in blind patients — tasimelteon (Hetlioz, FDA approved).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With intervention, most patients improve; chronic form requires ongoing strategy.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 7A40 Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders. 2024. Auger R.R. et al. AASM Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Intrinsic Circadian Rhythm Sleep-Wake Disorders. J Clin Sleep Med 2015;11(10):1199–1236.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Hipersomniya (XBT-11: 7A60 ; DSM-5-TR: 327.10 Hypersomnolence Disorder) — excessive daytime sleepiness or sleep episodes (after ≥ 7 hours of nighttime sleep); functional impairment. In the context of idiopathic hypersomnia, narcolepsy, or other sleep disorders.

title: 2. History
text: In the 1970s–1980s, differentiation from narcolepsy developed; AASM clinical guidelines.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Idiopathic hypersomnia: ~50/100,000. Kleine-Levin syndrome — rare (~1/1 million); recurrent episodes of hypersomnia + appetite/sexual disturbance. Komorbidlik: depresiv simptomlar, OSA, narkolepsiya.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Unknown (idiopathic) — neurobiological dysregulation hypothesis. Dysregulation of the GABA-ergic system (idiopathic). Secondary — Klein-Levin (autoimmune suspicion), post-TBI, MS, depressive disorder.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Excessive Daytime Sleepiness (EDS). Long night sleep (≥ 9 hours) is insufficient. "Sleep drunkenness" — confusion, disorientation upon awakening. Daytime sleep episodes are prolonged and non-restorative (in contrast to narcolepsy, where they are brief and restorative). Kleine-Levin — 1–2 week episodes of hypersomnia + hyperphagia + disinhibited behavior.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: AASM Practice Parameters — diagnostic PSG and MSLT (Multiple Sleep Latency Test). Idiopathic hypersomnia — mean sleep latency on MSLT ≤ 8 min; SOREMP ≤ 1 (different from narcolepsy). Clinical interview and sleep diary. Epworth Sleepiness Scale. PSG + MSLT (excluding narcolepsy). Medical assessment — thyroid, anemia, depressive, OSA.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Modafinil, armodafinil — first-line. Stimulants (methylphenidate, amphetamine) - refractory. Pitolisant (FDA 2019, idiopathic and narcolepsy). Solriamfetol (FDA 2019). Sleep hygiene and behavioral intervention (planned daytime naps). Kleine-Levin — supportive; asymptomatic between episodes. AASM Treatment Guidelines (Maski K. et al. 2021).

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic; functional improvement with pharmacotherapy.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 7A60 Hypersomnolence disorders. 2024. Maski K. et al. Treatment of central disorders of hypersomnolence: AASM Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Sleep Med 2021;17(9):1881–1893.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Narkolepsiya (XBT-11: 7A80 ; DSM-5-TR: 347.0x) — irresistible daytime sleep episodes; two types: Tip 1 (with cataplexy, orexin-A/hypocretin deficiency) and Tip 2 (katapleksiya olmadan).

title: 2. History
text: Gélineau J.B. (1880) — the term narcolepsy. Mignot E. et al. (2000) — discovery of the orexin/hypocretin system in narcolepsy etiology.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence: 25–50 per 100,000. Onset: 10–25 years (bimodal). Komorbidlik: OSA, depresiv simptomlar, OKP.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Type 1 — autoimmune damage to orexin-A neurons (CSF orexin <110 pg/ml); HLA-DQB1*06:02 strong association. H1N1 vaccine (Pandemrix, 2009) — increased incidence of narcolepsy type 1 in Scandinavian countries (Miller E. BMJ 2013). Type 2 — etiology not fully clear.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Complete tetrad rare; EDS main feature.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: ICSD-3 (International Classification of Sleep Disorders) — AASM. HLA typing and CSF orexin — in atypical cases. PSG (night) + MSLT (day) — gold standard. CSF orexin — selective. HLA-DQB1*06:02 — supportive.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: CSF orexin, HLA-DQB1*06:02.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Behavior — planned daydreaming (15–20 min) Driving restriction if EDS present. AASM Treatment Guidelines (Maski 2021). FDA approvals — pitolisant 2019, solriamfetol 2019, Xywav 2020.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Chronic; functional improvement with pharmacotherapy. Comorbidity (MDD, OSA) intervention essential.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. 7A80 Narcolepsy. 2024. Maski K. et al. AASM CPG. J Clin Sleep Med 2021;17(9):1881–1893. Miller E. et al. Risk of narcolepsy in children and young people receiving AS03 adjuvanted pandemic A/H1N1 2009 influenza vaccine. BMJ 2013;346:f794. Mignot E. et al. The role of cerebrospinal fluid hypocretin measurement in the diagnosis of narcolepsy. Arch Neurol 2002;59(10):1553–1562.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Erectile Dysfunction (ED; ICD-11: HA00 Erectile Dysfunction; DSM-5-TR: 302.72) — persistent difficulty in obtaining or maintaining an erection sufficient for sexual intercourse. Duration ≥ 6 months, in most attempts.

title: 2. History
text: 1998 — FDA approval of sildenafil (Viagra) — revolution in ED treatment. AUA (American Urological Association) Guideline (Burnett 2018, 2024 update). EAU (European Association of Urology) Guidelines.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: 5–10% at age 40, 50%+ at age 70. Risk factors: diabetes, cardiovascular disease (ED as cardiovascular early marker), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking, obesity, depression, antidepressants.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Organic (~70%) — vascular, neurogenic, endocrine (hypogonadism), medication-related. Psychogenic (~10%) — performance anxiety, relationship problems, depression. Princeton III Consensus (Nehra 2012) — ED an early cardiovascular marker, cardiac risk assessment important.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Difficulty obtaining or maintaining erection. Presence or absence of morning or nighttime erections (for psychogenic vs organic differentiation) Relationship and emotional burden.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: AUA 2018/2024 and EAU — stepwise approach. Clinical interview (sexual and medical history, relationships). IIEF (International Index of Erectile Function) / SHIM. Laboratory — morning testosterone, glucose, HbA1c, lipid, prolactin, thyroid. Cardiovascular evaluation. Penile ultrasound (vascular suspicion) — selective.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Medical and laboratory (above). Cardiac evaluation (ECG, stress test if needed).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Lifestyle — smoking cessation, weight, physical activity, cardiovascular management; PDE5 inhibitors — sildenafil (25–100 mg), tadalafil (5–20 mg as needed or 2.5–5 mg daily), vardenafil, avanafil; Never co-administered with nitrate ; caution with α-blockers. Intracavernosal alprostadil (PGE1); Transurethral alprostadil (MUSE); Vakuum erektil cihaz. Psychotherapy — in performance anxiety; couple t

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: PDE5 with 70% efficacy; refractory cases 2nd–3rd line; cardiovascular risk management reduces mortality.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. HA00 Erectile dysfunction. 2024. Burnett A.L. et al. Erectile Dysfunction: AUA Guideline. J Urol 2018;200(3):633–641. Salonia A. et al. EAU Guidelines on Sexual and Reproductive Health 2023. Nehra A. et al. The Princeton III Consensus recommendations for the management of erectile dysfunction and cardiovascular disease. Mayo Clin Proc 2012;87(8):766–778.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Premature Ejaculation (PE; ICD-11: HA01 Premature ejaculation; DSM-5-TR: 302.75) — ejaculation occurring involuntarily within a short time, often before or within ~1 minute of penetration; loss of control and distress. Subtypes: Lifelong (primary) — present from onset; Acquired (secondary) — developed later; Variable and Subjective — ISSM classification.

title: 2. History
text: Semans J.H. (1956) — 'squeeze' technique. Masters & Johnson (1970) — "start-stop" behavioral technique. ISSM (2014) — Althof S.E. international diagnostic and treatment guideline. 2009 — dapoxetine first approved drug for PE in the EU.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence in males 20–30% (self-reported); with strict ISSM criteria 4–5%. Age-related variability low; occurs in all age groups. Comorbidity: ED, anxiety, depression, relationship strain.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Neurobiological — serotonergic (5-HT2C, 5-HT1A balance); genetic component (lifelong). Psychological — performance anxiety, early sexual experience, relationship problems. Acquired — ED, prostatitis, hyperthyroidism, substance use.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Intravaginal ejaculation latency time (IELT) short (lifelong ~1 min; acquired ≤ 3 min). Distress, avoidance, relationship tension.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: ISSM (Althof S.E. et al. J Sex Med 2014). AUA and EAU guidelines. Clinical interview (sexual history, IELT, control, distress). PEDT (Premature Ejaculation Diagnostic Tool) — 5-item. Comorbid ED assessment (IIEF). Endocrine and urologic (acquired form).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: IELT (chronometry — when recommended). Endocrine and urologic (suspected acquired).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Start-stop (Masters & Johnson); Squeeze technique (Semans technique); Pelvic floor exercises (Pastore A. RI 2014). Tramadol — in selected cases (addiction risk must be considered). PDE5 inhibitor — only with comorbid ED. Couple therapy and CBT — for performance anxiety. Althof S.E. et al. ISSM Guideline. J Sex Med 2014. Waldinger M.D. et al. SSRI meta-analysis.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: Dapoxetine and SSRIs increase IELT by 2–4 times; behavioral combination supports long-term effect.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. HA01 Early ejaculation. 2024. Althof S.E. et al. An update of the International Society of Sexual Medicine's guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of premature ejaculation. J Sex Med 2014;11(6):1392–1422. Waldinger M.D. et al. Relevance of methodological design for the interpretation of efficacy of drug treatment of PE: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Int J Impot Res 2004;

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder (HSDD; ICD-11: HA02 Hypoactive sexual desire dysfunction; DSM-5-TR in females Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder 302.72, in males Male Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder 302.71) — persistent decrease or absence of sexual fantasies and desire for sexual activity; with distress. Duration ≥ 6 months; situational or generalized; lifelong or acquired.

title: 2. History
text: Kaplan H.S. (1977) — inclusion of the desire phase in the sexual response model. Basson R. (2000) — circular desire-arousal model in women. 2015 — flibanserin FDA approval; 2019 — bremelanotide FDA approval. ISSWSH (International Society for the Study of Women's Sexual Health) Process of Care 2018 (Clayton A.H.).

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Women: 8–10% distress (HSDD); men: 14–17%. Age-related increase; perimenopause is a risk period. Comorbidity: depression, antidepressant use (SSRIs), relationship problems.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Biological — hormonal (testosterone, estrogen), medication-related (SSRIs, OCs, antihypertensives), chronic diseases. Psychological — depression, anxiety, body image, trauma history. Relationship — conflict, partner sexual dysfunction. Social-cultural — taboo, lack of knowledge.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Reduction or absence of spontaneous sexual fantasy and thought. Not initiating and not responding to partner's initiative. Distress (critical criterion — mere low desire is insufficient).

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: ISSWSH Process of Care (Clayton A.H. et al. Mayo Clin Proc 2018). Clinical interview (sexual and relationship history). DSDS (Decreased Sexual Desire Screener) — 5-item in women. Hormonal assessment (morning testosterone in men; when indicated in women). Medication and comorbidity analysis. Depression, anxiety screening.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Depression/anxiety scales.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Flibanserin (100 mg at night) — FDA 2015 approval; caution with alcohol (modification in BPS); Bremelanotide (1.75 mg s/c as needed) — FDA 2019 approval; melanocortin agonist. Relationship and communication work is critical. Clayton A.H. et al. ISSWSH Process of Care. Mayo Clin Proc 2018. Davis S.R. et al. Global Consensus Position Statement on Testosterone Therapy for Women. J Clin Endocrinol Met

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With multimodal approach, most patients improve; chronic form requires ongoing strategy.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. HA02 Hypoactive sexual desire dysfunction. 2024. Clayton A.H. et al. The International Society for the Study of Women's Sexual Health Process of Care for Management of HSDD in Women. Mayo Clin Proc 2018;93(4):467–487. Davis S.R. et al. Global Consensus Position Statement on the Use of Testosterone Therapy for Women. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2019;104(10):4660–4666.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Anorgasmiya (XBT-11: HA03 Anorgasmia; DSM-5-TR Female Orgasmic Disorder 302.73 in women; Delayed Ejaculation 302.74 in men — persistent difficulty, delay, or absence of reaching orgasm after sufficient sexual arousal; or reduced intensity of orgasm. Duration ≥ 6 months; situational or generalized; lifelong or acquired.

title: 2. History
text: Masters & Johnson (1970) — classic description of orgasmic phase disorder. ISSWSH and ISSM guidelines.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Women experience clinically significant anorgasmia in 5–10% of cases; men experience delayed ejaculation in 1–4%. 30–70% sexual side effects with antidepressant (SSRI) use.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Medication-related (SSRI, antipsychotic, opioid). Neurological (pelvic surgery, MS, diabetic neuropathy). Endokrin (hipotireoz, hiperprolaktinemiya). Psychological — anxiety, trauma, knowledge deficiency. Age and hormonal changes.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Difficulty or absence of achieving orgasm. Decreased intensity of orgasm.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: ISSM Delayed Ejaculation Guideline. ISSWSH — orgasm dysfunction in women. Clinical interview (sexual history, orgasm with masturbation, orgasm with partner). FSFI (in women) / IIEF (in men). Medication and comorbidity analysis. Endokrin (testosteron, prolaktin, tireoid).

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Neurological and urologic (acquired suspicion).

title: 8. Treatment
text: Medication switch — bupropion, mirtazapine, vortioxetine alternative; Dose reduction; drug holiday (with caution in SSRIs); Sildenafil adjunct in women for SSRI-induced dysfunction — Nurnberg JAMA 2008 RCT. Delayed Ejaculation in males — psychotherapy, modification of masturbation pattern; no specific FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. Hormonal therapy only in confirmed deficiency.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: For women, directed masturbation yields 60–90% efficacy for primary anorgasmia; in SSRI-induced cases, pharmacological strategies provide improvement.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. HA03 Anorgasmia. 2024. Nurnberg H.G. et al. Sildenafil treatment of women with antidepressant-associated sexual dysfunction. JAMA 2008;300(4):395–404. Brotto L.A. et al. Psychological and Interpersonal Dimensions of Sexual Function and Dysfunction. J Sex Med 2016;13(4):538–571. Heiman J.R., LoPiccolo J. Becoming Orgasmic. Prentice Hall, 1988.

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Genito-pelvic pain / penetration disorder (ICD-11: HA04 DSM-5-TR Genito-Pelvic Pain/Penetration Disorder 302.76) — during penetration attempts: (a) difficulty, (b) genital or pelvic pain, (c) fear of pain, (d) tension of pelvic floor muscles. Classic vaginismus and dyspareunia are combined in this entity. Duration ≥ 6 months; lifelong or acquired.

title: 2. History
text: Sims J.M. (1861) — description of vaginismus. Masters & Johnson (1970) — pelvic floor tension. ISSWSH and ACOG guidelines.

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Prevalence in women is 5–17% (in clinical and non-clinical samples). Lifelong vaginismus — in young women, at first penetration attempt. Acquired — trauma, infection, atrophic vaginitis, post-surgery.

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis
text: Biological — atrophic vaginitis (postmenopausal), vulvodynia, endometriosis, infection, surgical wound. Psychological — sexual trauma, anxiety, abstinence education, fear of pain. Relationship and partner factors.

title: 5. Clinical picture
text: Reflexive tension of pelvic floor muscles during penetration. Pain (burning, stabbing). Penetration avoidance. Gynecological examination also difficult or impossible.

title: 6. Diaqnoz
text: Difficulty in penetration; Pain in vulvovaginal or pelvic region during or attempted penetration. Fear of pain associated with anticipation of penetration. In attempted penetration, tension of the pelvic floor muscles. Clinical interview (sexual history, pain characteristics, trauma). Careful gynecological examination — with caution, with patient consent.

title: 7. Examination and assessment
text: Gynecological examination. Pelvic floor assessment.

title: 8. Treatment
text: Sensate focus and couple therapy. Topical anesthetic (lidocaine) — in provoked vulvodynia (Goldstein A.T.). Local estrogen for atrophic vaginitis. Botulinum toxin A — in refractory vaginismus; evidence base is growing. Antidepressant/anticonvulsant (amitriptyline, gabapentin) — for neuropathic component. ter Kuile M.M. et al. CBT vaginismus. Goldstein A.T. et al. ISSWSH vulvodyniya konsensus.

title: 9. Proqnoz
text: With a multidisciplinary approach, prognosis is good (70–90%); in refractory cases, botox or intensive psychological intervention.

title: 11. Sources
text: WHO. ICD-11. HA04 Genito-pelvic pain/penetration disorder. 2024. Ter Kuile, M.M. et al. Therapist-aided exposure for women with lifelong vaginismus: a randomized controlled trial. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 2013;81(6):1127–1136. Goldstein A.T. et al. Vulvodynia: Assessment and Treatment. J Sex Med 2016;13(4):572–590. Bergeron S. et al. Genital pain in women and men: It can hurt

title: 1. Definition and nosology
text: Other sexual dysfunctions (ICD-11: HA05 ) — sexual dysfunctions (may involve desire, arousal, orgasm, pain areas) arising in the context of another physical illness, medication, substance, or mental disorder. Cases that do not fit specific HA00–HA04 categories or are comorbid. Subtypes: due to medication/substance (HA05.0), due to a medical condition (HA05.1), other (HA05.Y), unspecified (HA05.Z).
sub: SEXUAL DYSFUNCTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH ANOTHER DISEASE OR DISOR

title: 2. History
text: 2000s — SSRIs-induced sexual dysfunction was widely described (Montejo A.L. studies). PSSD (Post-SSRI Sexual Dysfunction) — Bahrick A. (2008), EMA 2019 confirmation.
sub: SEXUAL DYSFUNCTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH ANOTHER DISEASE OR DISOR

title: 3. Epidemiologiya
text: Sexual dysfunction with SSRI use 30–70%. ED in diabetic men 35–75%. High prevalence in cardiovascular diseases. Antipsychotic (especially risperidone, paliperidone) — due to hyperprolactinemia.
sub: SEXUAL DYSFUNCTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH ANOTHER DISEASE OR DISOR

title: 4. Etiology and pathogenesis

title: 5. Clinical picture

title: PSYCHIATRIC EMERGENCY SITUATIONS
text: This appendix provides a practical approach to psychiatric conditions that are life-threatening or require acute clinical evaluation.

title: High risk signs:
text: Concrete plan + access to means; Recent situational change (loss, severe illness); Loneliness + stubbornness; Psychosis or severe depression; New onset or profound hypomania (energy returns + mood remains low); Adult male + disability + alcohol.

title: Management:
text: Reducing access to means (gun removal, medication in small quantities) Close ambulatory monitoring (re-evaluate within 24-48 hours); Crisis hotline number; Family involvement (with consent). Close supervision – 1:1 if very high; Restriction of suicide methods (sharp objects, rope, window); Controlled medication intake.

title: Involuntary hospitalization within the Azerbaijani framework:
text: "On psychiatric assistance" Law of the Republic of Azerbaijan; For involuntary hospitalization decision by two psychiatrists and legal procedure; 72-hour emergency observation followed by a court order.

title: C.2. Delirium tremens and alcohol withdrawal
Treatment (in ICU or IMU setting):
text: Alternative: lorazepam (in those with hepatic dysfunction); Magnesium sulfate, potassium – often low; Mortality: untreated 15-20%, with adequate treatment <1%.

title: Treatment:
text: Support — fluids, electrolytes, cooling; In severe cases cyproheptadine 12 mg initial, 2 mg every hour maximum 32 mg/day ; Invasive intervention (intubation) in severe hyperthermia.

title: Treatment (ICU):
text: Fluids (risk of rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney failure); ECT in non-responsive cases.

title: Simptomlar:
text: Acute muscle spasms – neck (torticollis), tongue, eyes (oculogyric crisis), certain esophageal spasm; Life-threatening – laryngeal spasm.

title: Treatment:
text: Reducing or changing the dose of antipsychotics Prophylactic anticholinergic in high-risk patients.

title: C.6. Acute mania and psychomotor agitation
Management:
text: - Oral preferred: haloperidol 2-5 mg + lorazepam 1-2 mg; - IM regimen: haloperidol 5 mg + lorazepam 2 mg (50/50 syringe) or olanzapine IM 10 mg;

title: C.7. Acute psychosis
text: Similar protocol as above. Differential: organic causes, substance intoxication, postpartum psychosis, NMS.

title: Simptomlar:
text: Catalepsy ('waxy flexibility'); Mannerisms, stereotypies; Eholalia, ekopraksiya.

title: First-line treatment:
text: If response is obtained — increase to a dose of 4–24 mg/day; No answer – ECT Second-line, highly effective;

title: MILITARY-MEDICAL EXPERTISE (IN AZERBAIJAN)
text: This appendix provides practical guidance on psychiatric aspects for assessing fitness for military service in the Republic of Azerbaijan.

title: Main expert examination:
text: Complaints and history (patient and close relatives); Previous medical records; Clinical interview and mental status examination; Laboratory and instrumental examinations when necessary; Differential, diagnosis, and assessment of disease severity;

title: D.3. Psychiatric disorders and military fitness assessment
text: Classification by group (adaptation – based on local normative acts):

title: Group A – fully fit for service
text: No psychiatric history or minor transient issues.

title: Group B – useful with limitations
text: Mild personality traits (below impairment threshold); Transient adjustment disorders; Mild sleep disturbances; Neurotic disorders in remission state.

title: Group V — limited during service
text: Moderate psychiatric disturbances in partial remission state; Specific personality disorders; Past PTSD, compensated; Moderate neurocognitive dysfunction.

title: Group Q – Unfit for Service
text: Schizophrenia spectrum; Active PTSD/complex PTSD (Karabakh war veterans – separate treatment program); Substance use disorders; Severe personality disorders; Severe forms of autism spectrum;

title: D.4. Postpartum trauma and veteran assessment
text: In veterans of the Karabakh war: Evidence-based trauma therapies (PE, CPT, EMDR); Group therapy for veterans; Medication (SSRI/SNRI); Social rehabilitation (work, education).

title: RELATED NEUROLOGICAL CONDITIONS
text: Brief description of neurological diseases important for clinical practice in areas intersecting psychiatry and neurology.

title: Epileptic syndromes and psychiatry:
text: - Interictal personality changes (Geschwind syndrome – hyper religiosity, hypergraphia, "viscosity"); - Inter-ictal psychosis – long-standing TLE; - Post-ictal psychosis – psychotic episodes within 24-72 hours after a seizure; - Ictal anxiety and dissociative experiences;

title: Psikogen (qeyri-epileptik) tutmalar (PNES) – dissosiativ neyroloji simptom pozuntusu ( 6B60.4 ):
text: PNES frequently comorbid with epilepsy, 10-30%; Treatment: CBT-PNES, slow tapering of anticonvulsants.

title: Management:
text: SSRI/SNRI improves many types of headache; TCA (amitriptiline) historical standard for migraine prophylaxis Topiramate, valproate prophylactic; CBT is effective for chronic headache;
